cards.front: What maternal factors should be assessed when anticipating a neonatal resuscitation?
cards.back: Age, gestation and parity, comorbidities/pre-existing illnesses

cards.front: What fetal factors should be assessed when anticipating a neonatal resuscitation?
cards.back: Congenital anomalies, singleton vs multiple pregnancy, weight

cards.front: What equipment should be checked before neonatal resuscitation?
cards.back: All equipment available for resuscitation must be checked

cards.front: Why must a newborn be kept warm after delivery?
cards.back: The fetus has been in amniotic fluid which provides a warm environment, so post-delivery temperature control is essential

cards.front: What equipment can be used to maintain a newborn's temperature?
cards.back: Servicrib with heater, clean dry towels

cards.front: What is the important precaution when using towels to keep a newborn warm?
cards.back: Remove wet towels from under baby after use, as they cool the infant

cards.front: What are the three key questions to ask when assessing a newborn for resuscitation?
cards.back: 1) Is the baby breathing adequately? 2) Is the heart rate above 100 bpm? 3) Is the baby centrally pink?

cards.front: If a baby answers 'yes' to all three assessment questions, what does this indicate?
cards.back: Baby does not need resuscitation

cards.front: What is the critical first action when resuscitation is required?
cards.back: CALL FOR HELP — resuscitation is a team effort

cards.front: Define the anatomical extent of the airway in a neonate.
cards.back: The airway starts from the nose and ends in lung tissue

cards.front: What is the correct head position for a neonate's airway?
cards.back: Head in neutral position

cards.front: When should suction be applied to a neonate's airway?
cards.back: Only if obvious secretions (such as meconium) are blocking the nostrils or mouth

cards.front: What is the ILCOR 2015 guideline regarding routine suctioning of neonates with meconium exposure?
cards.back: Do NOT routinely suction neonates below the vocal cords with meconium exposure, even if the neonate is not vigorous. Intubate if needed to provide oxygenation as soon as possible

cards.front: What congenital abnormality can cause upper airway obstruction in neonates?
cards.back: Choanal atresia

cards.front: What airway intervention may be life-saving in a neonate with choanal atresia?
cards.back: Oropharyngeal airway

cards.front: What is the appropriate management for a peripherally cyanosed neonate with spontaneous breathing and heart rate >100?
cards.back: Provide supplemental oxygen only

cards.front: What is the normal respiratory rate in a neonate?
cards.back: 40 to 60 breaths per minute

cards.front: What interventions are indicated for a cyanotic neonate with heart rate <100 or apnoea?
cards.back: Provide breaths using bag-mask ventilation or neopuff for 30 seconds before reassessing

cards.front: If a neonate is still not breathing after initial intervention, what should be done?
cards.back: Continue to assist ventilation manually

cards.front: What is the critical consideration when providing breaths during neonatal resuscitation?
cards.back: Provide an adequate number of breaths — do not hyper or hypoventilate. Use counting rhythm like 'breath and 2 and 3 and breathe and 2 and 3 and breathe'

cards.front: What ventilation method is effective in most neonates?
cards.back: Bag-mask ventilation

cards.front: What is the important precaution when using an ambu-bag for neonatal ventilation?
cards.back: Do NOT hold down the pop-up valve, as increased pressures lead to higher risk of air leak syndromes

cards.front: When may intubation be considered during neonatal resuscitation?
cards.back: If ongoing resuscitation is necessary

cards.front: What is the normal heart rate range in a neonate?
cards.back: 120–160 beats per minute

cards.front: How should the heart rate be assessed during neonatal resuscitation?
cards.back: By palpating either the brachial or femoral pulse

cards.front: At what heart rate threshold should chest compressions be initiated in a neonate?
cards.back: Heart rate less than 60 bpm

cards.front: Describe the hand technique for neonatal chest compressions.
cards.back: 2 hands encircling the chest with thumbs in front

cards.front: Where should the thumbs be positioned during neonatal chest compressions?
cards.back: On the sternum in the midline along the nipple line

cards.front: What is the correct depth for neonatal chest compressions?
cards.back: At least 1/3 of the chest circumference

cards.front: What is the compression-to-breath ratio for neonatal chest compressions?
cards.back: 3:1 — 3 chest compressions to 1 breath

cards.front: How should the rate of chest compressions be determined?
cards.back: Match the normal heart rate as closely as possible, with compressions coordinated with breaths

cards.front: When should the heart rate be reassessed during neonatal resuscitation?
cards.back: After 30 seconds

cards.front: If heart rate remains <60 bpm after 30 seconds of chest compressions, what is the next step?
cards.back: Give necessary drugs (adrenaline)

cards.front: Why is intravenous access essential during neonatal resuscitation?
cards.back: For fluid administration and medication delivery

cards.front: What is the alternative to peripheral IV access if not easily available?
cards.back: Insert an umbilical venous catheter

cards.front: When is adrenaline indicated during neonatal resuscitation?
cards.back: If baby remains bradycardic after 30 seconds of chest compressions

cards.front: What is the concentration of adrenaline used in neonatal resuscitation?
cards.back: 1:10 000 solution

cards.front: How is adrenaline 1:10 000 prepared for neonatal resuscitation?
cards.back: Mix 1 ml adrenaline with 9 ml saline

cards.front: What is the dose of adrenaline for neonatal resuscitation?
cards.back: 0.01 mg/kg intravenously

cards.front: What is the dosing interval for adrenaline in neonatal resuscitation?
cards.back: May be repeated every 3 to 5 minutes

cards.front: When should dextrose be given during neonatal resuscitation?
cards.back: If the baby is hypoglycaemic — correct the hypoglycaemia

cards.front: Is sodium bicarbonate routinely used in neonatal resuscitation?
cards.back: No, sodium bicarbonate is NOT used routinely in the resuscitation of a newborn

cards.front: If a neonate responds well at any stage during resuscitation, what is the next step?
cards.back: Baby will still need admission for observations for at least 24 hours

cards.front: What is the likely outcome if a neonate shows some response with signs of life?
cards.back: May need admission into ICU/high care for ongoing support — intubation and ventilation, inotropes

cards.front: What is the outcome if a neonate shows no response during resuscitation?
cards.back: Death

cards.front: Who should be consulted before stopping neonatal resuscitation?
cards.back: A senior clinician — discussion with senior before stopping any resus

cards.front: After how long without a heart rate should resuscitation be stopped?
cards.back: After 10 minutes if no heart rate is present

cards.front: After how long without spontaneous breathing should resuscitation be stopped?
cards.back: After 20 minutes with no spontaneous breathing

cards.front: How should gasping be differentiated from breathing during neonatal resuscitation?
cards.back: Gasping is usually a terminal sign and should be differentiated from actual breathing

cards.front: Define apnoea in the neonate.
cards.back: Cessation of breathing (respiratory airflow).

cards.front: What is the time threshold for apnoea of 20 seconds or more?
cards.back: Apnoea ≥20 seconds, with or without cyanosis, pallor or bradycardia (<100 bpm).

cards.front: What is the time threshold for apnoea <20 seconds?
cards.back: Cessation of breathing <20 seconds associated with cyanosis, pallor or bradycardia (<100 bpm).

cards.front: What is the bradycardia threshold in apnoea definitions?
cards.back: Heart rate <100 bpm.

cards.front: At what age is periodic breathing predominantly seen?
cards.back: Age 2 to 4 weeks.

cards.front: When does periodic breathing resolve?
cards.back: By 6 months of age.

cards.front: Describe the pattern of apnoea episodes in periodic breathing.
cards.back: Brief episodes of apnoea occurring in short cycles of 5 to 10 seconds.

cards.front: In what gestational age group does apnoea occur most commonly?
cards.back: Preterm infants; incidence increases with decreasing gestational age.

cards.front: What percentage of infants <28 weeks gestation experience apnoea?
cards.back: Almost every infant younger than 28 weeks will have an episode.

cards.front: What percentage of infants born <34 weeks gestation experience apnoea?
cards.back: At least 85%.

cards.front: What is the incidence of apnoea in term infants?
cards.back: 1 per 1000. Apnoea in term infants is often abnormal and associated with serious identifiable causes.

cards.front: Name the three components of breathing control.
cards.back: 1. Control centre (brain and brainstem). 2. Afferent and efferent communication (nerves). 3. Receptors (central and peripheral chemoreceptors, stretch receptors).

cards.front: Name the four components of breathing ventilation.
cards.back: 1. Airway (upper and lower). 2. Gas exchange (lung physiological unit = alveoli). 3. Respiratory muscles (diaphragm, accessory). 4. Supporting structures (ribs, connective tissue).

cards.front: Which brain region controls voluntary respiration?
cards.back: Cerebral motor cortex.

cards.front: Which brain region controls involuntary respiration?
cards.back: Brainstem.

cards.front: What is the primary respiratory control centre?
cards.back: Medulla oblongata.

cards.front: What does VRG (ventral respiratory group) in the medulla stimulate?
cards.back: Expiratory movements.

cards.front: What does DRG (dorsal respiratory group, solitary tract) in the medulla stimulate?
cards.back: Inspiratory movements.

cards.front: What non-respiratory air movements does the medulla control?
cards.back: Coughing and sneezing.

cards.front: What does the pons control in respiration?
cards.back: Rate of involuntary respiration.

cards.front: What does the apneustic centre (in pons) send signals for?
cards.back: Long and deep breaths (increases tidal volume).

cards.front: What inhibits the apneustic centre and signals for maximum inspiration?
cards.back: Stretch receptors in pulmonary muscles at maximum inspiration (Hering-Breuer reflex).

cards.front: What does the pneumotaxic centre (in pons) send signals for?
cards.back: To inhibit inspiration (decreases tidal volume).

cards.front: What does the pneumotaxic centre inhibit?
cards.back: 1. The activity of the phrenic nerve. 2. The apneustic centre.

cards.front: What is the location of central chemoreceptors?
cards.back: Surface of the medulla oblongata.

cards.front: What substances do central chemoreceptors sense?
cards.back: H⁺ ion concentration and PaCO₂ in blood and CSF.

cards.front: How does increased CO₂ affect pH at the central chemoreceptor?
cards.back: Increased CO₂ leads to decreased pH due to production of H⁺ ions from H₂CO₃.

cards.front: Where are peripheral chemoreceptors located?
cards.back: Aortic arch and carotid bodies.

cards.front: What do peripheral chemoreceptors measure?
cards.back: PaO₂ in arterial blood.

cards.front: Where are stretch receptors located?
cards.back: Smooth muscle of airways.

cards.front: Which cranial nerve carries afferent signals from the carotid body?
cards.back: Glossopharyngeal nerve (CN IX).

cards.front: Which cranial nerve carries afferent signals from the aortic arch and lungs?
cards.back: Vagus nerve (CN X).

cards.front: Which nerves carry afferent signals in the respiratory pathway?
cards.back: 1. Glossopharyngeal (CN IX) from carotid body. 2. Vagus (CN X) from aortic arch chemoreceptors and lungs (stretch and irritant receptors).

cards.front: Which nerve innervates the diaphragm?
cards.back: Phrenic nerve.

cards.front: What are the spinal nerve roots of the phrenic nerve?
Which nerves innervate the accessory muscles of breathing?
cards.back: Thoracic nerves.

cards.front: Name the components of the upper airways.
cards.back: 1. Nostril. 2. Nasal cavity and paranasal sinuses. 3. Pharynx. 4. Larynx.

cards.front: Name the components of the lower airways.
cards.back: 1. Trachea. 2. Bronchi. 3. Bronchioles.

cards.front: What is the functional unit of the respiratory system for gas exchange?
cards.back: Alveolus.

cards.front: What is the primary respiratory muscle?
cards.back: Diaphragm.

cards.front: Name the accessory muscles of respiration.
cards.back: 1. Intercostal muscles and other chest wall muscles. 2. Sternocleidomastoid. 3. Abdominal wall muscles.

cards.front: Name the supporting structures for respiration.
cards.back: Bones, cartilage and connective tissue.

cards.front: What is the first factor in the pathogenesis of apnoea?
cards.back: Developmental immaturity of the central respiratory drive.

cards.front: What is the second factor in the pathogenesis of apnoea?
cards.back: Receptor response – apnoea in response to hypoxia and hypercapnoea (foetal/neonatal transition physiology).

cards.front: What is the third factor in the pathogenesis of apnoea?
cards.back: Reflexes – e.g. laryngeal chemical reflex depresses breathing during feeding.

cards.front: What is the fourth factor in the pathogenesis of apnoea?
cards.back: Increased sensitivity to stressors such as toxins, hypoglycaemia, hypothermia, anaemia.

cards.front: What is the fifth factor in the pathogenesis of apnoea?
cards.back: Respiratory muscles and chest wall – more easily fatigable. Thoracic wall and muscles more pliable leading to increased work of breathing.

cards.front: What is the sixth factor in the pathogenesis of apnoea?
cards.back: Airway obstruction.

cards.front: Define central apnoea.
cards.back: Inspiratory effort is absent. Comprises approximately 20% of apnoea cases.

cards.front: Define obstructive apnoea.
cards.back: Inspiratory efforts persist in the presence of airway obstruction. Comprises approximately 20% of apnoea cases.

cards.front: Define reflex apnoea.
cards.back: Apnoea that is vagally mediated.

cards.front: Define mixed apnoea.
cards.back: Airway obstruction with inspiratory effort precedes or follows central apnoea. Comprises approximately 60% of apnoea cases.

cards.front: List the reflex apnoea triggers in preterm infants.
cards.back: 1. GORD. 2. Overfeeding. 3. Deep suctioning. 4. Intubation.

cards.front: List the central apnoea causes in preterm infants (part 1).
cards.back: 1. Prematurity. 2. Hypoxia. 3. Acidosis. 4. CNS lesions (IVH, seizures, meningitis). 5. NEC.

cards.front: List the central apnoea causes in preterm infants (part 2).
cards.back: 6. Ambient temperature fluctuations. 7. Metabolic derangements (glucose and electrolyte abnormalities). 8. Anaemia. 9. Drugs (phenobarb, sedatives). 10. Pain and excessive handling.

cards.front: List the central apnoea causes in preterm infants (part 3).
cards.back: 11. Cardiac abnormalities e.g. PDA, heart failure.

cards.front: Why are neonates prone to obstructive apnoea?
cards.back: Neonates are obligatory nose breathers. Obstruction of nares leads to apnoeas.

cards.front: List the obstructive apnoea causes in preterm infants.
cards.back: 1. Choanal atresia/stenosis. 2. Macroglossia. 3. Micrognathia. 4. Secretions. 5. Excessive neck flexion or extension.

cards.front: What are the causes of mixed apnoea?
cards.back: Combination of central, reflex, and obstructive causes.

cards.front: What history differentiations are important in apnoea assessment?
cards.back: True apnoea versus periodic breathing versus breath holding.

cards.front: What perinatal and postnatal history elements should be obtained?
cards.back: Antenatal, perinatal, postnatal, and feeding history.

cards.front: What family history elements are relevant to apnoea assessment?
cards.back: Family history of seizures, infant deaths, serious family illnesses, consanguinity.

cards.front: What social history elements are relevant to apnoea assessment?
cards.back: Toxins, drugs, medications, tobacco smoke, CO.

cards.front: What associated symptoms should be documented in apnoea history?
cards.back: Sleep issues, snoring, mouth breathing.

cards.front: What should be assessed on examination in apnoea (part 1)?
cards.back: 1. Congenital anomalies. 2. Genetic syndromes. 3. Congenital infections. 4. IEM stigmata.

cards.front: What should be assessed on examination in apnoea (part 2)?
cards.back: 5. Fever or hypothermia. 6. Tachypnoea. 7. Stridor.

cards.front: What is a readily available monitoring method for apnoea?
cards.back: SpO₂ monitoring, though it may be insufficient.

cards.front: What are the limitations of SpO₂ monitoring for apnoea?
cards.back: 1. Movement-related false alarms. 2. Newer models more movement resistant.

cards.front: What is abdominal pressure monitoring?
cards.back: Diaphragm taped to abdominal wall to detect respiratory effort.

cards.front: What is transthoracic impedance pneumography?
cards.back: Electrodes placed above and below diaphragm on infant's chest. Cannot identify obstructive apnoea well.

cards.front: What is nasal thermistry?
cards.back: Device attached above upper lip that detects changes in temperature.

cards.front: What is respiratory inductance plethysmography?
cards.back: Coil bands placed around infant's abdomen and chest that can estimate tidal volume.

cards.front: What is the first principle of apnoea management?
cards.back: Identify and treat underlying cause.

cards.front: List the respiratory support options for apnoea (part 1).
cards.back: 1. NPO₂. 2. Warm, humidified HFNC. 3. NCPAP/SIPAP. 4. Mechanical ventilation.

cards.front: What class of drugs are used as central stimulants for apnoea?
cards.back: Methylxanthines.

cards.front: Which methylxanthines are used for apnoea of prematurity?
cards.back: 1. Caffeine (citrate/base) – Yes. 2. Aminophylline – Yes. 3. Theophylline – No.

cards.front: What is the mechanism of methylxanthines?
cards.back: Central blockade of GABA-ergic neuron + Adenosine₂ₐ receptors, resulting in decreased release of GABA.

cards.front: How do methylxanthines protect cells during hypoxia and ischaemia?
cards.back: They preserve brain ATP levels.

cards.front: What other effects do methylxanthines have on respiratory function?
cards.back: 1. Improved respiratory muscle function. 2. Enhanced chemoreceptor responsiveness to CO₂.

cards.front: What are the side effects of methylxanthines?
cards.back: 1. Increased energy expenditure and oxygen consumption – may decrease growth. 2. Behavioural problems in later childhood?

cards.front: What did 18-month follow-up of caffeine-treated neonates show?
cards.back: Those treated with caffeine as neonates had significantly improved survival without neurodevelopmental disability.

cards.front: Define SIDS (Sudden Infant Death Syndrome).
cards.back: The sudden unexplained death of a child less than one year of age.

cards.front: At what age is SIDS most common?
cards.back: Most commonly 2–4 months age; almost unheard of after 6 months.

cards.front: What is the primary prevention for SIDS?
cards.back: Sleeping on back.

cards.front: What environmental factors should be avoided to prevent SIDS?
cards.back: 1. No blankets or pillows. 2. Use firm mattress. 3. Smoking should be avoided by parents. 4. Avoid overheating. 5. Avoid sleeping in same bed.

cards.front: What protective factors reduce the risk of SIDS?
cards.back: 1. Breast feeding. 2. Pacifiers.

cards.front: What is the SIDS risk in infants born before 32 weeks gestation?
cards.back: Three-times-higher risk of sudden infant death syndrome (SIDS) compared to term infants.

cards.front: What are the short-term consequences of apnoea?
cards.back: 1. Hypoxaemia. 2. Bradycardia. 3. Impaired perfusion and drop in BP. 4. Decreased cerebral perfusion. 5. Reflex hyperperfusion.

cards.front: What is the relationship between apnoea and neurodevelopmental outcome?
cards.back: Apnoea is associated with poor neurodevelopmental outcome. Long-term effects are controversial, and it is difficult to establish causal relationship in patients with co-existing medical problems.

cards.front: What are irritant receptors and where are they located?
cards.back: Irritant receptors located in airways that respond to irritants.

cards.front: What is the relationship between increased CO2 and H+ ion production in the medulla?
cards.back: Increased CO2 leads to production of H⁺ ions from H₂CO₃ (carbonic acid), decreasing pH at central chemoreceptors.

cards.front: Which spinal segments comprise the phrenic nerve?
cards.back: C3, C4, and C5 spinal nerve roots (mnemonic: 'C3, 4, 5 keeps the diaphragm alive').

cards.front: Describe the laryngeal chemical reflex mechanism in apnoea pathogenesis.
cards.back: Laryngeal chemical reflex depresses breathing during feeding, contributing to reflex apnoea in preterm infants.

cards.front: Why are neonatal respiratory muscles and chest wall prone to fatigue in apnoea?
cards.back: Respiratory muscles and chest wall are more easily fatigable. Thoracic wall is more pliable, leading to increased work of breathing.

cards.front: What are the short-term consequences of apnoea on cerebral perfusion?
cards.back: Apnoea causes decreased cerebral perfusion followed by reflex hyperperfusion.

cards.front: How do CNS lesions contribute to central apnoea in preterm infants?
cards.back: CNS lesions (IVH, seizures, meningitis) disrupt respiratory control centres, leading to central apnoea.

cards.front: Why does NEC (necrotising enterocolitis) trigger central apnoea?
cards.back: NEC is a serious gastrointestinal condition that contributes to systemic disturbance, triggering central apnoea in preterm infants.

cards.front: How do cardiac abnormalities like PDA cause apnoea?
cards.back: Patent ductus arteriosus (PDA) and heart failure lead to impaired cardiac output and perfusion, triggering central apnoea.

cards.front: Which drugs are known to cause central apnoea in preterm infants?
cards.back: Phenobarbitone (phenobarb) and sedatives depress respiratory drive, causing central apnoea.

cards.front: Why is choanal atresia or stenosis significant in neonatal obstructive apnoea?
cards.back: Neonates are obligatory nose breathers; choanal atresia/stenosis blocks nasal airway, directly causing obstructive apnoea.

cards.front: How does macroglossia cause obstructive apnoea in neonates?
cards.back: Enlarged tongue (macroglossia) physically obstructs the nasopharynx, preventing adequate airflow and causing obstructive apnoea.

cards.front: How does micrognathia contribute to obstructive apnoea?
cards.back: Small mandible (micrognathia) reduces oral airway space and allows posterior tongue displacement, obstructing airflow.

cards.front: How does neck position affect neonatal airway patency?
cards.back: Excessive neck flexion or extension compresses the nasopharynx or airway structures, precipitating obstructive apnoea in obligatory nose breathers.

cards.front: How does GORD (gastro-oesophageal reflux disease) trigger reflex apnoea?
cards.back: GORD causes acid aspiration and irritation of laryngeal chemoreceptors, triggering vagally mediated reflex apnoea.

cards.front: Why does deep suctioning trigger reflex apnoea in preterm infants?
cards.back: Deep suctioning irritates laryngeal chemoreceptors and vagal afferents, triggering a protective laryngeal chemoreflex that depresses breathing.

cards.front: How does intubation trigger reflex apnoea?
cards.back: Intubation irritates the larynx and upper airway, activating vagal reflexes that can suppress spontaneous breathing in preterm infants.

cards.front: How does overfeeding trigger reflex apnoea in preterm infants?
cards.back: Overfeeding causes gastric distension and oesophageal irritation, activating laryngeal chemoreflex and vagal pathways that depress breathing.

cards.front: How does hypoxia cause apnoea in preterm infants?
cards.back: In foetal/neonatal transition physiology, hypoxia activates peripheral chemoreceptors which paradoxically suppress breathing (receptor response), causing apnoea.

cards.front: How does hypercapnoea cause apnoea in preterm infants?
cards.back: In foetal/neonatal transition physiology, hypercapnoea can paradoxically trigger a protective apneic response rather than increasing respiratory drive.

cards.front: How does acidosis contribute to central apnoea?
cards.back: Acidosis depresses the central respiratory control centre in the brainstem, reducing respiratory drive and precipitating central apnoea.

cards.front: What metabolic derangements trigger central apnoea in preterm infants?
cards.back: Glucose abnormalities (hypoglycaemia) and electrolyte abnormalities (hypokalaemia, hyponatraemia) depress respiratory centres, causing central apnoea.

cards.front: How does anaemia contribute to central apnoea?
cards.back: Anaemia reduces oxygen-carrying capacity and tissue oxygenation, increasing chemoreceptor sensitivity and triggering central apnoea episodes.

cards.front: How does pain and excessive handling cause central apnoea?
cards.back: Pain and excessive handling stress preterm infants and increase metabolic demands, destabilising already immature respiratory control.

cards.front: Why do ambient temperature fluctuations trigger central apnoea in preterm infants?
cards.back: Temperature fluctuations stress immature thermoregulatory systems and increase metabolic demands, destabilising respiratory control.

cards.front: What was the CAP trial and what did it study?
cards.back: Caffeine Therapy for Apnea of Prematurity (Schmidt B et al., NEJM 2006;354:2112-21) demonstrated efficacy of caffeine in reducing apnea episodes.

cards.front: What are the two formulations of caffeine used for apnoea of prematurity?
cards.back: Caffeine citrate and caffeine base.

cards.front: What is the adenosine pathway blocked by methylxanthines?
cards.back: Methylxanthines block Adenosine₂ₐ (A2a) receptors on GABA-ergic neurons, preventing GABA-mediated respiratory depression.

cards.front: Why is ATP preservation by methylxanthines protective during hypoxia-ischaemia?
cards.back: Preserving brain ATP levels maintains cellular energy homeostasis, protecting neurons from hypoxic and ischaemic injury during apnoea episodes.

cards.front: How do methylxanthines enhance chemoreceptor responsiveness?
cards.back: Methylxanthines increase the sensitivity of central and peripheral chemoreceptors to CO₂ and O₂ changes, improving respiratory control.

cards.front: What is the mechanism by which methylxanthines may decrease growth?
cards.back: Methylxanthines increase energy expenditure and oxygen consumption, potentially reducing calories available for growth.

cards.front: What potential long-term neurobehavioural effect of neonatal caffeine therapy remains controversial?
cards.back: Behavioural problems in later childhood may occur with neonatal caffeine therapy, though evidence is uncertain.

cards.front: Why is theophylline NOT used for apnoea of prematurity despite being a methylxanthine?
cards.back: Theophylline is not used due to unfavourable pharmacokinetics, unpredictable metabolism, and narrow therapeutic window in preterm infants.

cards.front: Why is warm, humidified HFNC used as respiratory support in apnoea?
cards.back: Warm, humidified high-flow nasal cannula (HFNC) maintains airway patency, reduces airway resistance, and provides gentle PEEP without intubation.

cards.front: What does NCPAP provide in the management of apnoea?
cards.back: NCPAP (nasal continuous positive airway pressure) and SIPAP (synchronized intermittent positive airway pressure) provide positive end-expiratory pressure to prevent airway collapse.

cards.front: How does PEEP from non-invasive support prevent obstructive apnoea?
cards.back: PEEP maintains positive pressure throughout the airway, preventing pharyngeal collapse and obstruction during inspiration.

cards.front: What is the main limitation of SpO2 monitoring alone for apnoea detection?
cards.back: SpO2 monitoring shows movement-related false alarms; newer pulse oximetry models are more resistant to motion artefact but remain imperfect.

cards.front: What is the key limitation of transthoracic impedance pneumography?
cards.back: Transthoracic impedance pneumography cannot reliably identify obstructive apnoea because it measures electrical impedance changes, not airflow.

cards.front: How does nasal thermistry detect apnoea?
cards.back: Nasal thermistry detects airflow by measuring temperature changes at the nostrils; cessation of airflow abolishes temperature changes.

cards.front: What additional information does respiratory inductance plethysmography provide?
cards.back: Respiratory inductance plethysmography can estimate tidal volume in addition to detecting breathing effort.

cards.front: Why is the 2-4 month age range the peak risk period for SIDS?
cards.back: Peak SIDS risk occurs at 2-4 months when protective arousal reflexes are immature and respiratory control is transitioning.

cards.front: Why should blankets and pillows be avoided to reduce SIDS risk?
cards.back: Blankets and pillows can obstruct the nose and mouth, reducing airway patency and increasing risk of suffocation and CO₂ rebreathing.

cards.front: Why is a firm mattress recommended for SIDS prevention?
cards.back: Firm mattress prevents infant from sinking into soft surfaces, maintaining airway patency and reducing rebreathing risk.

cards.front: How does parental smoking increase SIDS risk?
cards.back: Tobacco smoke exposure impairs respiratory chemoreceptor function and arousal responses, and may increase airway inflammation and obstruction.

cards.front: Why should overheating be avoided in SIDS prevention?
cards.back: Overheating reduces arousal responses and impairs thermoregulatory responses, increasing risk of fatal apnoea.

cards.front: Why is bed-sharing a SIDS risk factor?
cards.back: Co-sleeping increases risk of accidental suffocation, entrapment, and reduced infant arousal due to shared warmth and parent movement.

cards.front: How does breastfeeding reduce SIDS risk?
cards.back: Breastfeeding improves infant arousal responses, enhances immune function, reduces respiratory infections, and may improve chemoreceptor sensitivity.

cards.front: What is the mechanism by which pacifier use reduces SIDS risk?
cards.back: Pacifiers enhance arousal responses, maintain oral airway patency, and may improve protective airway reflexes during sleep.

cards.front: Why do infants born before 32 weeks gestation have three-times-higher SIDS risk?
cards.back: Very preterm infants have immature arousal reflexes, chemoreceptor dysfunction, and altered sleep architecture, all predisposing to SIDS.

cards.front: Why does periodic breathing predominantly occur at 2-4 weeks of age?
cards.back: At 2-4 weeks, neonatal respiratory control centres are immature and transitioning from foetal patterns, causing cyclic breathing instability.

cards.front: What does the pons control that the medulla does not?
cards.back: The pons controls the RATE of involuntary respiration via apneustic and pneumotaxic centres, while medulla controls basic rhythm.

cards.front: What is the significance of obligatory nasal breathing in neonates?
cards.back: Obligatory nasal breathing means neonates cannot switch to mouth breathing; any nasal obstruction directly causes upper airway obstruction and apnoea.

cards.front: What is the most widely used definition of neonatal hypoglycaemia?
cards.back: Whole blood glucose concentration <2.6 mmol/ℓ

cards.front: Define severe neonatal hypoglycaemia.
cards.back: Whole blood glucose ≤1.7 mmol/ℓ OR any symptomatic baby with low blood glucose

cards.front: What instruments measure whole blood glucose?
cards.back: Blood glucometers with reagent strips and blood gas analyzers

cards.front: How should persistent hypoglycaemia be confirmed?
cards.back: Confirm with a laboratory sample (serum glucose); however, abnormal whole blood readings must be acted on immediately without waiting

cards.front: What is the typical difference between whole blood and serum glucose?
cards.back: Whole blood glucose is usually 15% (0.5 mmol/ℓ) lower than serum glucose

cards.front: List 5 goals of managing neonatal hypoglycaemia.
cards.back:
1) Prevent/correct symptomatic hypoglycaemia in at-risk babies within first hour
2) Avoid hypothermia
3) Avoid unnecessary treatment of transitional low blood glucose
4) Identify underlying hypoglycaemic disorder
5) Prevent long-term neurologic sequelae

cards.front: When should babies with hypoglycaemia risk factors have blood glucose screening?
cards.back: During first 24 hours: after first feed, then before subsequent feeds or 6–8 hourly if not being fed

cards.front: List major risk factors for neonatal hypoglycaemia.
cards.back:
1) Prematurity
2) Post-maturity
3) Small/large for gestational age
4) Intrauterine growth restriction
5) Infants of diabetic mothers
6) Perinatal stress (hypoxia, MAS, haemolytic disease, hypothermia, fluid restriction, polycythaemia)
7) Family history of genetic hypoglycaemia
8) Syndromes/midline brain abnormalities (e.g. Beckwith-Wiedemann, hypopituitarism)

cards.front: Which healthy babies do NOT require blood glucose monitoring?
cards.back: Term infants without risk factors who are breast feeding or formula feeding normally

cards.front: Are babies with hypoglycaemia usually symptomatic?
cards.back: No; babies with hypoglycaemia are frequently asymptomatic and have non-specific signs when symptomatic

cards.front: List clinical features of symptomatic neonatal hypoglycaemia.
cards.back:
1) Jitteriness
2) Sweating
3) Tachypnoea
4) Tachycardia
5) Pallor
6) Cyanosis
7) Poor suck/feeding
8) Weak or high-pitched cry/irritability
9) Decreased level of consciousness
10) Seizures
11) Hypotonia
12) Apnoeas

cards.front: What is the glucose requirement in term babies?
cards.back: 6–8 mg/kg/minute

cards.front: What is the glucose requirement in preterm babies?
cards.back: 4–6 mg/kg/minute

cards.front: Should symptomatic hypoglycaemia always be corrected?
cards.back: Yes; persistent and prolonged hypoglycaemia is pathological and may cause brain injury

cards.front: Why is it important to differentiate transitional hypoglycaemia from pathological hypoglycaemia?
cards.back: To avoid over-treatment; normal transitional response involves transient physiological drop in blood glucose after birth but resolves without intervention in most cases

cards.front: What is the blood glucose range for asymptomatic well babies managed with feeds and warmth?
What is the first step in managing asymptomatic well babies (1.8–2.5 mmol/ℓ)?
cards.back:
1) Give milk feed immediately or start IV fluids if milk feeds contraindicated
2) Keep baby warm - ensure normothermia
3) Re-check blood glucose in 30 minutes

cards.front: What action after 30-minute recheck if asymptomatic baby's glucose ≥2.6 mmol/ℓ?
cards.back: Continue monitoring hourly for 3 hours, then 3-hourly for 24 hours

cards.front: If asymptomatic baby's glucose is still <2.6 mmol/ℓ but increasing, what is the next step?
cards.back: Re-check blood glucose in 30 minutes

cards.front: If asymptomatic baby's glucose is not increasing nor decreasing, what are the interventions?
cards.back:
1) Increase frequency of feeds to 2-hourly
2) Increase daily volume of feeds by 20–30 ml/kg/day
3) Re-check blood glucose in 30 minutes

cards.front: If asymptomatic baby's glucose is decreasing or still <2.6 mmol/ℓ, how is it managed?
cards.back: Manage as per Severe Hypoglycaemia protocol

cards.front: What is the blood glucose threshold for severe neonatal hypoglycaemia management protocol?
cards.back: ≤1.7 mmol/ℓ OR symptomatic

cards.front: What is the first intervention for symptomatic/severe hypoglycaemia?
cards.back: Give milk by nasogastric tube (if not fed within previous 2 hours) whilst preparing IV access, OR give 2–5 ml/kg 10% dextrose water IV as bolus

cards.front: What is glucagon dosing and route for emergency severe hypoglycaemia if IV access delayed?
cards.back: Glucagon: 0.2 mg/kg/dose IM; can repeat after 20 minutes; maximum dose 1 mg

cards.front: When should emergency umbilical venous catheter (UVC) be inserted?
cards.back: If unable to establish peripheral IV access in severe/symptomatic hypoglycaemia

cards.front: What is hydrocortisone dosing for hypoglycaemia not responding to maximum IV glucose?
cards.back: Hydrocortisone: 1–2 mg/kg/dose IV or PO 6-hourly

cards.front: What IV infusion is started once glucose >2.5 mmol/ℓ?
cards.back: 10% dextrose at 60–120 ml/kg/24 hours depending on gestational age and day of life; beware volumes >120 ml/kg/day on day 1

cards.front: If hypoglycaemia persists despite IV dextrose, what is the escalation?
cards.back: Increase volume of IV infusion OR increase dextrose concentration sequentially to 12.5%, 15%, 20% if no improvement at each concentration

cards.front: How should glucose concentrations >12.5% be infused?
cards.back: Via central line; emergency UVC can be used for short-term control during transfer until sterile UVC inserted at referral centre

cards.front: Why must 50% glucose never be given IV or orally?
cards.back: Due to its hypertonicity

cards.front: What is the blood glucose recheck schedule after severe hypoglycaemia intervention?
cards.back: Every 30 minutes after intervention until normal; then 2-hourly and/or before feeds until ≥2.6 mmol/ℓ for two consecutive readings

cards.front: When should milk feeds be added in severe hypoglycaemia?
cards.back: When glucose is normal and no clinical contraindications; gradually build up feeds while decreasing IV fluids provided blood glucose remains ≥2.6 mmol/ℓ

cards.front: When should dextrose-containing IV fluids be discontinued?
cards.back: Do not stop until feeds are well established

cards.front: What is the formula for dextrose delivery rate (mg/kg/minute)?
cards.back: (ml/kg/24 hours) × (% dextrose) × 0.007

cards.front: Define persistent neonatal hypoglycaemia.
cards.back: Persistently low blood glucose >48 hours of life OR requirement of parenteral glucose infusions >48 hours to treat hypoglycaemia

cards.front: What should be done for all babies with persistent hypoglycaemia?
cards.back: Discuss with referral centre for advice and admission if unable to be managed at place of birth

cards.front: What should be considered first in persistent hypoglycaemia workup?
cards.back: Sepsis: take blood culture and start broad spectrum antibiotics appropriate for early or late onset sepsis

cards.front: Who should guide further investigation in persistent hypoglycaemia?
cards.back: Paediatrician, neonatologist, or paediatric endocrinologist

cards.front: What is the critical sample for persistent hypoglycaemia and when is it drawn?
cards.back: Blood drawn at time of hypoglycaemia for: serum glucose, insulin, and cortisol; always confirm hypoglycaemia with laboratory sample

cards.front: What is the commonest cause of persistent neonatal hypoglycaemia?
cards.back: Hyperinsulinism (elevated insulin despite hypoglycaemia)

cards.front: List associations with neonatal hyperinsulinism.
cards.back:
1) Infants of diabetic mothers
2) Intrauterine growth restriction
3) Intrapartum stress

cards.front: What is the typical course of hyperinsulinism in neonates?
cards.back: Usually transient and typically resolves in first few weeks of life; persistent forms require specialist referral

cards.front: What is the drug and dosing for hypoglycaemia secondary to low cortisol?
cards.back: Hydrocortisone: 1–2 mg/kg/dose IV or PO 6-hourly

cards.front: What is diazoxide dosing in hyperinsulinism?
cards.back: Diazoxide: 2–6 mg/kg/day IV/PO 8-hourly

cards.front: What is hydrochlorothiazide dosing and role in hyperinsulinism?
cards.back: Hydrochlorothiazide: 1–3 mg/kg/dose PO 6–12 hourly; potentiates the effect of diazoxide

cards.front: What are discharge criteria after neonatal hypoglycaemia?
cards.back: Baby can maintain two normal pre-prandial blood glucose levels consecutively on full 8-hourly feeds

cards.front: What neurodevelopmental follow-up is required after severe neonatal hypoglycaemia?
cards.back: Follow-up at 20 weeks corrected gestational age; consider brain MRI for signs of brain injury; other follow-up dates depend on aetiology

cards.front: Define neonatal hyperglycaemia.
cards.back: Whole blood glucose >7.0 mmol/ℓ OR plasma (laboratory) glucose >8.3 mmol/ℓ

cards.front: Are hyperglycaemic levels in newborns always concerning?
cards.back: No; elevated glucose frequently observed, especially in extreme preterm babies, and does not always require intervention

cards.front: At what plasma glucose level can hyperglycaemia cause osmotic diuresis and polyuria?
cards.back: When plasma glucose >11 mmol/ℓ

cards.front: Is glycosuria a good marker for neonatal hyperglycaemia?
cards.back: No; glycosuria is not a good marker for hyperglycaemia

cards.front: How frequently should blood glucose be monitored in at-risk hyperglycaemic babies?
cards.back: 6–8 hourly in first 24–48 hours (and beyond or more frequently if abnormal)

cards.front: Which babies require glucose monitoring for hyperglycaemia?
cards.back:
1) Babies receiving IV glucose infusions
2) ELBW babies
3) Septic babies
4) Intubated babies
5) Babies with intraventricular haemorrhages
6) Babies receiving inotropes, steroids or insulin

cards.front: List causes of neonatal hyperglycaemia.
cards.back:
1) IV glucose administration
2) ELBW/VLBW status
3) Stress response (sepsis, illness, intubation, IVH, hypoxia)
4) Drugs (corticosteroids, inotropes)
5) Neonatal diabetes mellitus (rare)

cards.front: What is the stepwise approach threshold for treating hyperglycaemia?
cards.back: Stepwise approach in babies with hyperglycaemia >10 mmol/ℓ

cards.front: What is the first step in treating hyperglycaemia >10 mmol/ℓ?
cards.back: Decrease parenteral glucose infusion rate to 4–6 mg/kg/minute by reducing dextrose concentration from 10% to 5% (not below 5%) and/or volume

cards.front: When should insulin therapy be considered in neonatal hyperglycaemia?
cards.back: When persistent hyperglycaemia ≥12 mmol/ℓ despite reductions in glucose infusion rate (always confirm reading with laboratory or blood gas analyzer before starting)

cards.front: Can insulin be given as a bolus in neonatal hyperglycaemia?
cards.back: Never give insulin as a bolus; always discuss with specialist before starting and refer to neonatal unit per local pathways

cards.front: What investigations are needed for hyperglycaemia >10 mmol/ℓ?
cards.back:
1) Investigate and treat underlying cause
2) Septic screen and antibiotics
3) Cranial ultrasound for IVH
4) If neonatal diabetes suspected, discuss with endocrinologist

cards.front: Why must plastic tubing be primed with insulin before neonatal insulin infusion?
cards.back: Insulin binds to tubing, resulting in decreased availability to the baby

cards.front: What is the dose range for neonatal insulin infusion?
cards.back: 0.02–0.5 units/kg/hour

cards.front: How is the neonatal insulin infusion solution prepared?
cards.back: Add 2 ml (100 units) Actrapid to 10 ml normal saline. Then add 1 ml/kg (=10 units/kg) of this solution to 0.9% normal saline to make 50 ml total. Then 0.1 ml/hour = 0.02 units/kg/hour

cards.front: How should blood glucose be monitored during neonatal insulin infusion?
cards.back: Monitor blood glucose level hourly; stop insulin infusion when blood glucose <8 mmol/ℓ

cards.front: What insulin infusion rate for blood glucose >25 mmol/ℓ?
cards.back: 1 ml/hour

cards.front: What insulin infusion rate for blood glucose 20–25 mmol/ℓ?
cards.back: 0.5 ml/hour

cards.front: What insulin infusion rate for blood glucose 18–19 mmol/ℓ?
cards.back: 0.4 ml/hour

cards.front: What insulin infusion rate for blood glucose 15–17 mmol/ℓ?
cards.back: 0.3 ml/hour

cards.front: What insulin infusion rate for blood glucose 12–14 mmol/ℓ?
cards.back: 0.2 ml/hour

cards.front: What insulin infusion rate for blood glucose 8–11 mmol/ℓ?
cards.back: 0.1 ml/hour

cards.front: What insulin infusion rate for blood glucose <8 mmol/ℓ?
cards.back: Stop insulin infusion

cards.front: Define kangaroo care according to WHO
cards.back: Skin-to-skin contact between a mother and her baby along with exclusive breastfeeding. If the mother is unable to provide care, any other family member or caregiver can step into that role.

cards.front: When should kangaroo care contact begin?
cards.back: As early as possible after birth and for as many hours as possible per day.

cards.front: What is the ideal duration of kangaroo care daily for preterm and low birth weight babies?
cards.back: 8 to 24 hours daily

cards.front: Describe intermittent kangaroo care
cards.back: KMC for infants not on full oral feeds, still requiring oxygen therapy. Ranges from few hours a day to once every few days. Duration dependent on baby's condition and mother's availability. Encourages mother to participate in care while baby is in nursery.

cards.front: Describe continuous kangaroo care
cards.back: KMC provided day and night, interrupted only when mother attends to personal needs. Can be practiced in hospital or at home. Ideal for low birth weight infants.

cards.front: What are the three main components of kangaroo care?
cards.back:
1. Kangaroo position
2. Kangaroo nutrition
3. Kangaroo discharge and follow-up

cards.front: Describe the correct kangaroo position
cards.back: Baby placed in upright position against the chest. Neck turned to the side and slightly extended. Nappy and woolen cap worn. Mother/caregiver covered together with baby using clothing/sheet/wrap/blanket.

cards.front: What does kangaroo nutrition involve?
cards.back:
1. Exclusive breastmilk
2. Colostrum
3. Tube feeding initially
4. Rooting reflex improves as baby matures
5. Non-nutritive sucking

cards.front: What support do healthcare workers provide in kangaroo care?
cards.back:
1. Emotional support
2. Education
3. Empowerment of mother to perform care
4. Follow-up post-discharge

cards.front: What role does family play in kangaroo care?
cards.back:
1. Assist to continue practice at home
2. Step in when mother is unable to provide care

cards.front: What are the discharge criteria for a baby in kangaroo care?
cards.back: Baby must be: well, breastfeeding, and gaining weight steadily. Continued until baby chooses not to be in that position again or once weight exceeds 2500 g.

cards.front: What are the maternal discharge criteria for kangaroo care?
cards.back: Mother must be: confident in handling and caring for baby and have support to continue KMC at home.

cards.front: List the benefits of kangaroo care for the mother
cards.back:
1. Empowers mothers to be primary care provider
2. Builds confidence in caring for baby
3. Strengthens attachment and bonding
4. 24% lower risk of moderate to severe post-partum depression

cards.front: List the benefits of kangaroo care for the healthcare facility
cards.back:
1. Significant cost saving through decreased hospital stay
2. Better survival through decreased overcrowding and infection

cards.front: List the immediate benefits of kangaroo care to the infant
cards.back:
1. Decreased hypothermia
2. Increased breastmilk production promoting exclusive breastfeeding
3. Decreased hypoglycaemic episodes
4. Enhances immune function through protective microbiota from mother's skin
5. Stimulates oxytocin secretion

cards.front: What are the effects of oxytocin in kangaroo care?
cards.back:
1. Cardiovascular stability
2. Weight gain
3. Better sleep
4. Fetal/maternal bonding
5. Brain and cognitive strength

cards.front: Name four interventions to decrease complications of prematurity
cards.back:
1. Antenatal steroids
2. CPAP
3. Surfactant
4. Kangaroo care

cards.front: What is the new vision for maternal newborn care in kangaroo care?
cards.back: Mothers, newborns, parents and families form an inseparable centre around which the entire maternal-newborn service delivery is organized, with close collaboration between health care providers.

cards.front: What is immediate kangaroo care?
cards.back: A type of kangaroo care initiated immediately after birth, typically provided in hospital settings for preterm and low birth weight infants requiring close monitoring.

cards.front: Describe the supportive environment that surrounds kangaroo care practice
cards.back: Health care facility, staff, family, and community form the supportive context around the three core KMC components (position, nutrition, discharge).

cards.front: How is kangaroo care adapted when the mother is unable to provide care?
cards.back: Any other family member or caregiver can step into the role of providing kangaroo care.

cards.front: What is the global proportion of neonatal deaths attributable to sepsis or meningitis?
cards.back: 4% of all neonatal deaths globally.

cards.front: List the proportions of global neonatal deaths by cause.
cards.back: Preterm complications 10%, Intrapartum-related 8%, Sepsis/meningitis 4%, Tetanus 1%, Congenital abnormalities 2%, Other 1%, Pneumonia 14%, Diarrhoea 11%, Injury 4%, Measles 1%, Malaria 15%, AIDS 4%, Meningitis 3%, Other disorders 19%.

cards.front: Define neonatal sepsis.
cards.back: Clinical syndrome of bacteremia with systemic signs and symptoms of infection in the first 28 days of life.

cards.front: What happens to maternal IgG levels in the neonate after birth?
cards.back: Passively transferred maternal IgG falls to a transient low at birth–9 months while infant IgM and IgG ramp up; IgA lags behind.

cards.front: What are the two main categories of neonatal infections?
cards.back: 1) Congenital (antenatal acquisition: transplacental, ascending, intrapartum, from birth canal); 2) Acquired (typically occurs later).

cards.front: Define early neonatal infection by time frame.
cards.back: Birth to 7 days of life.

cards.front: Define late neonatal infection by time frame.
cards.back: 8 days to 28 days; acquired in hospital, home, or community.

cards.front: List general risk factors for neonatal infection.
cards.back: 1) Maternal infection (subclinical, UTI, chorioamnionitis); 2) Prematurity (less capable immune response); 3) Absence of IgM/IgA antibodies; 4) Delicate/cracked skin; 5) VLBW neonates with prolonged facility stay; 6) Facility overcrowding, poor hand-washing, understaffing.

cards.front: List risk factors specific to early neonatal infection.
cards.back: 1) Prematurity; 2) Chorioamnionitis; 3) PROM >18 hours; 4) Maternal colonization with Group B Strep.

cards.front: List risk factors specific to late neonatal infection.
cards.back: 1) Prematurity; 2) Invasive procedures; 3) Indwelling devices; 4) Ventilators; 5) VP shunts.

cards.front: List the TORCH organisms causing congenital infection.
cards.back: Toxoplasma; Other (parvo, varicella, hepatitis); Rubella; Cytomegalovirus; Herpes; HIV; Syphilis.

cards.front: List gram-positive organisms causing acquired neonatal infection.
cards.back: Group B Streptococcus (GBS); Streptococcus species; Staphylococcus species; Listeria monocytogenes.

cards.front: List gram-negative organisms causing acquired neonatal infection.
cards.back: Escherichia coli; Klebsiella pneumoniae.

cards.front: List the clinical signs of neonatal sepsis. Why is high index of suspicion needed?
cards.back: Signs are vague: temperature instability, respiratory distress/apnoea, feeding problems/vomiting, lethargy/refusal to suckle, pallor/petechiae/mottling/jaundice, poor perfusion, unstable blood glucose. High suspicion needed because presentation is nonspecific.

cards.front: List nonspecific investigations for neonatal sepsis.
cards.back: Full blood count (FBC); Platelets may be low; White cell count increased or decreased (normal 5–20); Immature-to-total (IT) neutrophil ratio >0.2 predictive; C-reactive protein.

cards.front: List specific investigations for neonatal sepsis.
cards.back: Cultures: blood, CSF, urine, superficial swabs; Serology for congenital infection; Radiology (X-rays of affected areas).

cards.front: List supportive management components for neonatal sepsis.
cards.back: 1) Fluid and electrolyte/glucose correction; 2) Respiratory support—may require ventilation; 3) Cardiovascular support—inotropes for septic shock.

cards.front: What is first-line antimicrobial therapy for suspected neonatal sepsis?
cards.back: Penicillin +/- aminoglycoside. Continue, stop, or change based on culture and sensitivity results.

cards.front: What is the duration of antimicrobial treatment for proven septicemia in neonates?
cards.back: 7 to 10 days.

cards.front: What is the duration of antimicrobial treatment for confirmed bacterial meningitis in neonates?
cards.back: 21 days.

cards.front: List preventive measures for congenital infections.
cards.back: 1) Improved antenatal care; 2) Syphilis testing during pregnancy with documented results and treatment; 3) PMTCT and fast-tracked ARVs for mother; 4) TB screening; 5) Education on respiratory/viral pathogen exposure; 6) Effective management of maternal infections and prolonged rupture of membranes.

cards.front: List preventive measures for acquired (nosocomial) infections.
cards.back: 1) Early skin-to-skin contact with mother (KMC); 2) Exclusive breastfeeding; 3) Strict hand washing; 4) Aseptic technique during procedures; 5) Limit duration of ventilation with active weaning.

cards.front: What is the ranking of HIV as a cause of maternal mortality in South Africa?
cards.back: 3rd leading cause of maternal mortality in South Africa.

cards.front: List important congenital infections in South Africa.
cards.back: HIV; Syphilis; Tuberculosis; Hepatitis B; Malaria; Listeria monocytogenes; COVID-19.

cards.front: What is the mortality rate for congenital TB in South Africa?
What is the key message regarding congenital infections?
cards.back: Most are PREVENTABLE and TREATABLE (especially if early) with good outcome (CURE and CONTROL); if one infection present, GO LOOK for others.

cards.front: What is the source of congenital TB in the neonate?
cards.back: Primary infection in mother, transmitted through the placenta (via umbilical vein) just before or during delivery.

cards.front: Describe the pathophysiology of congenital TB after placental transmission.
cards.back: Tubercle bacilli first reach the liver; periportal lymph node involvement occurs; main fetal circulation leads to infection of many organs.

cards.front: What other routes of congenital TB transmission exist?
cards.back: Ingestion or aspiration of infected amniotic fluid.

cards.front: What is the most common cause of TB at birth in neonates?
cards.back: Postnatal airborne transmission of disease (not congenital).

cards.front: What is the diagnostic yield of gastric aspirate for TB in neonates?
cards.back: Acid-fast smear 8/9; mycobacterial culture 8/9; smear or culture 9/11.

cards.front: What is the diagnostic yield of endotracheal aspirate for TB in neonates?
cards.back: Acid-fast smear 7/7; mycobacterial culture 7/7; smear or culture 7/7.

cards.front: What is the diagnostic yield of CSF for TB in neonates?
cards.back: Acid-fast smear 1/2; mycobacterial culture 1/2; smear or culture 1/2.

cards.front: What is the diagnostic yield of urine for TB in neonates?
cards.back: Acid-fast smear 0/2; mycobacterial culture 0/2; smear or culture 0/2.

cards.front: What is the diagnostic yield of biopsy specimens for TB in neonates?
cards.back: Acid-fast smear 14/19; mycobacterial culture 11/12; smear or culture 16/21.

cards.front: What is the diagnostic yield of lymph node biopsy for TB in neonates?
cards.back: Acid-fast smear 7/8; mycobacterial culture 6/6; smear or culture 7/8. Note: all negative smear/culture specimens showed histopathological changes (granulomas, giant-cell transformation ± caseation).

cards.front: What is the diagnostic yield of liver biopsy for TB in neonates?
cards.back: Acid-fast smear 4/6; mycobacterial culture 1/2; smear or culture 4/6. Note: all negative smear/culture specimens showed histopathological changes.

cards.front: Describe the TB neonate management algorithm at first check-up (<4 weeks) per NEMLC 2019.
cards.back: All pregnant women with TB or recent TB exposure: assess TB symptoms and check sputum testing/X-ray.

cards.front: Describe the TB neonate management at 6 weeks well-baby visit per NEMLC 2019.
cards.back: Continue isoniazid prophylaxis (3HP/INH) for 6 months in HIV-exposed; assess TB symptoms; check sputum testing or X-ray.

cards.front: What is the duration of INH prophylaxis for TB-exposed neonates?
cards.back: 6 months.

cards.front: What is the dosing of isoniazid (INH) for TB prophylaxis in neonates with weight <5 kg?
cards.back: ½ tablet of 100 mg = ~10 mg/kg.

cards.front: What is the dosing of isoniazid (INH) for TB prophylaxis in neonates with weight 5.0–7.9 kg?
cards.back: 1 tablet of 100 mg = ~10 mg/kg.

cards.front: What is the dosing of isoniazid (INH) for TB prophylaxis in neonates with weight 8.0–10.9 kg?
cards.back: 1½ tablets of 100 mg = ~10 mg/kg.

cards.front: What is the dosing of isoniazid (INH) for TB prophylaxis in neonates with weight ≥25 kg?
cards.back: 1 tablet of 300 mg = ~10 mg/kg.

cards.front: List the clinical features of early congenital syphilis.
cards.back: Snuffles; nasal discharge; hepatosplenomegaly; skin rash/desquamation; bone changes; pseudoparalysis; rash on edges of soles; pallor; lymphadenopathy.

cards.front: List the clinical features of late congenital syphilis (>2 years of age).
cards.back: Dentition: Hutchinson's teeth; Eye: interstitial keratitis; Ear: eighth nerve deafness; Skin/face: frontal bossing, saddle nose, rhagades; CNS: mental retardation, hydrocephalus; Bones/joints: sabre shins, Clutton's joints.

cards.front: When should syphilis testing be performed in pregnant women per DOH 2020?
cards.back: At first antenatal care (ANC) visit; if negative before 20 weeks gestation, repeat at 32–34 weeks.

cards.front: What is the principle for treating syphilis in pregnant women?
cards.back: Treat all women with a positive syphilis screening test, irrespective of titer.

cards.front: When is benzathine penicillin indicated for asymptomatic neonates born to RPR-positive mothers?
cards.back: If mother has received <3 doses of benzathine benzylpenicillin, OR if mother delivers within 4 weeks of commencing treatment.

cards.front: What is the dose and route of benzathine penicillin for asymptomatic neonates with maternal syphilis?
cards.back: 50,000 U/kg IM as a single dose (stat).

cards.front: List symptoms in neonates that warrant treatment for congenital syphilis.
cards.back: Hepatosplenomegaly; pseudoparalysis; snuffles; oedema; jaundice; anaemia; purpura; desquamative rash (especially palms and soles).

cards.front: What are the treatment regimens for symptomatic congenital syphilis in neonates?
cards.back: Option 1: Procaine penicillin 50,000 U/kg IM daily for 10 days. Option 2: Benzyl penicillin (penicillin G) 50,000 U/kg/dose IV 12-hourly for 10 days.

cards.front: What is the notification status of congenital syphilis?
cards.back: Notifiable condition.

cards.front: When should HBV testing be performed in pregnant women living with HIV?
cards.back: All women living with HIV on 1st-line ART (containing TDF and 3TC/FTC) are automatically treated for HBV. HIV-negative women should have HBV testing within the first 12 months.

cards.front: What is the vaccination schedule for hepatitis B in neonates?
cards.back: All babies should receive hepatitis B vaccinations in accordance with the Expanded Programme on Immunisation (EPI) schedule.

cards.front: What is the frequency of mother-to-child transmission (MTCT) of malaria?
cards.back: Rare; however, malaria in pregnancy poses serious risks for both mother and baby.

cards.front: What is the key to diagnosing malaria in pregnancy?
cards.back: High index of suspicion. If woman presents with fever in pregnancy, always ask about travel history. Recognize that malaria may be unrecognized or misdiagnosed with severe consequences.

cards.front: What is PMTCT?
cards.back: Prevention of Mother-to-Child Transmission of HIV.

cards.front: When should HIV testing be performed in pregnant women per PMTCT 2020?
cards.back: At first ANC visit and at every subsequent ANC visit (ideal 8 visits total).

cards.front: What is the principle of HIV treatment in pregnancy per Option B+ (2015–present)?
cards.back: Start ALL HIV-positive pregnant women on ART as soon as possible, regardless of gestation, CD4 count, or clinical stage. Continue ART for life.

cards.front: What is the preferred first-line ART regimen for newly diagnosed pregnant women per PMTCT 2020?
cards.back: TDF (tenofovir), 3TC (lamivudine), and DTG (dolutegravir) as a fixed-dose combination.

cards.front: What is an alternative first-line ART regimen for newly diagnosed pregnant women if DTG is not suitable?
cards.back: TDF, 3TC, and EFV (efavirenz) as a fixed-dose combination. Note: EFV carries risks; DTG is now preferred.

cards.front: What special counselling is needed when initiating dolutegravir in pregnant women?
cards.back: Careful, individualised counselling and informed decision-making about neural tube defect (NTD) risk, especially in 1st–3rd trimester.

cards.front: When should co-trimoxazole prophylaxis be commenced in pregnant women living with HIV?
cards.back: After 14 weeks gestation, regardless of CD4 count or clinical stage.

cards.front: What is the viral load threshold indicating successful PMTCT?
cards.back: VL <50 copies/ml.

cards.front: What is the viral load monitoring schedule for newly diagnosed and ART-initiated pregnant women?
cards.back: 1st VL at 3 months on ART; if VL <50 c/ml, repeat VL at delivery.

cards.front: What is the viral load monitoring schedule for women already on ART?
cards.back: VL at first/booking ANC visit; if VL <50 c/ml, repeat VL at delivery.

cards.front: What is the timing of birth HIV-PCR in HIV-exposed neonates?
cards.back: All HIV-exposed infants should receive birth HIV-PCR to identify transmission that occurred in utero.

cards.front: What is the minimum duration of nevirapine (NVP) prophylaxis for all HIV-exposed neonates?
cards.back: Minimum of six weeks post-exposure prophylaxis with NVP.

cards.front: List criteria for identifying high-risk HIV-exposed infants who require additional prophylaxis.
cards.back: 1) Mother with VL ≥1,000 cells/ml at delivery or no VL within 12 weeks of antenatal care; 2) Mother on ART <12 weeks at delivery; 3) Mother had treatment interruption ≥4 weeks during pregnancy.

cards.front: What additional prophylaxis is given to high-risk HIV-exposed breastfed infants?
cards.back: Additional AZT for the first six weeks of life and NVP for at least 12 weeks.

cards.front: What additional prophylaxis is given to high-risk HIV-exposed formula-fed infants?
cards.back: NVP can be stopped earlier than 12 weeks in formula-fed infants.

cards.front: What is the target dose of lamivudine (3TC) for neonates aged 0–28 days?
cards.back: 4 mg/kg twice daily.

cards.front: What is the available formulation of lamivudine (3TC) for neonates?
cards.back: 10 mg/ml.

cards.front: What is the target dose of zidovudine (AZT) for neonates aged 0–28 days?
cards.back: 4 mg/kg twice daily.

cards.front: What is the available formulation of zidovudine (AZT) for neonates?
cards.back: 10 mg/ml.

cards.front: What is the target dose of nevirapine (NVP) for neonates aged 0–28 days?
cards.back: 6 mg/kg twice daily.

cards.front: What is the available formulation of nevirapine (NVP) for neonates?
cards.back: 10 mg/ml.

cards.front: What are the ARV doses for neonates weighing 2.5–3 kg (0–28 days, birth weight ≥2.5 kg)?
cards.back: 3TC: 1.0 ml AM and PM; AZT: 1.0 ml AM and PM; NVP: 2.0 ml AM and PM.

cards.front: What are the ARV doses for neonates weighing 3.1–4 kg (0–28 days, birth weight ≥2.5 kg)?
cards.back: 3TC: 1.5 ml AM and PM; AZT: 1.5 ml AM and PM; NVP: 2.5 ml AM and PM.

cards.front: What are the ARV doses for neonates weighing 4.1–5 kg (0–28 days, birth weight ≥2.5 kg)?
cards.back: 3TC: 2 ml AM and PM; AZT: 2 ml AM and PM; NVP: 3.5 ml AM and PM.

cards.front: What is the principle of ARV dosing in neonates 0–28 days?
cards.back: Dosing is based on birth weight and does not need to change before 28 days (e.g., if weight decreases in first weeks of life).

cards.front: What is recommended for caregivers administering ARV medications to neonates?
cards.back: Caregivers should be supplied with a syringe (5 ml or 3 ml) for each of the 3 ARVs and shown how to prepare and administer the correct dose. Bottles and syringes should be colour-coded if required (e.g. red for AZT, blue for 3TC, green for NVP).

cards.front: What is the peak mortality period for HIV-infected infants in South Africa?
cards.back: 2–4 months of age.

cards.front: What is the target timing for ART initiation in HIV-infected (birth-PCR reactive) neonates?
cards.back: Within 7 days of birth (ideally at 1st post-natal visit in primary health care setting).

cards.front: Describe the management pathway for a neonate with positive birth PCR.
cards.back: 1) Actively trace positive BPCR and link to care; 2) Post-test counselling for mother; 3) Baseline assessment (clinical and lab: HIV PCR, CD4, FBC/Hb, AST); 4) ART adherence counselling; 5) Start ART same day when baby tolerates enteral feeds (AZT/3TC/NVP).

cards.front: What is the follow-up schedule after ART initiation in neonates (1st month)?
cards.back: Review in 1 week: check results, monitor compliance/tolerability, adjust dosage; Review in 2 weeks: monitor compliance/tolerability, adjust dosage; Review at 1 month: monitor compliance, repeat bloods (FBC/Hb, Chol/Tg), change regimen to ABC/3TC/Kaletra.

cards.front: How is congenital CMV diagnosed?
cards.back: Send blood and urine for CMV PCR. If positive or infant <3 weeks of age with confirmed congenital CMV, IgM is most sensitive in this period.

cards.front: List the signs and symptoms of symptomatic congenital CMV infection.
cards.back: Chorioretinitis; neurological abnormalities; microcephaly; hearing impairment; hepatitis; thrombocytopenia; intrauterine growth restriction (IUGR); jaundice; sepsis.

cards.front: What is the treatment regimen for symptomatic congenital CMV in neonates?
cards.back: Ganciclovir IV 6 mg/kg/dose 12-hourly for 6 weeks, then oral valganciclovir 16 mg/kg/dose 12-hourly to complete 6 months total.

cards.front: How is congenital rubella diagnosed?
cards.back: Detected by IgM antibodies (positive in first month). PCR can detect virus from urine, throat swab, blood, or CSF for 6 months.

cards.front: List the neurological and other features of congenital rubella.
cards.back: Deafness; cataracts; cardiac lesions (PDA, peripheral pulmonary stenosis); thrombocytopenia; hepatosplenomegaly; microcephaly; growth failure; blueberry muffin baby appearance (extramedullary haematopoiesis).

cards.front: Is there specific treatment for congenital rubella?
cards.back: No specific treatment. Babies are infectious, so isolation/precautions needed. Ophthalmology and hearing screening essential. Neurodevelopmental follow-up required.

cards.front: Is congenital HSV a true congenital infection?
cards.back: Rarely; HSV is usually acquired postnatally.

cards.front: What are the manifestations of HSV infection in neonates?
cards.back: Skin lesions with vesicles; disseminated form with severe pneumonitis or encephalitis (50–60% mortality without treatment).

cards.front: What is the supportive and specific management of neonatal HSV?
cards.back: Supportive care: parenteral nutrition, ventilation when indicated. Specific: IV aciclovir. Investigation: HSV PCR from skin lesions, blood, CSF; continue monitoring disease activity.

cards.front: What is the dose and duration of high-dose aciclovir for neonatal HSV encephalitis?
cards.back: 20 mg/kg 8-hourly for 3 weeks.

cards.front: What is the duration of high-dose aciclovir for HSV with skin, eye, and mouth (SEM) disease only?
cards.back: 2 weeks.

cards.front: What is the typical mode of presentation of congenital toxoplasmosis?
cards.back: Intrauterine growth restriction (IUGR), intrauterine fetal death (IUFD), or stillbirth.

cards.front: What are the clinical manifestations of congenital toxoplasmosis?
cards.back: Encephalitis; hydrocephalus; cataracts; chorioretinitis.

cards.front: How is congenital toxoplasmosis diagnosed?
cards.back: Serology: IgM/IgG antibodies. Send blood for CMV, EBV, Rubella PCR for diagnosis.

cards.front: What is the prognosis for congenital toxoplasmosis infection timing?
cards.back: All infants infected at all stages of pregnancy may be infected for life once exposed.

cards.front: What is the Mosteller formula for calculating body surface area (BSA)?
cards.back: BSA (m²) = √(height (cm) × weight (kg) / 3600).

cards.front: Which HIV-exposed neonates should be referred for follow-up at a Regional level center?
cards.back: Neonates born <35 weeks gestational age or with birth weight <2.5 kg.

cards.front: Which HIV-exposed neonates can continue follow-up at district level center?
cards.back: Neonates born ≥35 weeks gestational age and with birth weight ≥2.5 kg.

cards.front: When should BCG vaccination NOT be given to HIV-exposed neonates?
cards.back: Do not give BCG if baby is TB-exposed and will be receiving TB prophylaxis.

cards.front: What is the HIV-PCR testing schedule for HIV-exposed neonates?
cards.back: At birth, 10 weeks, 6 months, 10 months, and 18 months.

cards.front: When is HIV antibody testing performed in HIV-exposed neonates?
cards.back: At 18 months for breastfeeding children, or 6 weeks after cessation of breastfeeding if still breastfed at 18 months.

cards.front: When should co-trimoxazole prophylaxis be started in HIV-exposed neonates?
cards.back: From 6 weeks onwards until HIV negative 6 weeks after cessation of breastfeeding.

cards.front: What is required for confirmation of a positive HIV-PCR in neonates?
cards.back: Confirmation of any positive PCR with another sample is mandatory.

cards.front: What food handling measures should pregnant women take to prevent toxoplasmosis?
cards.back: Avoid raw or undercooked lamb, pork, beef, or poultry. Cook all meat until no longer pink and juices run clear.

cards.front: What dairy precautions should pregnant women take?
cards.back: Do not eat unpasteurized milk products (including all soft cheeses).

cards.front: What produce handling measures should pregnant women take?
cards.back: Peel or wash raw fruit and vegetables thoroughly. Wash hands, knives, and cutting boards after handling uncooked food or fluids from their packages.

cards.front: How should pregnant women protect themselves from respiratory infections?
cards.back: Avoid close or intimate contact with adults with communicable respiratory diseases, acute or recent fever, or flu symptoms. Avoid kissing, sharing utensils, drinking from same container. Wash hands frequently; use alcohol gel after handshakes and before eating.

cards.front: What sexual precautions should pregnant women take?
cards.back: Use male latex condoms consistently and correctly. Avoid receptive oral sex with partner with oral herpes or intercourse during 3rd trimester with men with genital herpes. Ensure all sexual contacts of treated STI individuals are linked to care and receive treatment.

cards.front: What blood-contact precautions should pregnant women take?
cards.back: Consider risks of tattoos/body piercing (infected tools transmit hepatitis). Do not share personal care items with blood on them (razors, toothbrushes). Avoid drug use; do not share needles or drug equipment.

cards.front: What is the prevention measure for malaria in pregnant women in endemic areas?
cards.back: Always use insecticide-treated bed nets if living in a malaria endemic area.

cards.front: What precautions should pregnant women take with children with respiratory/rash symptoms?
cards.back: Careful hand washing with soap and running water (use alcohol gel if available) after: exposure to child's bodily fluids, diaper changes, bathing child, handling dirty laundry, touching toys. Avoid close contact: mouth/cheek kissing (kiss head or hug instead), sleeping together, sharing towels. Avoid contact with baby's saliva while feeding.

cards.front: Describe DOH 4-Pillar approach Pillar 1 for prevention of MTCT.
cards.back: Pillar 1: Primary prevention of HIV/STI/TB infection, especially among women of childbearing age.

cards.front: Describe DOH 4-Pillar approach Pillar 2 for prevention of MTCT.
cards.back: Pillar 2: Preventing unintended pregnancies among women diagnosed with HIV/syphilis/TB infections.

cards.front: Describe DOH 4-Pillar approach Pillar 3 for prevention of MTCT.
cards.back: Pillar 3: Preventing disease transmission from a woman living with HIV/syphilis/TB to her infant.

cards.front: Describe DOH 4-Pillar approach Pillar 4 for prevention of MTCT.
cards.back: Pillar 4: Providing appropriate treatment, care, and support to women, their children, partners and families.

cards.front: What should be done if VL is ≥50 c/ml in any PMTCT scenario?
cards.back: Follow the VL Non-suppression Algorithm (referenced on page 21 of guidelines).

cards.front: What screening and management should be performed for PMTCT in HIV+ pregnant women?
cards.back: Screen for TB (and other opportunistic infections); consider TPT (TB preventive therapy). Screen and treat for syphilis.

cards.front: Is CD4 count still required for ART initiation in pregnant women per PMTCT 2020?
cards.back: No, CD4 count is no longer required for ART initiation. However, baseline and follow-up CD4 counts are still checked to determine WHO clinical staging and assess for prophylaxis (e.g., CTX, INH).

cards.front: Should creatinine be checked before ART initiation in pregnant women?
cards.back: Yes, creatinine should be checked at baseline and results followed up.

cards.front: Should pregnant women already on ART continue or switch their regimen?
cards.back: Pregnant women already on ART should continue their current ART regimen during their first ANC visit unless side effects necessitate a switch.

cards.front: Why are maternal infections often undiagnosed in neonatal sepsis risk assessment?
cards.back: Due to lack of or very nonspecific signs in the mother.

cards.front: List the facility-related risk factors for neonatal infection.
cards.back: Overcrowding; poor hand-washing facilities; understaffing.

cards.front: What is the dosing of isoniazid (INH) for TB prophylaxis in neonates with weight 11.0–13.9 kg?
cards.back: 2 tablets of 100 mg = ~10 mg/kg.

cards.front: What is the dosing of isoniazid (INH) for TB prophylaxis in neonates with weight 14.0–19.9 kg?
cards.back: 2½ tablets of 100 mg = ~10 mg/kg.

cards.front: What is the dosing of isoniazid (INH) for TB prophylaxis in neonates with weight 20.0–24.9 kg?
cards.back: 3 tablets of 100 mg = ~10 mg/kg.

cards.front: At what point in TB-exposed neonate follow-up should BCG vaccination be deferred?
cards.back: BCG vaccination must be deferred at every visit until TB-exposure management is complete.

cards.front: What should be done at follow-up visits if a TB-exposed neonate develops symptoms? If asymptomatic?
cards.back: If symptoms develop: investigate for TB. If asymptomatic: continue INH prophylaxis to complete 6 months.

cards.front: Should EPI vaccinations continue in TB-exposed neonates during TB prophylaxis?
cards.back: Yes, continue with EPI vaccinations as per schedule.

cards.front: What must be documented for TB-exposed neonates?
cards.back: INH-prophylaxis (IPT) start date must be documented.

cards.front: What steps are taken after IPT is complete in TB-exposed neonates?
cards.back: Obtain BCG vaccination. If TB is confirmed during follow-up, treat per pediatric TB guidelines.

cards.front: What should be done if INH prophylaxis is delayed due to other clinical complications?
cards.back: Discuss with a TB/HIV expert. Look for evidence of TB disease before starting or restarting INH.

cards.front: What is the recommended breastfeeding duration in HIV-exposed neonates?
cards.back: Exclusive breastfeeding for the first 6 months is recommended.

cards.front: What should be done if the mother is diagnosed with HIV after delivery or during the breastfeeding period?
cards.back: Refer to the Management algorithm for High Maternal VL or HIV Diagnosis after Delivery (page 24).

cards.front: What is the timing target for ART initiation in HIV-infected neonates relative to feeding tolerance?
cards.back: Start ART the same day when baby can tolerate enteral feeds.

cards.front: In infants <3 weeks old with confirmed congenital CMV, what diagnostic test is most sensitive?
cards.back: IgM is most sensitive in infants less than 3 weeks of age.

cards.front: What specimens can be used for rubella PCR to detect virus in congenital rubella?
cards.back: Urine, throat swab, blood, or cerebrospinal fluid (CSF). PCR can detect virus for 6 months.

cards.front: What is important to remember about congenital rubella infection in neonates?
cards.back: Babies are infectious, but there is no specific treatment for congenital rubella.

cards.front: What is a critical risk with malaria in pregnancy?
cards.back: Malaria can be misdiagnosed with severe consequences in pregnancy; high index of suspicion needed.

cards.front: Define: birth defect
cards.back: Abnormality of structure or function present from birth; diagnosed clinically at birth or later in life; often presents as abnormal appearance or failure to grow/develop normally

cards.front: Define: malformations (in context of birth defects)
cards.back: Commonest form of birth defect; congenital malformations develop in 1st trimester from failure of embryo to develop normally; result in birth defects affecting ≥1 organs

cards.front: Define: birth prevalence
cards.back: Measure of how common a birth defect is among newborns; expressed as number of affected infants per 1000 live births

cards.front: Define: population prevalence (in birth defects)
cards.back: Measure of how common a birth defect is in general population; expressed as number of affected individuals per 1000/10,000/100,000 people

cards.front: What % of infants have ≥1 congenital abnormality?
What % of infants have chromosome abnormality or single gene disorder?
What % of adults have genetic disorder?
Breakdown of birth defect etiology: genetic vs non-genetic vs unknown
cards.back: 40% preconceptual genetic (chromosomal, single gene, multifactorial); 10% post-conception non-genetic (teratogens, constraint); 50% unknown

cards.front: Define: cell nucleus
cards.back: Location where chromosomes live

cards.front: Define: chromosomes (in genetics)
cards.back: Contain all genetic material of a cell

cards.front: Define: DNA
cards.back: Material from which chromosomes are constructed

cards.front: Define: gene
cards.back: Segment of a chromosome made up of DNA; codes for inherited biochemical product (enzyme/protein) or physical feature

cards.front: Define: diploid number (in humans)
cards.back: 46 chromosomes

cards.front: Define: haploid number (in humans)
cards.back: 23 chromosomes

cards.front: Define: meiosis
cards.back: Cell division in which gametes are formed and number of chromosomes is halved

cards.front: What chromosomes/genetic material does ovum contain post-meiosis?
cards.back: 22 autosomes + 1 X chromosome

cards.front: What chromosomes/genetic material does sperm contain post-meiosis?
cards.back: 22 autosomes + either Y or X chromosome

cards.front: How many chromosomes do humans have total?
