cards.back: 46 chromosomes: 22 autosomes + 1 pair of sex chromosomes

cards.front: Female human karyotype
cards.back: 46,XX

cards.front: Male human karyotype
cards.back: 46,XY

cards.front: How is chromosome inheritance determined (which parent contributes which)?
cards.back: For each chromosome pair: 1 chromosome inherited from mother, 1 from father

cards.front: Define: non-disjunction
cards.back: During gamete formation, pair of parent chromosomes splits abnormally → 1 gamete with 24 chromosomes, 1 gamete with 22 chromosomes

cards.front: Define: trisomy
cards.back: Presence of 47 chromosomes (extra chromosome); results from abnormal gamete with 24 chromosomes + normal gamete with 23 chromosomes

cards.front: Define: monosomy
cards.back: Presence of 45 chromosomes (missing chromosome); results from abnormal gamete with 22 chromosomes + normal gamete with 23 chromosomes

cards.front: List 3 common trisomies
cards.back: Trisomy 13, Trisomy 18, Trisomy 21

cards.front: Which monosomy is compatible with life?
cards.back: Turner syndrome (45,X) - only monosomy compatible with life

cards.front: Turner syndrome karyotype
Define: mosaicism
cards.back: Presence of 2 different cell lines of same genetic origin in same person; occurs when monosomy cell usually dies and trisomy cell continues dividing

cards.front: Define: translocation (chromosomal)
cards.back: Piece of chromosome breaks off and joins another chromosome

cards.front: Define: balanced translocation
cards.back: Piece of chromosome breaks off and joins another with NO genetic material lost or gained; person clinically normal but at risk of passing unbalanced translocations to offspring; can cause recurrent miscarriages

cards.front: Define: unbalanced translocation
cards.back: Translocation with genetic material lost or gained

cards.front: Define: deletion (chromosomal)
cards.back: Piece of chromosome of variable length is missing

cards.front: What deletion syndrome is associated with chromosome 15?
cards.back: Prader-Willi syndrome and Angelman syndrome

cards.front: What deletion syndrome is associated with a specific piece of chromosome 22?
cards.back: Deletion 22

cards.front: What deletion syndrome is associated with small arm of chromosome 5?
cards.back: Cri du chat

cards.front: Define: chromosomal duplication
cards.back: Piece of chromosome copies itself; resultant chromosome has 2 identical pieces of same chromosome; example: Cat Eye Syndrome

cards.front: Define: ring chromosome
cards.back: One end of chromosome breaks off; broken end sticks to other end making ring shape

cards.front: How many gene copies (alleles) does each person have for any given gene?
cards.back: 2 alleles; can be: 2 normally functioning, OR 1 normal + 1 abnormal, OR 2 abnormal functioning

cards.front: Define: dominant disorder
cards.back: Disorder in which only 1 abnormal gene copy is required for expression of phenotype

cards.front: Define: recessive disorder
cards.back: Disorder in which both gene copies must be abnormal for expression of phenotype (homozygous state)

cards.front: Define: gene mutation
cards.back: Abnormality in a gene that can occur spontaneously or from environmental factors

cards.front: List characteristics of autosomal dominant (AD) inheritance
cards.back: 1. Affects males and females equally; 2. Evidence of transmission across generations; 3. Transmission between all sexes possible (male-male, female-female, male-female, female-male); 4. Affected individual × normal = 50% affected children per pregnancy

cards.front: What is the recurrence risk for affected parent (AD) × normal parent?
cards.back: 50% chance of affected children with each pregnancy

cards.front: Waardenburg syndrome origin: what % from affected parent vs de novo?
cards.back: 2/3 from affected parent; 1/3 de novo mutation (associated with advanced paternal age)

cards.front: Waardenburg syndrome prevalence
List clinical features of Waardenburg syndrome (5)
cards.back: 1. Heterochromia iridis (different colored irises); 2. Abnormal tear ducts; 3. Sensorineural deafness; 4. White forelock; 5. Early greying of hair and eyebrows; 6. Partial albinism

cards.front: List complications of Waardenburg syndrome
cards.back: 1. Deafness → lack of speech/communication, schooling problems, social stigma; 2. Recurrent eye infections from abnormal tear ducts; 3. Normal intelligence but may present with developmental delay due to lack of speech and communication inability

cards.front: List management/care strategies for Waardenburg syndrome
cards.back: 1. Early diagnosis and intervention for deafness; 2. Audiology (hearing aids); 3. Speech therapy; 4. Schools for hearing impaired; 5. Treat recurrent eye infections; 6. Genetic counselling; 7. Psychosocial support

cards.front: List major clinical features of Marfan syndrome (4 domains)
cards.back: 1. Skeletal: tall stature, arachnodactyly (long slender digits), long thin feet, pectus excavatum, scoliosis, lumbar lordosis; 2. Ocular: lens dislocation (ectopia lentis); 3. Cardiovascular: aortic dilatation, aortic aneurysm; 4. General: arm span exceeding height

cards.front: Define: arachnodactyly
cards.back: Abnormally long, slender fingers and toes

cards.front: What is Walker-Murdoch wrist sign in Marfan syndrome?
cards.back: Thumb crosses palm with distal phalanx extending well beyond ulnar border; positive sign indicates arachnodactyly

cards.front: What is Steinberg thumb sign in Marfan syndrome?
cards.back: Thumb opposed across palm extending beyond ulnar border when closed fist; positive sign indicates arachnodactyly

cards.front: What is ectopia lentis?
cards.back: Subluxation (dislocation) of the lens; seen in Marfan syndrome; lens edge visible at pupillary border

cards.front: List clinical features of myotonic dystrophy (autosomal dominant)
cards.back: 1. Frontal balding; 2. Hatchet facies from temporalis muscle atrophy; 3. Cataracts; 4. Ptosis and drooping mouth from facial muscle weakness; 5. Sternocleidomastoid wasting; 6. Gynecomastia

cards.front: What is anticipation in myotonic dystrophy?
cards.back: Pattern of inheritance in myotonic dystrophy showing earlier age of onset and increased severity in successive generations; autosomal dominant pattern with anticipation

cards.front: List clinical features of achondroplasia
cards.back: 1. Relative macrocephaly (large head relative to body); 2. Frontal bossing; 3. Short proximal (rhizomelic) limbs; 4. Short trident hands; 5. Lumbar lordosis; 6. Short stature; 7. Short-limbed dwarfism

cards.front: List major clinical features of neurofibromatosis (NF-1)
cards.back: 1. Skin: café-au-lait spots, neurofibromas of varying sizes; 2. Ophthalmologic: visual impairment/blindness, optic glioma, Lisch nodules; 3. Skeletal: scoliosis, bone deformities, pseudarthrosis; 4. CNS: seizures, headaches, brain tumors, macrocephaly, learning disabilities, mental retardation; 5. Vascular: high BP, brain blood vessel defects; 6. Other: speech impairments, digestive symptoms, freckling in skin folds (armpits, groin), early/delayed puberty, short stature, cancer risk

cards.front: Define: café-au-lait spots
cards.back: Light-brown macules on skin; characteristic feature of neurofibromatosis; usually well-circumscribed

cards.front: What are Lisch nodules?
cards.back: Tan/orange pigmented nodules on iris surface; pathognomonic of neurofibromatosis type 1 (NF-1)

cards.front: What is Crowe's sign (axillary freckling)?
cards.back: Dense small brown freckles in axillae (armpits); pathognomonic of neurofibromatosis type 1 (NF-1)

cards.front: List clinical features of osteogenesis imperfecta
cards.back: 1. Skeletal fractures; 2. Brittle bones; 3. Blue sclerae

cards.front: List clinical features of ectodermal dysplasia
cards.back: 1. Hypohidrosis (decreased sweating); 2. Transparent/translucent skin; 3. Abnormal and absent teeth; 4. Hypodontia (missing teeth); 5. Abnormal abdomen

cards.front: List characteristics of autosomal recessive (AR) inheritance
cards.back: 1. Affects males and females equally; 2. Trait manifest only when homozygous (2 abnormal alleles); 3. Heterozygotes perfectly healthy; 4. 2 heterozygous parents → 25% affected offspring per pregnancy; 5. Consanguinity often involved; 6. Affected individuals usually in single generation

cards.front: What is recurrence risk for 2 heterozygous parents (AR)?
cards.back: 25% affected offspring with each pregnancy (1 normal:2 carriers:1 affected ratio)

cards.front: Define: oculocutaneous albinism
cards.back: Lack of melanin in skin, hair, and eyes; autosomal recessive inheritance

cards.front: Oculocutaneous albinism prevalence
cards.back: 1/4000 in black population; higher in communities with consanguinity

cards.front: List skin features of oculocutaneous albinism
cards.back: 1. Pale, corn-colored hair; 2. Lighter than normal skin

cards.front: List ocular features of oculocutaneous albinism
cards.back: 1. Lighter than normal eyes; 2. Photophobia (light sensitivity); 3. Nystagmus; 4. Poor vision; 5. Astigmatism; 6. No binocular vision

cards.front: List skin complications of oculocutaneous albinism
cards.back: 1. Sunburn; 2. Rapid aging of skin; 3. Skin cancer risk

cards.front: List ocular complications of oculocutaneous albinism
cards.back: 1. Sun-induced damage to eyes; 2. Abnormal eyesight; 3. Poor school performance from visual disability

cards.front: List social/cultural complications of oculocutaneous albinism
cards.back: Social stigmatism from African myths regarding albinism

cards.front: List management/care strategies for oculocutaneous albinism
cards.back: 1. Skin care: sunscreen, clothes to cover skin, umbrellas, hats; 2. Eye care: sunglasses, regular ophthalmic reviews; 3. NDT therapy for severe visual disability; 4. Genetic counselling; 5. Psychosocial support

cards.front: List clinical features of cystic fibrosis
cards.back: 1. Malabsorption; 2. Failure to thrive; 3. Recurrent chest infections; 4. Protuberant abdomen; 5. Wasted limbs

cards.front: List clinical features of spinal muscular atrophy (SMA) type 1 (Werdnig-Hoffmann)
cards.back: 1. Frog-legged posture (abducted hips); 2. Hypotonia (low muscle tone); 3. Flaccid lying position; 4. Paradoxical breathing pattern

cards.front: List common autosomal recessive disorders
cards.back: 1. Adrenogenital syndrome; 2. Oculocutaneous albinism; 3. Cystic fibrosis; 4. Deafness (some types); 5. Galactosaemia; 6. Mucopolysaccharidoses; 7. Phenylketonuria (PKU); 8. Retinitis pigmentosa (some); 9. Sickle cell anemia; 10. Spinal muscular atrophy; 11. Tay-Sachs disease; 12. Thalassemia

cards.front: Define: X-linked (sex-linked) inheritance
cards.back: Genes carried on the X chromosome; inheritance pattern where genes are located on sex chromosomes

cards.front: Define: hemizygous
cards.back: Male with abnormal allele on single X chromosome; genotype has only one copy of X-linked gene

cards.front: How is X-linked recessive expressed in males?
cards.back: ALWAYS manifest; male has no normal allele on X to counteract abnormal allele (hemizygous state)

cards.front: How is X-linked recessive expressed in females?
cards.back: Manifest only when homozygous (2 abnormal alleles on both X chromosomes)

cards.front: What is recurrence risk for carrier mother × unaffected father (X-linked recessive like haemophilia)?
cards.back: Sons: 50% affected; Daughters: 50% carriers

cards.front: What is recurrence risk for affected father × unaffected mother (X-linked recessive like haemophilia)?
cards.back: Sons: 0% affected (receive Y from father); Daughters: 100% carriers (receive X from father)

cards.front: What is male-to-male transmission in X-linked inheritance?
cards.back: ABSENT; males cannot transmit X-linked disorders to sons (sons receive Y from father); characteristic feature of X-linked recessive pedigrees

cards.front: Define: haemophilia
cards.back: Inherited bleeding disorder affecting mainly males; X-linked inheritance; gene defects on chromosome X

cards.front: Haemophilia A: which clotting factor is deficient?
cards.back: Factor 8 (Factor VIII)

cards.front: Haemophilia B: which clotting factor is deficient?
cards.back: Factor 9 (Factor IX / Christmas factor)

cards.front: What % of female carriers of haemophilia have signs of mild haemophilia?
Haemophilia A prevalence in white males
Haemophilia A prevalence in black males
cards.back: 1 in 20,000 (though poor registration may affect accuracy)

cards.front: List sites of bleeding in haemophilia
cards.back: 1. Skin; 2. Mucosa; 3. Muscles; 4. Joints (haemarthrosis); 5. Internal organs; 6. Brain

cards.front: What determines severity of haemophilia?
cards.back: Concentration of clotting factors; higher concentration = milder disease

cards.front: What diagnostic test is abnormal in haemophilia?
cards.back: PTT (Partial Thromboplastin Time) is prolonged; clotting factor levels are low; gene/DNA testing available but expensive

cards.front: List complications of haemophilia
cards.back: 1. Haemarthrosis (joint bleeding); 2. Soft tissue bleeding; 3. Internal bleeding; 4. Intracranial bleeding → headache, vomiting, lethargy

cards.front: List clinical clues suggesting haemophilia in newborns/children
cards.back: 1. Large cephalhaematoma in newborn; 2. Excess bleeding from circumcision; 3. Prolonged/repeated nosebleeds; 4. Early and excessive bleeding after minor trauma; 5. Deep muscle haematoma; 6. Haemarthrosis (joint bleeding)

cards.front: List signs suggesting female carrier of haemophilia
cards.back: 1. Close relative with haemophilia; 2. Menorrhagia (heavy periods); 3. Easy bruising; 4. Bleeding post-trauma; 5. Bleeding post-surgery; 6. Bleeding at childbirth

cards.front: What is treatment of haemophilia?
cards.back: Factor replacement therapy

cards.front: What are management strategies for haemarthrosis and soft tissue haematomata in haemophilia?
cards.back: 1. Ice packs on affected area; 2. Physiotherapy and OT to prevent contractures; 3. Factor replacement for major bleeding

cards.front: What is the inheritance pattern in haemophilia?
cards.back: 80% from carrier mothers; 10% of carrier mothers have mild signs/symptoms of haemophilia; 20% de novo mutations

cards.front: What is important to confirm/exclude before pregnancy in female carrier of haemophilia?
cards.back: Diagnosis must be confirmed or excluded; if carrier confirmed, risk of heavy bleeding during vaginal delivery or cesarean section; male fetus has 1-2% risk of intracranial bleed

cards.front: What precautions should be taken in haemophilia infants regarding vaccinations?
cards.back: NO circumcision; NO IM (intramuscular) injections; SC (subcutaneous) immunization only

cards.front: What is emergency treatment for neck swelling/bleeding in haemophilia?
cards.back: EMERGENCY situation (risk of airway compromise); treat with MAJOR dose of clotting factor

cards.front: What is management of soft tissue bleeds and bruising in haemophilia?
cards.back: No functional impairment; tenderness but no severe pain; NO factor replacement needed

cards.front: What is management of deltoid/forearm bleeds in haemophilia?
cards.back: Routine factor dose; MAJOR factor dose if compartment syndrome suspected

cards.front: What is presentation and management of iliopsoas bleed in haemophilia?
cards.back: Presentation: flexed hip, pain, inability to extend leg on affected side; Management: MAJOR dose of factor replacement

cards.front: What is presentation and management of buttock bleed in haemophilia?
cards.back: Presentation: pain ± swelling; Management: routine factor dose; MAJOR factor dose if leg tingling/swelling on affected side

cards.front: What is presentation and management of thigh/calf bleed in haemophilia?
cards.back: Presentation: pain ± swelling, impaired mobility; Management: routine factor dose; MAJOR factor dose if compartment syndrome suspected

cards.front: What is Gower's manoeuvre in Duchenne muscular dystrophy?
cards.back: Characteristic sign of proximal muscle weakness; patient uses hands to push body up from floor due to weak hip and thigh muscles

cards.front: What is calf hypertrophy in Duchenne muscular dystrophy?
cards.back: Bilateral calf muscle pseudohypertrophy (false hypertrophy from fat/fibrous tissue replacement of muscle); classic sign of Duchenne dystrophy

cards.front: Define: fetal alcohol syndrome (FAS)
cards.back: Alcohol-related birth defects and neurodevelopmental disorders resulting from prenatal alcohol exposure

cards.front: List discriminating facial features of FAS (5)
cards.back: 1. Short palpebral fissures (shortened eye openings); 2. Flat midface; 3. Short nose; 4. Indistinct philtrum; 5. Thin upper lip

cards.front: List associated facial features of FAS (4)
cards.back: 1. Epicanthal folds; 2. Low nasal bridge; 3. Minor ear anomalies; 4. Micrognathia (small jaw)

cards.front: Define: palpebral fissures
cards.back: Opening between eyelids; short palpebral fissures means horizontally shortened eye openings

cards.front: Define: philtrum
cards.back: Groove/indentation between nose and upper lip

cards.front: List systemic features of FAS
cards.back: 1. Microcephaly; 2. Low birth weight; 3. Delayed growth/development; 4. Intellectual disability; 5. Behavior problems

cards.front: What congenital malformations can result from prenatal alcohol exposure?
cards.back: Cleft lip and palate

cards.front: FAS prevalence in Western Cape (South Africa)
cards.back: 50 per 1000 population

cards.front: FAS prevalence in Soweto (South Africa)
cards.back: 20 per 1000 population

cards.front: List management/rehabilitation strategies for FAS
cards.back: 1. Neurodevelopmental therapy; 2. Community-based rehabilitation; 3. Physiotherapy for slow motor milestones; 4. OT for fine motor coordination and social development; 5. Audiology and speech therapy; 6. Psychosocial counselling and intervention

cards.front: What maternal cardiac condition causes teratogenic risk?
cards.back: Maternal cardiac disease on Warfarin → Warfarin Embryopathy risk

cards.front: What maternal metabolic condition causes teratogenic risk?
cards.back: Poorly controlled diabetes (high HbA1c) → Diabetic Embryopathy

cards.front: What psychiatric drug causes teratogenic risk?
cards.back: Lithium (mood stabilizer) → MHC-related embryopathy risk

cards.front: What antiepileptic drug causes teratogenic risk?
cards.back: Valproate (valproic acid) → Valproate Embryopathy risk

cards.front: What dermatologic drug causes teratogenic risk?
cards.back: Retinoic acid (retinoid) → Retinoic acid Embryopathy risk

cards.front: What chemotherapy causes teratogenic risk?
cards.back: Maternal chemotherapy → associated embryopathy risk

cards.front: Define: multifactorial inheritance
cards.back: Multiple genes interact with one another and with environmental factors to cause phenotype; GENE-GENE + GENE-ENVIRONMENT interactions

cards.front: In multifactorial inheritance, how does risk compare in relatives vs general population?
cards.back: Higher risk in relatives than in general population

cards.front: What is recurrence risk for multifactorial traits in future pregnancies?
cards.back: 3-5% recurrence risk in future pregnancies

cards.front: List examples of multifactorial congenital malformations
cards.back: 1. Cleft lip and palate; 2. Neural tube defects (anencephaly, myelomeningocele)

cards.front: Define: neural tube defects (NTD)
cards.back: Congenital malformations from failure of neural tube (which develops into skull, brain, spinal cord, nerves) to close at end of 4th week post-conception

cards.front: Define: anencephaly
cards.back: Neural tube defect with absent brain; sometimes called SB (spina bifida) or END (exencephaly with no development); incompatible with life

cards.front: Define: encephalocele
cards.back: Failure of midline skull closure between eyes and occiput; meninges with or without brain tissue protrude through skull defect

cards.front: Define: spina bifida
cards.back: Opening in spinal column from failure of closure of bony vertebral arches

cards.front: List 3 forms of spina bifida
cards.back: 1. Meningomyelocele (meninges and spinal cord protrude); 2. Meningocoele (meninges only protrude); 3. Spina bifida occulta (hidden defect, no protrusion)

cards.front: What role does folic acid play in neural tube closure?
cards.back: Folic acid is necessary (NB = nota bene/important) for proper closure of the neural tube during 4th week post-conception

cards.front: List genetic/chromosomal causes of neural tube defects
cards.back: 1. Chromosomal abnormalities; 2. Single gene defects; 3. Multifactorial conditions (genes + environmental factors)

cards.front: How do epilepsy drugs affect neural tube closure?
cards.back: Epilepsy drugs (anticonvulsants like valproate) antagonize/work against the protective effect of folic acid on neural tube closure

cards.front: List environmental factors affecting neural tube development
cards.back: 1. Viral infections; 2. Dietary deficiencies; 3. Toxic exposures; 4. Radiation

cards.front: What is recurrence risk for neural tube defects in future pregnancies?
cards.back: 3-5% (higher than general population)

cards.front: List medical management strategies for neural tube defects
cards.back: 1. Antibiotics for meningitis/UTI prevention; 2. Anticonvulsants for epilepsy and encephalocele-related seizures; 3. Urinary catheterization for neurogenic bladder

cards.front: List surgical management of neural tube defects
cards.back: 1. Neurosurgery to close the defect; 2. VP (ventriculoperitoneal) shunt placement for associated hydrocephalus

cards.front: List rehabilitation for neural tube defects
cards.back: NDT (Neurorehabilitation/Neurodevelopmental) therapy including: physiotherapy, occupational therapy, speech therapy, audiology

cards.front: List prevention strategies for neural tube defects
cards.back: 1. Periconceptual folic acid supplementation; 2. Genetic screening and counselling; 3. Food fortification with folic acid; 4. Dietary supplements

cards.front: List screening methods for neural tube defects during pregnancy
cards.back: 1. Maternal serum screening (FP = first pass screen); 2. Ultrasound screening (18-23 weeks); 3. If AFP elevated but US normal → amniocentesis showing elevated AFP + acetylcholinesterase

cards.front: Down syndrome karyotype
cards.back: 47,XX or 47,XY,+21 (trisomy 21; three copies of chromosome 21)

cards.front: List general approach to diagnosing birth defects (3 steps)
cards.back: 1. FULL history: birth history, PMHx, social history, FmHx (3-generation pedigree); 2. Assess dysmorphic features; 3. Investigations: karyotyping, gene testing; THEN offer genetic counselling + psychosocial support

cards.front: List treatment modalities for birth defects
cards.back: 1. Medical management; 2. Surgical intervention; 3. Neurorehabilitation/NDT; 4. Genetic counselling and psychosocial support

cards.front: List primary prevention strategies for birth defects
cards.back: 1. Basic reproductive health approaches; 2. Family planning (awareness of advanced maternal age); 3. Periconception care; 4. Improve diet of women of childbearing age; 5. Community education on teratogens; 6. Avoid/treat maternal infections; immunize girls (e.g., rubella); 7. Treat maternal health problems (e.g., diabetes); 8. Avoid teratogenic drugs (warfarin, lithium, anticonvulsants)

cards.front: Define: genetic screening
cards.back: Process to identify pregnant women at increased risk of having babies with birth defects; also screens for carrier states

cards.front: What is periconception screening in genetic counselling?
cards.back: Screening before pregnancy that allows reproductive choices (e.g., contraception, IVF with PGD, adoption, acceptance of risk)

cards.front: What is prenatal screening in genetic counselling?
cards.back: Screening during pregnancy that allows genetic counselling, diagnosis, and option of termination of pregnancy (TOP) if abnormality detected

cards.front: List prenatal screening methods for birth defects
cards.back: 1. Ultrasound screening; 2. Maternal serum screening; 3. Rhesus blood group screening

cards.front: What is postnatal newborn screening?
cards.back: Screening of newborns after birth for birth defects (e.g., Down syndrome) and metabolic disorders (e.g., congenital hypothyroidism)

cards.front: List indications for genetic counselling (9 categories)
cards.back: 1. People with birth defect; 2. Parents of fetus/baby/child with birth defect; 3. Unexplained IUD, neonatal or infant death; 4. Family history of birth defect in those planning pregnancy; 5. Consanguinity (related parents); 6. Carriers or at-risk of carrier status; 7. Diagnosis of abnormal fetus on ultrasound; 8. Recurrent miscarriages or bad obstetric history (BOH); 9. Maternal disease (e.g., diabetes); 10. Advanced maternal age (AMA), advanced paternal age (APA), abnormal US screening, positive maternal serum testing

cards.front: Why is early identification of birth defects important?
cards.back: Early identification allows best response from treatment, early genetic counselling, and early psychosocial support for family

cards.front: When a definite diagnosis cannot be made, what should be provided?
cards.back: Recognition of disabilities and provision of appropriate treatment; genetic counselling; psychosocial support

cards.front: List what genetic history should include (4 components)
cards.back: 1. Birth history; 2. Past medical history (PMHx); 3. Social history; 4. Family history (FmHx) - 3 generations/pedigree

cards.front: What are dysmorphic features?
cards.back: Abnormal physical features or characteristics that may indicate genetic syndrome or birth defect; assessed as part of clinical examination

cards.front: Trisomy 21 (Down syndrome) karyotype
List clinical features of Down syndrome (Trisomy 21)
cards.back: 1. Intellectual disability; 2. Hypotonia; 3. Flat facial profile; 4. Upslanting palpebral fissures; 5. Low nasal bridge; 6. Single palmar crease; 7. Cardiac defects (AV canal, VSD); 8. Short stature; 9. Increased risk of leukemia; 10. Thyroid disease

cards.front: Down syndrome (Trisomy 21) prevalence
cards.back: Approximately 1 in 700 live births (varies by maternal age)

cards.front: Trisomy 18 (Edward syndrome) karyotype
List clinical features of Edward syndrome (Trisomy 18)
cards.back: 1. Severe growth restriction; 2. Intellectual disability; 3. Distinctive clenched fists (overlapping fingers); 4. Rocker bottom feet; 5. Microcephaly; 6. Micrognathia; 7. Low-set ears; 8. Congenital heart defects (VSD, PDA, ASD); 9. Renal anomalies; 10. Cleft lip/palate; 11. Short lifespan (most die within first year)

cards.front: Edward syndrome (Trisomy 18) prevalence
cards.back: Approximately 1 in 6000 live births

cards.front: Trisomy 13 (Patau syndrome) karyotype
List clinical features of Patau syndrome (Trisomy 13)
cards.back: 1. Severe growth restriction; 2. Intellectual disability; 3. Holoprosencephaly; 4. Cleft lip/palate; 5. Polydactyly (postaxial); 6. Rocker bottom feet; 7. Microcephaly/abnormal head shape; 8. Micrognathia; 9. Congenital heart defects; 10. Renal anomalies; 11. Cryptorchidism; 12. Scalp defects; 13. Short lifespan (most die within first year)

cards.front: Patau syndrome (Trisomy 13) prevalence
cards.back: Approximately 1 in 10,000 live births

cards.front: Klinefelter syndrome karyotype
cards.back: 47,XXY

cards.front: List clinical features of Klinefelter syndrome (47,XXY)
cards.back: 1. Tall stature with long legs; 2. Infertility/hypogonadism; 3. Reduced testosterone levels; 4. Gynecomastia (breast tissue development); 5. Reduced facial/body hair; 6. Increased risk of developmental delay; 7. Small/soft testes; 8. Often asymptomatic (late diagnosis)

cards.front: Klinefelter syndrome prevalence
cards.back: Approximately 1 in 500 to 1 in 1000 males; many cases undiagnosed

cards.front: Turner syndrome (45,X) - List clinical features
cards.back: 1. Short stature; 2. Ovarian dysgenesis/infertility; 3. Characteristic facial features (broad flat face, micrognathia, low-set ears); 4. Webbed neck; 5. Shield chest; 6. Cardiac defects (bicuspid aortic valve, aortic stenosis, coarctation); 7. Renal anomalies; 8. Normal intelligence usually (learning difficulties possible); 9. Lymphoedema; 10. Hearing loss

cards.front: Turner syndrome prevalence
cards.back: Approximately 1 in 2500 live female births

cards.front: Marfan syndrome inheritance pattern and prevalence
cards.back: Autosomal dominant inheritance (caused by FBN1 gene mutation on chromosome 15); prevalence approximately 1 in 5000 live births

cards.front: Define: frontal bossing (dysmorphology)
cards.back: Prominent protrusion of forehead; common in achondroplasia, Down syndrome, congenital hydrocephalus

cards.front: Define: macrocephaly (dysmorphology)
cards.back: Abnormally large head; can result from hydrocephalus, neurofibromatosis, or overgrowth syndromes

cards.front: Define: microcephaly (dysmorphology)
cards.back: Abnormally small head with reduced brain volume; associated with intellectual disability, congenital infections, NTDs

cards.front: Define: syndactyly (dysmorphology)
cards.back: Webbing or fusion of digits (fingers or toes); can be simple (skin only) or complex (bone fusion); seen in Apert syndrome

cards.front: Define: polydactyly (dysmorphology)
cards.back: Extra digits (fingers or toes); can be preaxial (thumb/great toe side) or postaxial (pinky/little toe side); seen in Trisomy 13

cards.front: Define: micrognathia (dysmorphology)
cards.back: Abnormally small chin/jaw; seen in Trisomy 18, Pierre Robin sequence

cards.front: Define: holoprosencephaly (dysmorphology)
cards.back: Failure of the forebrain to divide into two hemispheres; associated with facial midline defects and intellectual disability; seen in Trisomy 13

cards.front: Folic acid prophylaxis for neural tube defect prevention - recommended periconceptual dose
cards.back: 5 mg daily (high-dose) or standard 400 mcg daily; started before conception and continued through first trimester

cards.front: Fetal alcohol spectrum disorder (FASD) - at what gestational age is alcohol teratogenic?
cards.back: All trimesters carry risk; first trimester has highest risk for CNS and facial features; third trimester for growth restriction; no safe threshold established

cards.front: Valproate (Depakote) embryopathy - what birth defects?
cards.back: 1. Neural tube defects (spina bifida) - 1-2% risk; 2. Cardiac defects; 3. Craniofacial abnormalities; 4. Digital anomalies; 5. Growth restriction; 6. Developmental delay

cards.front: Lithium teratogenic effects - what cardiac defect?
cards.back: Ebstein anomaly (displacement of tricuspid valve); also associated with cardiac arrhythmias if used in third trimester; teratogenic window first trimester

cards.front: Warfarin embryopathy - what birth defects?
cards.back: 1. Fetal warfarin syndrome: nasal hypoplasia, skeletal abnormalities (stippled epiphyses); 2. CNS abnormalities: microcephaly, hydrocephalus, agenesis of corpus callosum; 3. Eye abnormalities; 4. Growth restriction; teratogenic window first trimester

cards.front: Retinoic acid (retinoids/Accutane) embryopathy - what birth defects?
cards.back: Highly teratogenic - 1st trimester exposure causes: 1. CNS malformations (hydrocephalus, microcephaly, cleft palate); 2. Cardiac defects; 3. Facial clefting; 4. Thymic hypoplasia; 5. Growth restriction; 6. Intellectual disability

cards.front: ACE inhibitors teratogenic effects - critical window and defects
cards.back: Second and third trimester exposure: renal dysgenesis, oligohydramnios, intrauterine growth restriction, fetal/neonatal renal failure, oliguria, hypotension, and fetal death; first trimester use generally considered safe

cards.front: Apert syndrome - define and list key features
cards.back: Autosomal dominant disorder caused by FGFR2 mutation; characterized by: 1. Craniosynostosis (fused skull sutures); 2. Symmetric syndactyly of hands and feet; 3. Distinctive midfacial hypoplasia; 4. High-arched palate; 5. Hearing loss; 6. Cardiac defects (20%); 7. Normal or mildly reduced intelligence

cards.front: Cat Eye Syndrome (Ocular Coloboma Syndrome) - etiology and key features
cards.back: Result of chromosomal duplication (usually partial trisomy 22); characterized by: 1. Ocular colobomas (iris cleft); 2. Preauricular pits/tags; 3. Conductive hearing loss; 4. Cardiac defects; 5. Cleft palate; 6. Anal atresia; 7. Renal anomalies; 8. Usually normal intelligence

cards.front: Prader-Willi syndrome vs Angelman syndrome - compare (chromosome, inheritance, features)
cards.back: Both involve chromosome 15 deletion (same region): PRADER-WILLI: paternal deletion/maternal imprinting inactivation → hypotonia, failure to thrive, short stature, hypogonadism, intellectual disability, hyperphagia (food-seeking), obesity. ANGELMAN: maternal deletion/paternal imprinting inactivation → intellectual disability, movement disorder (ataxia, tremor), happy demeanor, seizures, inappropriate laughter, no hyperphagia

cards.front: Cystic fibrosis (AR) - prevalence and key inheritance/diagnostic features
cards.back: Prevalence: 1 in 2500-3500 live births (varies by ethnicity; higher in Caucasians); Autosomal recessive (CFTR gene mutations); Sweat chloride test diagnostic (>60 mmol/L); Pancreatic insufficiency, recurrent respiratory infections, failure to thrive typical

cards.front: SMA Type 1 (Werdnig-Hoffmann disease) - inheritance, features, and prevalence
cards.back: Autosomal recessive (SMN1 gene); prevalence 1 in 10,000 births; onset before 6 months; features: profound hypotonia, frog-legged posture, paradoxical breathing, absent deep tendon reflexes, tongue fasciculations, feeding/respiratory difficulties; most severe form; median survival without intervention <2 years

cards.front: Duchenne muscular dystrophy (X-linked) - karyotype, prevalence, and inheritance
cards.back: X-linked recessive disorder (DMD gene on Xp21); prevalence 1 in 3500 males; karyotype normal (46,XY) but gene mutation present; mothers are carriers; affected males show symptoms by age 3-5 years; progressive weakness; wheelchair bound by teens; cardiac involvement common

cards.front: Hemophilia A (Factor VIII deficiency) - inheritance pattern
cards.back: X-linked recessive inheritance; males affected (46,XY with hemophilia A allele); females are carriers (heterozygous usually unaffected); ~10% carrier females show mild symptoms (Turner syndrome, extreme lyonization, or homozygous rare); 80% inherited from carrier mothers; 20% de novo mutations

cards.front: Hemophilia B (Factor IX deficiency) - inheritance pattern and prevalence vs Hemophilia A
cards.back: X-linked recessive inheritance (identical to Hemophilia A); prevalence 1 in 25,000-30,000 males (less common than Hemophilia A which is ~1 in 5000); males affected; females carriers; bleeding manifestations identical to Hemophilia A

cards.front: Define: holoprosencephaly - classification
cards.back: Failure of embryonic forebrain to divide into two hemispheres; three forms: 1. ALOBAR (most severe): single ventricle, complete fusion; 2. SEMILOBAR (intermediate): partial division; 3. LOBAR (least severe): nearly complete separation with abnormal olfactory bulbs/tracts; associated with severe facial midline defects (cyclopia to hypotelorism)

cards.front: Define: cryptorchidism (dysmorphology/clinical)
cards.back: Failure of testes to descend into scrotum; can be unilateral or bilateral; risk factors include prematurity, low birth weight, and genetic syndromes (Trisomy 13, Klinefelter); increased cancer risk if not corrected; usually corrects by 1 year; surgical intervention after 6 months if not descended

cards.front: Define: meningomyelocele (spina bifida type)
cards.back: Spinal cord and meninges protrude through unfused vertebral arches; most common and most severe form of spina bifida; results in neurologic deficit (paraplegia, bowel/bladder dysfunction); requires emergency surgical closure

cards.front: Define: meningocoele (spina bifida type)
cards.back: Meninges alone protrude through spinal defect; spinal cord remains in normal position; results in minimal or no neurologic deficit; generally better prognosis than meningomyelocele

cards.front: Define: spina bifida occulta (spina bifida type)
cards.back: Mildest form of spina bifida; vertebral arches fail to fuse but spinal cord and meninges remain within spinal canal; often asymptomatic; discovered incidentally on imaging; may have skin dimple or tuft of hair over site

cards.front: Encephalocoele - definition and clinical significance
cards.back: Protrusion of meninges ± brain tissue through skull defect (usually midline between eyes or occiput); results from failure of neural tube closure in 4th week gestation; associated with hydrocephalus, seizures, developmental delay; requires neurosurgical repair

cards.front: Anencephaly - definition and prognosis
cards.back: Complete absence of cerebral hemispheres and scalp due to failure of neural tube closure; incompatible with life; diagnosed prenatally by elevated AFP and ultrasound; results in stillbirth or early neonatal death; detected on US as absence of skull vault

cards.front: Define: amniotic banding syndrome (congenital constraint)
cards.back: Congenital anomalies from mechanical constraint; fibrous bands entangle fetal parts causing constriction rings, amputation of digits/limbs, cleft lip/palate, club foot; non-genetic; recurrence risk low (<1%)

cards.front: Ventricular septal defect (VSD) - most common congenital heart defect; timing of symptoms
cards.back: Most common CHD (~25-30% of all CHD); small VSDs often asymptomatic and close spontaneously; large VSDs present with signs of heart failure (feeding difficulty, poor growth, tachypnea) by 2-8 weeks of life as pulmonary vascular resistance drops; cyanosis minimal unless Eisenmenger complex develops

cards.front: Atrial septal defect (ASD) - timing of clinical presentation
cards.back: Often asymptomatic in childhood; diagnosis often delayed until adolescence/adulthood when symptoms appear (dyspnea, fatigue from left-to-right shunt); risk of arrhythmias and right heart failure in adulthood; surgical repair indicated for large defects (Qp:Qs >1.5:1)

cards.front: Patent ductus arteriosus (PDA) - risk factors and timing of symptoms
cards.back: Ductus arteriosus fails to close after birth; risk increased by prematurity, maternal rubella, hypoxia; presents within first week of life with wide pulse pressure, bounding pulses, continuous machinery murmur; symptoms include feeding intolerance, poor growth, respiratory distress; more common in premature infants

cards.front: Tetralogy of Fallot (TOF) - four components
cards.back: 1. Ventricular septal defect (VSD); 2. Right ventricular hypertrophy; 3. Right ventricular outflow tract obstruction (pulmonary stenosis); 4. Overriding aorta (receives blood from RV); presents with cyanosis, 'tet spells' (acute hypercyanotic episodes with hyperpnea), clubbing of digits

cards.front: Transposition of the great arteries (TGA) - definition and presentation
cards.back: Aorta arises from right ventricle; pulmonary artery from left ventricle (reverse of normal); incompatible with life unless PDA/ASD/VSD present for mixing; presents with severe cyanosis within first hours/days of life; blue baby syndrome; requires emergency balloon atrial septostomy (Rashkind procedure) or surgical repair

cards.front: Atrioventricular canal defect (AVCD/Endocardial cushion defect) - association and anatomy
cards.back: Ostium primum ASD + inlet VSD + cleft mitral/tricuspid valves; strongly associated with Down syndrome (Trisomy 21) and other chromosomal abnormalities; presents with cyanosis, heart failure; requires surgical repair

cards.front: Coarctation of aorta - presentation and associations
cards.back: Narrowing of descending aorta (usually just distal to left subclavian artery); presents with hypertension in upper extremities, weak/delayed femoral pulses, lower blood pressure in legs; associated with Turner syndrome, Bicuspid aortic valve; diagnosed by echocardiography or CT; may present with heart failure or incidentally on BP screening

cards.front: Ebstein anomaly - definition and association
cards.back: Tricuspid valve displacement apically into right ventricle with tricuspid regurgitation and atrialization of RV; presents with cyanosis from right-to-left shunt through ASD/PFO; associated with maternal lithium exposure in first trimester; arrhythmias common

cards.front: Surgical timing for patent ductus arteriosus (PDA)
cards.back: Indomethacin or ibuprofen tried first (inhibit prostaglandins, promote ductal closure); if medical management fails or contraindicated, surgical ligation indicated; typically performed at 3-6 weeks of age if symptomatic; earlier closure in symptomatic infants

cards.front: Surgical timing for ventricular septal defect (VSD)
cards.back: Small VSDs: follow expectantly (many close spontaneously by age 2-5 years); Large VSDs with heart failure: surgical closure indicated, typically at 3-6 months of age; emergency surgery for VSD with severe heart failure, pulmonary edema

cards.front: Surgical timing for atrial septal defect (ASD)
cards.back: Elective surgical closure recommended for secundum ASD if significant left-to-right shunt (Qp:Qs >1.5:1); timing: 3-4 years old (allows normal growth); earlier if significant symptoms; device closure alternative for some ASDs (catheter-based)

cards.front: Surgical timing for Tetralogy of Fallot (TOF)
cards.back: Early surgical repair recommended in infancy (typically 3-6 months of age) to prevent complications; prostaglandin E1 to keep ductus arteriosus open pre-operatively; survival significantly improved with early surgery vs historical use of palliative procedures (Blalock-Taussig shunt)

cards.front: Surgical timing for transposition of great arteries (TGA)
cards.back: Emergency intervention required within first hours-days of life; balloon atrial septostomy (Rashkind procedure) creates ASD for mixing; definitive corrective surgery (arterial switch/Jatene operation) within first weeks of life; prostaglandin E1 to keep PDA patent pre-operatively

cards.front: Surgical timing for coarctation of aorta
cards.back: Surgical or catheter-based intervention indicated if significant gradient (>20 mmHg); timing: 3-6 months of age for symptomatic infants; can be delayed to 3-4 years in asymptomatic children with good collateral flow; resection with end-to-end anastomosis or graft placement standard surgical approach

cards.front: Surgical timing for atrioventricular canal defect (AVCD)
cards.back: Surgical repair indicated for all complete AVCDs; timing: 3-6 months of age (before irreversible pulmonary vascular disease develops); partial AVCD may be managed longer if asymptomatic; repair involves septation of atria/ventricles and valve reconstruction

cards.front: Surgical timing for spina bifida (meningomyelocele)
cards.back: Emergency surgical closure indicated within first 24-48 hours of birth to prevent infection/further neurologic deterioration; closure performed to prevent CSF leakage and prevent meningitis; VP shunt often required simultaneously if associated hydrocephalus present

cards.front: Surgical timing for encephalocoele
cards.back: Surgical repair indicated to close skull defect; timing: early infancy (weeks to months of life) depending on size and content; earlier closure if small and symptomatic; VP shunt may be required if hydrocephalus present

cards.front: Define: hemizygous (genetics)
cards.back: Condition in males with single X chromosome who carry one allele of an X-linked gene; males are hemizygous for all X-linked genes; mutant allele on X chromosome is always expressed (no second allele to mask the effect)

cards.front: Define: consanguinity (genetics)
cards.back: Marriage or reproduction between blood relatives; increases homozygosity and risk of autosomal recessive disorders; parents share more DNA than unrelated individuals

cards.front: Define: advanced maternal age (AMA) - definition and risk
cards.back: Maternal age ≥35 years at delivery; increases risk of chromosomal abnormalities (Down syndrome, Trisomy 18, Trisomy 13); risk increases with increasing maternal age (e.g., 1/250 at age 35; 1/30 at age 45)

cards.front: Define: advanced paternal age (APA) - definition and risk
cards.back: Paternal age ≥40-50 years at conception; increases risk of de novo mutations (e.g., autosomal dominant conditions like achondroplasia, Waardenburg); associated with 1/3 of Waardenburg syndrome cases

cards.front: Define: de novo mutation
cards.back: New genetic mutation arising spontaneously in a gamete or early zygote; not inherited from either parent; first manifestation of genetic disorder in a family; risk of recurrence to siblings low but risk to offspring depends on whether mutation is germline or somatic

cards.front: Define: anticipation (genetics)
cards.back: Phenomenon where genetic disorder shows progressively earlier age of onset and/or increased severity in successive generations; seen in trinucleotide repeat disorders like myotonic dystrophy and Huntington disease

cards.front: Define: imprinting (genetics) - example with Prader-Willi
cards.back: Selective silencing of one parental allele by DNA methylation; in Prader-Willi syndrome, paternal chromosome 15 inherited normally but maternal chromosome 15 is imprinted (silenced); if paternal chromosome 15 deleted, Prader-Willi results; if maternal chromosome deleted, Angelman syndrome results

cards.front: Recurrence risk: 2 affected AR parents × 1 affected child
cards.back: 100% - all offspring will be affected (homozygous for recessive allele); each parent contributes recessive allele with 100% certainty

cards.front: Recurrence risk: 1 affected + 1 carrier (AR condition)
cards.back: 50% affected offspring, 50% carrier offspring; affected parent is homozygous (contributes recessive allele with 100% certainty); carrier parent is heterozygous (contributes recessive or normal allele 50% each)

cards.front: Recurrence risk: X-linked recessive condition, affected male × normal female
cards.back: 0% affected sons; 100% carrier daughters; affected male (XaY) contributes Y to sons (normal) and Xa to daughters (carriers)

cards.front: Recurrence risk: X-linked recessive condition, carrier female × normal male
cards.back: 50% affected sons; 50% normal sons; 50% carrier daughters; 50% normal daughters; carrier mother (XAXa) has 50% chance of passing Xa (disease allele)

cards.front: Recurrence risk: X-linked recessive condition, affected female × affected male
cards.back: 100% affected sons; 100% affected daughters; rare scenario; both parents contribute disease allele to all offspring

cards.front: Recurrence risk: multifactorial inheritance (e.g., NTD, cleft palate)
cards.back: 3-5% for first-degree relatives (siblings, offspring) of affected individual; increases if multiple family members affected; higher if parent is affected female (higher recurrence than affected male for NTD)

cards.front: Neural tube defect screening - prenatal detection methods
cards.back: 1. Maternal serum: alpha-fetoprotein (AFP) elevated in NTD; abnormal at 15-20 weeks; 2. Ultrasound: direct visualization of defect at 18-23 weeks; 3. Amniocentesis: if AFP elevated but US normal → check amniotic fluid AFP and acetylcholinesterase (confirmatory)

cards.front: Define: acetylcholinesterase (in context of NTD screening)
cards.back: Enzyme present in CSF and amniotic fluid when there is exposure of spinal cord/brain (as in NTD); acetylcholinesterase in amniotic fluid is specific marker for NTD when combined with elevated AFP; used to confirm diagnosis in amniocentesis

cards.front: What is the most widely used definition of neonatal hypoglycemia?
cards.back: Whole blood glucose concentration of <2.6 mmol/ℓ

cards.front: What defines severe neonatal hypoglycemia?
cards.back: Whole blood glucose ≤1.7 mmol/ℓ OR any symptomatic baby with low blood glucose level

cards.front: How much lower is whole blood glucose compared to serum glucose?
cards.back: Usually 15% (approximately 0.5 mmol/ℓ) lower than serum glucose

cards.front: What immediate action should be taken for abnormal whole blood glucose measurements?
cards.back: Act immediately and do not wait for laboratory sample results, even if persistent hypoglycemia needs to be confirmed later

cards.front: What instruments measure whole blood glucose in neonates?
cards.back: Blood glucometers with reagent strips and blood gas analyzers

cards.front: List the 6 main goals of managing neonatal hypoglycemia.
cards.back:
1. Prevent and correct symptomatic hypoglycemia in at-risk babies with feeds/fluids within first hour depending on GA
2. Avoid hypothermia
3. Avoid unnecessary treatment of transitional low blood glucose
4. Identify infants with underlying hypoglycemic disorders
5. Prevent long-term neurologic sequelae
6. Use stepwise treatment approach

cards.front: What is the glucose requirement for term babies?
cards.back: 6–8 mg/kg/min

cards.front: What is the glucose requirement for preterm babies?
cards.back: 4–6 mg/kg/min

cards.front: List all risk factors requiring blood glucose monitoring in the first 24 hours of life.
cards.back:
1. Prematurity
2. Post-maturity
3. Small or large for gestational age
4. Intrauterine growth restriction
5. Infants of diabetic mothers
6. Perinatal stress (intrapartum hypoxia, meconium aspiration, haemolytic disease, hypothermia, fluid restriction, polycythaemia)
7. Family history of genetic hypoglycemia cause
8. Syndromes (Beckwith-Wiedemann) or midline brain abnormalities causing hypopituitarism

cards.front: When should blood glucose monitoring be performed in at-risk babies?
cards.back: After first feed, then before subsequent feeds or every 6–8 hours if not being fed

cards.front: Which healthy newborns do NOT require blood glucose monitoring?
cards.back: Healthy term infants without risk factors who are breast feeding or formula feeding normally

cards.front: List all clinical features of neonatal hypoglycemia.
cards.back:
1. Jitteriness
2. Sweating
3. Tachypnoea
4. Tachycardia
5. Pallor
6. Cyanosis
7. Poor suck/feeding
8. Weak or high-pitched cry/irritability
9. Decreased level of consciousness
10. Seizures
11. Hypotonia
12. Apnoeas
Note: Babies are frequently asymptomatic; signs are non-specific

cards.front: What occurs after birth regarding blood glucose levels?
cards.back: There is a transient physiological drop in blood glucose levels

cards.front: Describe the first-line management for asymptomatic well babies with blood glucose 1.8–2.5 mmol/ℓ.
cards.back:
1. Give milk feed immediately or start IV fluids if contraindicated
2. Keep baby warm, ensure normothermia
3. Re-check blood glucose in 30 minutes

cards.front: What is the next step if re-checked glucose is ≥2.6 mmol/ℓ in an asymptomatic baby?
cards.back: Continue monitoring hourly for 3 hours, then 3-hourly for 24 hours

cards.front: If glucose still <2.6 mmol/ℓ but increasing in asymptomatic baby, what is the next action?
cards.back: Re-check blood glucose in 30 minutes

cards.front: If glucose is not increasing but not decreasing in asymptomatic baby, what adjustments are made?
cards.back: Increase frequency of feeds to 2-hourly and increase daily volume of feeds by 20–30 ml/kg/day. Re-check blood glucose in 30 minutes

cards.front: What triggers transition to severe hypoglycemia management in asymptomatic babies?
cards.back: Glucose decreasing OR glucose still <2.6 mmol/ℓ despite interventions

cards.front: What is the first intervention for symptomatic or severe hypoglycemia (≤1.7 mmol/ℓ)?
cards.back: Give milk by nasogastric tube if not fed within previous 2 hours, whilst preparing to insert an intravenous line

cards.front: What is the IV dextrose bolus dose for severe neonatal hypoglycemia?
cards.back: 2–5 ml/kg of 10% dextrose water IV as a bolus

cards.front: What emergency medication is used if IV access cannot be established for >30 minutes in severe hypoglycemia?
cards.back: Glucagon 0.2 mg/kg/dose IM, can repeat after 20 min, max 1 mg. Discuss with referral centre

cards.front: What alternative is used if peripheral IV access cannot be established in severe hypoglycemia?
cards.back: Insert an emergency umbilical venous catheter (UVC)

cards.front: What medication is used for hypoglycemia not responding to maximum IV glucose (awaiting critical sample)?
cards.back: Hydrocortisone 1–2 mg/kg/dose IV or PO 6-hourly

cards.front: What is the starting IV dextrose infusion rate once glucose >2.5 mmol/ℓ?
cards.back: 10% dextrose at 60–120 ml/kg/24 h depending on gestational age and day of life; beware of fluid volumes >120 ml/kg/day on day 1

cards.front: How should dextrose concentration be escalated if hypoglycemia persists?
cards.back: Increase sequentially to 12.5%, 15%, and 20%

cards.front: Which dextrose concentrations require a central line for infusion?
cards.back: >12.5% dextrose requires a central line; emergency UVC may be used for short-term control during transfer

cards.front: Why should 50% glucose never be given IV or orally in neonates?
cards.back: Due to its hypertonicity

cards.front: What is the blood glucose monitoring frequency after intervention until normal?
cards.back: Every 30 minutes after the intervention until normal, then 2-hourly and/or before feeds until normal (≥2.6 mmol/ℓ) for two consecutive readings

cards.front: When should milk feeds be added once glucose is normal?
cards.back: Once glucose is normal and there are no clinical contraindications; gradually build up feeds while decreasing IV fluids provided BG remains ≥2.6 mmol/ℓ

cards.front: When should dextrose-containing IV fluids be stopped?
cards.back: Do not stop until feeds are well established

cards.front: What is the formula for calculating dextrose delivery rate?
cards.back: Dextrose Delivery Rate (mg/kg/min) = (ml/kg/24 h) × (% dextrose) × 0.007

cards.front: Define persistent neonatal hypoglycemia.
cards.back: Persistently low blood glucose concentrations beyond the first 48 hours of life OR requirement of parenteral glucose infusions to treat hypoglycemia beyond 48 hours of life

cards.front: What warrants investigation in persistent neonatal hypoglycemia?
cards.back: Need to determine the underlying cause and discuss all babies with referral centre for advice and admission if unable to be managed at place of birth or presentation

cards.front: List investigations for persistent neonatal hypoglycemia.
cards.back:
1. Consider sepsis: blood culture and start broad spectrum antibiotics
2. Critical sample: blood at time of hypoglycemia for serum glucose, insulin, and cortisol
3. Always confirm hypoglycemia with laboratory sample
4. Further investigation discussed with paediatrician/neonatologist or paediatric endocrinologist

cards.front: What is the commonest cause of persistent neonatal hypoglycemia?
cards.back: Hyperinsulinism (elevated insulin despite hypoglycemia)

cards.front: In which conditions does hyperinsulinism occur?
cards.back: Infants of diabetic mothers (IDM), intrauterine growth restriction (IUGR), and intrapartum stress

cards.front: What is the typical course of hyperinsulinism in neonates?
cards.back: Usually transient, resolves in first few weeks of life; persistent hyperinsulinism requires specialist referral

cards.front: What medication is used for low cortisol in persistent hypoglycemia?
cards.back: Hydrocortisone 1–2 mg/kg/dose IV or PO 6-hourly

cards.front: What medications are used for hyperinsulinism in persistent hypoglycemia?
cards.back:
1. Diazoxide 2–6 mg/kg/day IV/PO 8-hourly
2. Hydrochlorothiazide 1–3 mg/kg/dose PO 6–12 hourly
Requires specialist guidance

cards.front: What are the discharge criteria for babies with hypoglycemia?
cards.back: Two normal pre-prandial blood glucose readings consecutively on full 8-hourly feeds

cards.front: What follow-up is required for all babies with severe hypoglycemia?
cards.back: Neurodevelopmental follow-up at 20 weeks corrected gestational age; consider brain MRI

cards.front: What defines neonatal hyperglycemia?
cards.back: Whole blood glucose >7.0 mmol/ℓ or plasma >8.3 mmol/ℓ

cards.front: Is hyperglycemia in extreme preterms always pathological?
cards.back: No, frequent in extreme preterms but not always requires intervention

cards.front: What physiological consequence occurs when plasma glucose >11 mmol/ℓ?
cards.back: Osmotic diuresis and polyuria

cards.front: Why is glycosuria not a good marker for hyperglycemia in neonates?
cards.back: Because it correlates with plasma glucose >11 mmol/ℓ, not lower hyperglycemia thresholds

cards.front: What is the monitoring frequency for hyperglycemia in the first 24–48 hours?
cards.back: Every 6–8 hours in first 24–48 hours in babies on IV glucose, ELBW, septic, intubated, with IVH, or on inotropes/steroids/insulin

cards.front: List all causes of neonatal hyperglycemia.
cards.back:
1. IV glucose
2. ELBW/VLBW
3. Stress response
4. Drugs (steroids, inotropes)
5. Neonatal diabetes mellitus (rare)

cards.front: At what glucose threshold should treatment of hyperglycemia be considered?
cards.back: Stepwise approach in babies with hyperglycemia >10 mmol/ℓ

cards.front: What is the first-line approach to reducing glucose in hyperglycemic neonates?
cards.back: Decrease parenteral glucose to 4–6 mg/kg/min by reducing dextrose concentration from 10% to 5% (not below 5%) and/or reducing volume

cards.front: When should insulin therapy be considered for hyperglycemia?
cards.back: If persistent ≥12 mmol/ℓ despite reduction in dextrose, with specialist guidance; confirm with lab/blood gas before starting

cards.front: Why should insulin never be bolused in neonates?
cards.back: Bolus insulin is contraindicated in neonatal management

cards.front: What investigations are needed for persistent hyperglycemia?
cards.back:
1. Septic screen and antibiotics
2. Cranial ultrasound for intraventricular hemorrhage (IVH)
3. Endocrinology if neonatal diabetes is considered

cards.front: Why must plastic tubing be primed with insulin before insulin infusion?
cards.back: Because insulin binds to tubing

cards.front: What is the insulin dose range for neonatal hyperglycemia?
cards.back: 0.02–0.5 units/kg/hour

cards.front: Describe the insulin infusion preparation for neonatal use.
cards.back: Add 2 ml (100 units) Actrapid to 10 ml normal saline. Then add 1 ml/kg (=10 units/kg) to 0.9% NS to final volume 50 ml. Then 0.1 ml/hour = 0.02 units/kg/hour

cards.front: What is the monitoring frequency during neonatal insulin infusion?
cards.back: Hourly blood glucose

cards.front: At what blood glucose level should insulin infusion be stopped?
cards.back: When blood glucose <8 mmol/ℓ

cards.front: What is the insulin infusion rate when blood glucose >25 mmol/ℓ?
cards.back: 1 ml/hour

cards.front: What is the insulin infusion rate when blood glucose 20–25 mmol/ℓ?
cards.back: 0.5 ml/hour

cards.front: What is the insulin infusion rate when blood glucose 18–19 mmol/ℓ?
cards.back: 0.4 ml/hour

cards.front: What is the insulin infusion rate when blood glucose 15–17 mmol/ℓ?
cards.back: 0.3 ml/hour

cards.front: What is the insulin infusion rate when blood glucose 12–14 mmol/ℓ?
cards.back: 0.2 ml/hour

cards.front: What is the insulin infusion rate when blood glucose 8–11 mmol/ℓ?
cards.back: 0.1 ml/hour

cards.front: What is the insulin infusion rate when blood glucose <8 mmol/ℓ?
cards.back: Stop insulin infusion

cards.front: What is the specific blood glucose range for the asymptomatic well baby algorithm?
Why is it important to differentiate normal transitional glucose response from pathological hypoglycemia?
cards.back: To avoid over-treatment of normal physiological drop in blood glucose after birth

cards.front: What statement is made about the exact blood glucose level and duration that causes brain injury in neonates?
cards.back: Exact blood glucose levels and/or duration causing brain injury has not been established

cards.front: How often should feeds be increased if glucose is not increasing but not decreasing in asymptomatic babies?
cards.back: 2-hourly feed frequency with daily volume increased by 20–30 ml/kg/day

cards.front: What is the time limit for recent feeding before nasogastric tube milk can be given in severe hypoglycemia?
cards.back: If not been fed within the previous 2 hours

cards.front: What is the time threshold for considering glucagon if IV access cannot be established in severe hypoglycemia?
cards.back: Delay beyond 30 minutes

cards.front: What is the repeat interval for glucagon IM in severe hypoglycemia?
cards.back: Can repeat after 20 minutes, maximum 1 mg total

cards.front: What is the specific monitoring schedule after glucose reaches ≥2.6 mmol/ℓ in asymptomatic babies?
cards.back: Hourly for 3 hours, then 3-hourly for 24 hours

cards.front: What is the minimum dextrose concentration that should not be gone below when reducing parenteral glucose?
cards.back: 5% dextrose (do not reduce below 5%)

cards.front: What is the target parenteral glucose delivery rate when reducing from higher levels in hyperglycemia?
cards.back: 4–6 mg/kg/min

cards.front: What is the threshold for osmotic diuresis and polyuria in hyperglycemic neonates?
cards.back: Plasma glucose >11 mmol/ℓ

cards.front: What persistent hyperglycemia level warrants specialist insulin consideration after dextrose reduction?
cards.back: Persistent ≥12 mmol/ℓ despite reduction in dextrose concentration and/or volume

cards.front: What must be confirmed before starting insulin in neonatal hyperglycemia?
cards.back: Confirm hyperglycemia with laboratory blood gas or lab measurement before starting insulin

cards.front: What criterion determines when neonatal diabetes should be referred for endocrinology?
cards.back: When neonatal diabetes mellitus is considered as the underlying cause of persistent hyperglycemia

cards.front: What specific corrective action must always be taken for symptomatic hypoglycemia?
cards.back: Hypoglycemia should always be corrected in symptomatic babies

cards.front: What is the clinical significance of persistent and prolonged hypoglycemia?
cards.back: Persistent and prolonged hypoglycemia is pathological and may cause brain injury

cards.front: What is the primary concern with fluid volumes on day 1 of IV dextrose therapy?
cards.back: Beware of fluid volumes >120 ml/kg/day on day 1

cards.front: When should the cause of persistent hypoglycemia be identified?
cards.back: After initial stabilization when hypoglycemia extends beyond 48 hours or requires parenteral glucose beyond 48 hours

cards.front: What is the diagnostic criterion for sepsis evaluation in persistent hypoglycemia?
cards.back: Blood culture and start broad spectrum antibiotics to consider sepsis

cards.front: What is the timing for obtaining critical samples in persistent hypoglycemia?
cards.back: Blood obtained at the time the baby is hypoglycemic for serum glucose, insulin, and cortisol

cards.front: When is a referral centre discussion mandatory in persistent hypoglycemia?
cards.back: Discuss all babies with referral centre for advice and admission if unable to be managed at place of birth or presentation

cards.front: What is the timeline for hyperinsulinism resolution in transient cases?
cards.back: Usually transient, resolves in first few weeks of life

cards.front: What diagnostic feature characterizes hyperinsulinism?
cards.back: Elevated insulin despite hypoglycemia

cards.front: What is the route and frequency for diazoxide in hyperinsulinism?
cards.back: Diazoxide 2–6 mg/kg/day IV or PO 8-hourly, requires specialist guidance

cards.front: What is the route and frequency for hydrochlorothiazide in hyperinsulinism?
cards.back: Hydrochlorothiazide 1–3 mg/kg/dose PO 6–12 hourly, requires specialist guidance

cards.front: What is the requirement for discharge readiness in neonatal hypoglycemia?
cards.back: Two normal pre-prandial blood glucose readings consecutively on full 8-hourly feeds

cards.front: What neurological investigation is recommended for severe hypoglycemia follow-up?
cards.back: Brain MRI should be considered alongside neurodevelopmental follow-up at 20 weeks corrected gestational age

cards.front: What is the definition of extreme prematurity in the context of hyperglycemia discussion?
cards.back: ELBW (extremely low birth weight) and VLBW (very low birth weight) infants who commonly develop hyperglycemia without always requiring intervention

cards.front: List clinical scenarios requiring hyperglycemia monitoring at 6–8 hourly intervals.
cards.back: Babies on IV glucose, ELBW infants, septic babies, intubated infants, those with intraventricular hemorrhage (IVH), or those on inotropes, steroids, or insulin

cards.front: What is the stepwise threshold for treating neonatal hyperglycemia?
cards.back: Stepwise approach in babies with hyperglycemia >10 mmol/ℓ

cards.front: What are the four key questions to ask during first assessment of a breastfed baby?
cards.back:
1. How is breastfeeding going?
2. Number of feeds in 24 hours?
3. Other supplemental feeds?
4. Has baby fed in last hour?

cards.front: What is the normal weight loss range after birth in a newborn?
cards.back: Less than 10% weight loss after birth is normal

cards.front: What is the minimum expected weight gain per day in a newborn?
cards.back: At least 10 g/kg/day

cards.front: Name the four components of LOOK, LISTEN, FEEL assessment for feeding problems
cards.back:
1. Attachment
2. Positioning
3. Sucking
4. Problems (e.g. blocked nose, oral thrush, mouth ulcers)

cards.front: What are the two signs of correct infant latch-on position?
cards.back:
1. Mouth covers areola
2. Lips are flanged out

cards.front: Name the four correct positions for breastfeeding
cards.back:
1. Cradle hold
2. Cross-cradle hold
3. Football hold
4. Side-lying position

cards.front: List the three danger signs indicating a baby is NOT ABLE TO FEED
cards.back:
1. Not feeding
2. Not attaching
3. Not sucking

cards.front: What is the immediate management for a baby with danger signs for feeding in the context of infection?
cards.back: Investigate and treat for INFECTION

cards.front: For a baby <3 days old with danger signs for feeding and no risk factors for infection, what condition should be treated?
cards.back: ENCEPHALOPATHY

cards.front: What does the DR RED mnemonic stand for?
cards.back:
O - Observations
O - Oxygen
S - Sugar
H - HypoThermia
I - Infection

cards.front: List the four management steps for a baby with a feeding problem due to incorrect latch-on
cards.back:
1. Position (correct)
2. Assess for breast problems
3. Feed on demand or 8 times a day
4. Counsel on exclusive breastfeeding

cards.front: What are the three management steps for poor weight gain/poor growth in a breastfed baby?
cards.back:
1. Encourage exclusive breastfeeding on demand
2. Exclude sepsis (note: check for UTI)
3. Keep warm

cards.front: What is the management for a breastfed baby with good weight gain?
cards.back: Encourage mom to breastfeed on demand

cards.front: When is replacement feeding advised in HIV-positive mothers?
cards.back: Only if the mother is HIV-positive AND requests replacement feeds

cards.front: What does AFASS stand for in the context of replacement feeding?
cards.back:
A - Accessible
F - Feasible
A - Affordable
S - Safe
S - Sustainable

cards.front: What is the transmission rate of HIV through breastfeeding?
cards.back: 5–15% depending on multiple factors

cards.front: List the six factors affecting HIV transmission rate in breastfeeding
cards.back:
1. Mother's viral load
2. Recent new HIV infection
3. Length of breastfeeding
4. Condition of breasts
5. Condition of baby's mouth
6. Exclusive or mixed feeding

cards.front: When should HAART be initiated in an HIV-positive breastfeeding mother?
cards.back: If CD4 is <350 (reduces transmission rate to 1–2%)

cards.front: What ARV should be given to a baby whose HIV-positive mother is breastfeeding?
cards.back: ARV to baby for the entire duration of breastfeeding

cards.front: How long should an HIV-positive mother breastfeed exclusively to reduce transmission?
cards.back: Exclusively for 6 months

cards.front: What is the recommended milk handling method for HIV-positive mothers to reduce transmission?
cards.back: Expressing and heat treating all breast milk

cards.front: What is the main infectious risk of replacement milk compared to breast milk?
cards.back: No risk of HIV transmission

cards.front: What gastrointestinal complications occur with replacement milk feeding?
cards.back:
1. Increased frequency and severity of diarrhoea and respiratory infections
2. Increased risk of persistent diarrhoea
3. Increased risk of malnutrition

cards.front: Name the six key questions to ask when assessing feeding in babies receiving replacement milk
cards.back:
1. How is feeding going?
2. How many feeds given during day and night?
3. How are you preparing milk?
4. Are you giving any breast milk?
5. How is the milk given?
6. How are the bottles/utensils washed and cleaned?

cards.front: What two main things should be assessed in LOOK, LISTEN, FEEL for replacement-fed babies?
cards.back:
1. Assess for possible feeding problem (thrush/mouth ulcers, blocked nose)
2. Assess for weight growth

cards.front: What is the danger sign for a replacement-fed baby that requires immediate action?
cards.back: NOT ABLE TO SUCK/FEED

cards.front: For a replacement-fed baby <3 days old who cannot feed and has no risk of infection, what should be treated?
cards.back: Treat as asphyxia

cards.front: List five causes of feeding problems in replacement-fed babies
cards.back:
1. Milk incorrectly or unhygienically prepared
2. Inappropriate replacement milk
3. Insufficient amounts of milk
4. Mixing breast and replacement milk
5. Thrush

cards.front: List four management steps for poor weight gain in a replacement-fed baby
cards.back:
1. Check volumes
2. Feed correctly prepared
3. Check for infection
4. KMC (Kangaroo Mother Care)

cards.front: What is the management for good weight gain in a replacement-fed baby?
cards.back: Encourage mom to continue feeding

cards.front: List three components of weight assessment in LBW (low birth weight) babies
cards.back:
1. Weigh daily and record weight
2. Plot daily weight on graph
3. Calculate daily/weekly weight gain

cards.front: What is the expected weight loss in the first week of life?
cards.back: Up to 10% of birth weight

cards.front: By when should a newborn regain its birth weight after initial loss?
cards.back: In 7–14 days (i.e. back to birth weight)

cards.front: What is the minimum expected weight gain per day in a preterm baby after initial loss?
cards.back: 10 g/kg/day

cards.front: What is the minimum expected weight gain per day in a term baby after initial loss?
cards.back: 20 g/kg/day

cards.front: When is weight loss considered inadequate in a newborn?
cards.back: When weight loss exceeds 10% of birth weight

cards.front: What is the management for adequate weight loss (<10%) in a newborn?
cards.back: Continue feeding and start breastfeeding when baby is able to suckle

cards.front: List the five 'i's to consider when investigating inadequate weight gain
cards.back:
1. Illness
2. Insufficient feeds
3. Inadequate temperature control
4. Incorrect feeding method
5. Idiot (examiner's note: likely 'education deficit')

cards.front: Name the three main counselling skills
cards.back:
1. Communication skills
2. Listening and learning skills
3. Confidence building skills

cards.front: What is counselling NOT about?
cards.back: Counselling is NOT about giving instructions. The critical issue is to reach an appropriate decision based on understanding, together with the mother or family.

cards.front: List four signs requiring immediate return to PHC clinic
cards.back:
1. Discharging eyes
2. Umbilical sepsis
3. Septic skin lesions
4. Jaundice

cards.front: List five signs requiring immediate return to hospital
cards.back:
1. Not feeding/suckling well
2. Convulsions
3. Rapid breathing
4. Diarrhoea
5. Bleeding

cards.front: What are the follow-up timepoints for all babies?
cards.back: 3 days, 6 weeks, and normal routine

cards.front: What are the follow-up timepoints for HIV-exposed babies?
cards.back: 6 weeks, then monthly for first year

cards.front: What are the follow-up timepoints for babies <2 kg?
cards.back: 3 days, weekly until 2.5 kg, then 6 weeks

cards.front: What are the follow-up timepoints for high-risk babies?
cards.back: 3 days, weekly until 2.5 kg, 6 weeks, 4 months, 9 months

cards.front: List the four signs of good attachment at the breast
cards.back:
1. Mouth covers areola (not just nipple)
2. Lips are flanged/everted outward
3. Chin touching the breast
4. Cheeks are full and rounded (not drawn in)

cards.front: List the signs of effective suckling/milk transfer
cards.back:
1. Audible swallows (1-2 per suck)
2. Milk visible at corners of mouth
3. Slow, deep sucks with jaw movement
4. Regular breathing pattern (not gasping)
5. Baby relaxed at end of feed

cards.front: What are the recommended milk volumes for neonates from day 1 to day 5?
cards.back:
Day 1: 10 ml per feed
Day 2: 20 ml per feed
Day 3: 30 ml per feed
Day 4: 40 ml per feed
Day 5+: 60-80 ml per feed (or 150-200 ml/kg/day total)

cards.front: How should growth charts be interpreted in neonates?
cards.back:
Plot daily or weekly weight. Look for:
1. Weight loss >10% = inadequate intake
2. Weight loss <10% = normal, expected recovery in 7-14 days
3. After regaining birth weight: minimum gain 10 g/kg/day (preterm) or 20 g/kg/day (term)
4. Crossing percentiles downward = faltering growth, investigate cause

cards.front: What does the APAC counselling framework stand for?
cards.back:
A - ASK (listen to mother's concerns, understand her perspective)
P - PRAISE (acknowledge her efforts and strengths)
A - ADVISE (provide information and recommendations)
C - CHECK (confirm understanding and plan together)

cards.front: How should breast engorgement be managed?
cards.back:
1. Frequent feeding (8+ times daily)
2. Warm compresses before feed to improve milk flow
3. Hand expression or gentle massage to soften areola
4. Cold compresses between feeds to reduce swelling
5. Proper positioning and attachment to ensure complete emptying
6. Analgesics if needed

cards.front: How should mastitis (breast infection) be managed?
cards.back:
1. Continue breastfeeding on affected breast (safe for baby)
2. Frequent feeds to empty breast completely
3. Warm compresses and gentle massage before feeds
4. Antibiotics (appropriate to breastfeeding: flucloxacillin, cephalexin)
5. Analgesics (paracetamol or ibuprofen)
6. Bed rest, adequate hydration
7. Ensure correct positioning and attachment to prevent recurrence

cards.front: How should inverted or flat nipples be managed during breastfeeding?
cards.back:
1. Nipple shields (temporary aid to enable direct breastfeeding)
2. Hand expression to draw out the nipple before attachment
3. Use breast pump briefly before feed to evert nipple
4. Frequent feeding to maintain milk supply
5. Positional techniques (baby may attach better in football hold)
6. Ensure good attachment despite nipple shape
7. Consider expressing and feeding expressed milk if breastfeeding impossible

cards.front: How should cracked or sore nipples be managed?
cards.back:
1. Correct attachment (main cause is poor latch)
2. Vary feeding position (different pressure points)
3. Express milk manually to soften areola before feed
4. Lanolin or expressed breast milk to nipples after feed
5. Air drying between feeds
6. Breast shells to prevent clothing friction
7. Continue breastfeeding (pain reduces as healing occurs)
8. If too painful: express and cup-feed until healed

cards.front: How should insufficient milk supply be managed?
cards.back:
1. Increase feeding frequency to 8-12 times daily
2. Ensure correct attachment and milk transfer
3. Drain breasts completely at each feed
4. Alternate breasts multiple times per feed
5. Hand expression after feeds to improve emptying
6. Assess for tongue tie in baby
7. Check for underlying health issues (maternal infection, thyroid, stress)
8. Consider galactagogues (fenugreek, blessed thistle) with support
9. Maintain maternal nutrition and hydration

cards.front: How should tongue tie in a baby be managed during breastfeeding?
cards.back:
1. Diagnose: assess if frenulum restricts tongue movement
2. Positioning: use football hold or other positions to improve latch
3. Hand expression: provide milk to baby if breastfeeding ineffective
4. Refer for frenulotomy (frenotomy) if:
   - Breastfeeding unsuccessful despite support
   - Baby unable to feed effectively
   - Mother experiencing pain/damage
5. After frenulotomy: reassess latch and continue breastfeeding
6. May see immediate improvement or need repositioning support

cards.front: Describe the correct technique for hand expression of breast milk
cards.back:
1. Wash hands thoroughly
2. Warm compress or shower to stimulate letdown
3. Cup breast in hand, fingers below, thumb above at edge of areola
4. Press breast tissue gently against chest wall
5. Roll thumb and fingers forward (compress and express simultaneously)
6. Release, then repeat in rhythmic pattern
7. Rotate position around breast to empty all quadrants
8. Express for 5-10 minutes or until flow slows
9. Collect milk in sterile cup/bottle

cards.front: What are the key positioning criteria for successful breastfeeding?
cards.back:
1. Baby's head and body in straight line (not twisted)
2. Baby's face close to breast (nose level with nipple)
3. Mother's back supported
4. Baby's whole body supported (not just head)
5. Mother and baby skin-to-skin contact if possible
6. Baby's mouth brought to breast (not breast to baby)
7. Baby's body turned toward mother (chest-to-chest)
8. Comfortable for mother (no pain, no tension)

cards.front: Define prematurity by gestational age.
cards.back: Birth occurring before 37 completed weeks (< 259 days) of gestation.
cards.source: Complications of Prematurity LBW and RDS
cards.sourceFile: Complications of Prematurity LBW and RDS.yml

cards.front: What is the gestational age range for late preterm birth?
cards.back: GA between 34 and less than 37 weeks.
cards.source: Complications of Prematurity LBW and RDS
cards.sourceFile: Complications of Prematurity LBW and RDS.yml

cards.front: What is the gestational age cutoff for very preterm (VPT) birth?
cards.back: GA less than 32 weeks.
cards.source: Complications of Prematurity LBW and RDS
cards.sourceFile: Complications of Prematurity LBW and RDS.yml

cards.front: What is the gestational age cutoff for extremely preterm (EPT) birth?
cards.back: GA at or below 28 weeks.
cards.source: Complications of Prematurity LBW and RDS
cards.sourceFile: Complications of Prematurity LBW and RDS.yml

cards.front: List socioeconomic and psychosocial risk factors for preterm birth.
cards.back:
1. No partner
2. Low socioeconomic level
3. Anxiety, stress
4. Depression, use of SSRIs
5. Life events (divorce, separation, death)
6. Low educational achievement
cards.source: Complications of Prematurity LBW and RDS
cards.sourceFile: Complications of Prematurity LBW and RDS.yml

cards.front: List occupational risk factors for preterm birth.
cards.back:
1. Upright posture
2. Use of industrial machines
3. Physical exertion
4. Mental stress related to work
5. Environmental stress related to working conditions
cards.source: Complications of Prematurity LBW and RDS
cards.sourceFile: Complications of Prematurity LBW and RDS.yml

cards.front: What is an important surgical risk factor for preterm birth?
cards.back: Abdominal surgery during pregnancy.
cards.source: Complications of Prematurity LBW and RDS
