cards.back: Hypocalcaemia

cards.front: IDM metabolic complication (5)
cards.back: Respiratory Distress Syndrome (RDS)

cards.front: IDM metabolic complication (6)
cards.back: Polycythaemia

cards.front: IDM cardiac/gastrointestinal/renal complication (1)
cards.back: Cardiomyopathy

cards.front: IDM cardiac/gastrointestinal/renal complication (2)
cards.back: Small left colon syndrome (transient obstruction)

cards.front: IDM cardiac/gastrointestinal/renal complication (3)
cards.back: Renal vein thrombosis (presenting with haematuria)

cards.front: IDM congenital malformation (1)
cards.back: Neural tube defects (NTD)

cards.front: IDM congenital malformation (2)
cards.back: Vertebral defects

cards.front: IDM congenital malformation (3)
cards.back: Sacral agenesis

cards.front: IDM congenital malformation (4)
cards.back: Cardiac defects (VSD, TGV, COA)

cards.front: Define hypocalcaemia in term infants
cards.back: Serum calcium <2 mmol/ℓ (Normal: 2.1–2.7 mmol/ℓ)

cards.front: Define hypocalcaemia in preterm infants
cards.back: Serum calcium <1.8 mmol/ℓ (Normal: 1.9–2.8 mmol/ℓ)

cards.front: What is the incidence of hypocalcaemia in neonates?
cards.back: About 1/3 of low birth weight infants (LBWI) and 50% of IDM within 3 days of life

cards.front: When does hypocalcaemia occur in the first week vs after first week?
cards.back: First week: prems calcium deficient; After 1st week: formula-fed infants with high phosphate

cards.front: Hypocalcaemia cause (1)
cards.back: Relative hypoparathyroidism (suppression of fetal parathyroid by transferred maternal parathyroid hormone; improves within days after birth)

cards.front: Hypocalcaemia cause (2)
cards.back: Preterm infants: calcium deficient

cards.front: Hypocalcaemia cause (3)
cards.back: Asphyxia, correction of acidosis, endotracheal feeding

cards.front: Hypocalcaemia clinical feature: asymptomatic
cards.back: Asymptomatic

cards.front: Hypocalcaemia clinical feature (2)
cards.back: Twitching

cards.front: Hypocalcaemia clinical feature (3)
cards.back: Jitteriness

cards.front: Hypocalcaemia clinical feature (4)
cards.back: Seizures

cards.front: Hypocalcaemia clinical feature (5)
cards.back: High pitched cry

cards.front: Hypocalcaemia clinical feature: electrocardiogram
cards.back: Electrocardiogram shows prolonged Q-T segment

cards.front: Hypocalcaemia treatment (1)
cards.back: 3 ml of 10% Calcium Gluconate per 100 ml neonatal normal saline

cards.front: Hypocalcaemia treatment (2): oral for asymptomatic
cards.back: Calcium Gluconate or Lactate 1–3 g po daily

cards.front: Hypocalcaemia treatment (3): intravenous direct
cards.back: Calcium Gluconate 10% 2 ml/kg IV diluted, given slowly

cards.front: Define hypomagnesaemia
cards.back: Serum magnesium <0.62 mmol/ℓ (Normal: 0.7–0.9 mmol/ℓ)

cards.front: High risk group for hypomagnesaemia (1)
cards.back: IDM (Infant of Diabetic Mother)

cards.front: High risk group for hypomagnesaemia (2)
cards.back: SGA (Small for Gestational Age)

cards.front: High risk group for hypomagnesaemia (3)
cards.back: Exchange transfusion

cards.front: What are the clinical signs of hypomagnesaemia?
cards.back: Similar to hypocalcaemia

cards.front: Treatment of hypomagnesaemia
cards.back: 0.1 MgSO₄ 50% IV/IM 12 hourly for 3 doses

cards.front: What is the cause of hypermagnesaemia in neonates?
cards.back: Eclamptic mothers are treated with magnesium sulfate

cards.front: Sign of hypermagnesaemia (1)
cards.back: Central nervous system depression

cards.front: Sign of hypermagnesaemia (2)
cards.back: Apnoea

cards.front: Treatment of hypermagnesaemia
cards.back: Supportive care

cards.front: Define hypernatraemia
cards.back: Serum sodium >150 mmol/ℓ

cards.front: Explain the pathophysiology of hypernatraemia
cards.back: Shift of water from brain tissue → brain cell shrinkage, pressure differences → capillary dilatation and rupture, cerebral venous thrombosis and haemorrhage

cards.front: Cause of hypernatraemia (1)
cards.back: Insufficient fluid intake

cards.front: Cause of hypernatraemia (2)
cards.back: Dehydration secondary to excessive insensible water loss (phototherapy lamps, radiant heaters)

cards.front: Sign of hypernatraemia (1)
cards.back: Lethargy

cards.front: Sign of hypernatraemia (2)
cards.back: Irritability

cards.front: Sign of hypernatraemia (3)
cards.back: Convulsions

cards.front: Treatment of hypernatraemia
cards.back: Lower serum sodium over 24–48 hours to prevent cerebral oedema

cards.front: Define hyponatraemia
cards.back: Serum sodium <125 mmol/ℓ

cards.front: Explain the pathophysiology of hyponatraemia
cards.back: Water is drawn into brain cells → central nervous system injury

cards.front: Cause of hyponatraemia (1)
cards.back: Very low birth weight (VLBW) <32 weeks gestation unable to conserve sodium (high urinary loss)

cards.front: Cause of hyponatraemia (2)
cards.back: RDS + asphyxia → SIADH → loss of sodium in urine, water intoxication, diarrhoea

cards.front: Sign of hyponatraemia (1)
cards.back: Lethargy

cards.front: Sign of hyponatraemia (2)
cards.back: Poor feeding

cards.front: Sign of hyponatraemia (3)
cards.back: Apnoea

cards.front: Sign of hyponatraemia (4)
cards.back: Shock

cards.front: Define hypokalaemia
cards.back: Serum potassium <3 mmol/ℓ

cards.front: Cause of hypokalaemia (1)
cards.back: Poor intake

cards.front: Cause of hypokalaemia (2)
cards.back: Alkalosis

cards.front: Cause of hypokalaemia (3)
cards.back: Diarrhoea

cards.front: Cause of hypokalaemia (4)
cards.back: Diuretics

cards.front: Define hyperkalaemia
cards.back: Serum potassium >7 mmol/ℓ

cards.front: Electrocardiogram sign of hyperkalaemia (1)
cards.back: Peaked T waves

cards.front: Electrocardiogram sign of hyperkalaemia (2)
cards.back: Prolonged PR interval

cards.front: Electrocardiogram sign of hyperkalaemia (3)
cards.back: Absent P waves

cards.front: Electrocardiogram sign of hyperkalaemia (4)
cards.back: Arrhythmias

cards.front: Cause of hyperkalaemia (1)
cards.back: Severe catabolic state

cards.front: Cause of hyperkalaemia (2)
cards.back: Acute renal failure

cards.front: Cause of hyperkalaemia (3)
cards.back: Hypoxia

cards.front: Cause of hyperkalaemia (4)
cards.back: Shock

cards.front: Cause of hyperkalaemia (5)
cards.back: Acidosis

cards.front: Cause of hyperkalaemia (6)
cards.back: Congenital Adrenal Hyperplasia (CAH)

cards.front: Hyperkalaemia management step 1
cards.back: Electrocardiogram monitoring

cards.front: Hyperkalaemia management step 2
cards.back: Calcium Gluconate 10% 1 ml/kg given slowly

cards.front: Hyperkalaemia management step 3
cards.back: Sodium bicarbonate (Sodabic) 2 mmol/kg (to correct acidosis)

cards.front: Hyperkalaemia management step 4
cards.back: Salbutamol 4 mcg/kg IV bolus

cards.front: Hyperkalaemia management step 5
cards.back: Glucose and Insulin

cards.front: Hyperkalaemia management step 6
cards.back: Kayexalate 1 g/kg/day po/pr

cards.front: What enzyme inhibitor blocks heme oxygenase in bilirubin synthesis?
cards.back: Metalloporphyrins inhibit heme oxygenase

cards.front: What causes increased fluid loss in open incubators?
cards.back: Osmotic diuresis

cards.front: What is the mechanism of fluid loss under phototherapy lights?
cards.back: Direct insensible water loss from skin surface exposed to light

cards.front: What is the exact dose of 10% calcium gluconate to add to neonatal feeds?
cards.back: 3 ml of 10% calcium gluconate per 100 ml neonatal normal saline

cards.front: What is the IV dose of 10% calcium gluconate for symptomatic hypocalcaemia?
cards.back: 2 ml/kg IV, diluted and given slowly

cards.front: What is the exact dose and schedule for hypomagnesaemia treatment?
cards.back: 0.1 ml of MgSO₄ 50% IV/IM, repeated 12-hourly for 3 doses

cards.front: How should hypernatraemia correction be timed to prevent cerebral complications?
cards.back: Serum sodium must be lowered over 24–48 hours to avoid cerebral oedema

cards.front: What is the treatment for hyponatraemia?
cards.back: Fluid restriction; treatment depends on severity and rate of onset

cards.front: What is the exact insulin preparation for hyperkalaemia management?
cards.back: Mix 2 iu Actrapid in 20 ml of half-saline solution

cards.front: What is the insulin dosing regimen for hyperkalaemia?
cards.back: Give 0.05 iu/kg stat, then commence continuous infusion at 0.05 iu/kg

cards.front: What is the dose of sodium bicarbonate (Sodabic) for hyperkalaemia?
cards.back: 2 mmol/kg intravenously to correct acidosis

cards.front: What is the dose of salbutamol for acute hyperkalaemia management?
cards.back: 4 mcg/kg IV bolus

cards.front: What is the exact dose and route of kayexalate for hyperkalaemia?
cards.back: 1 g/kg/day orally or per rectum (po/pr)

cards.front: How does preconceptual glycaemic control affect IDM outcomes?
cards.back: Preconceptual control is essential to reduce malformation incidence; pregnancy control reduces most complications but NOT malformations

cards.front: What feto-maternal bleeding events increase Rh sensitisation?
cards.back: Abortion, amniocentesis, external cephalic version (ECV)

cards.front: What is the role of glucuronic acid in bilirubin metabolism?
cards.back: Bilirubin is conjugated with glucuronic acid in the liver to become water-soluble

cards.front: What role does enterohepatic circulation play in neonatal jaundice?
cards.back: Unconjugated bilirubin is reabsorbed from the intestine and recirculated to the liver, prolonging jaundice

cards.front: What is the monitoring protocol for neonatal hypoglycaemia screening?
cards.back: Check glucose meter within 1 hour of birth in at-risk infants; then hourly until glucose >3 mmol/ℓ

cards.front: What is the composition and use of a 5% dextrose cocktail in hyperglycaemia?
cards.back: 5% dextrose solution used when switching from higher concentration to prevent hyperglycaemia; monitor glucosuria

cards.front: At what glucose threshold should insulin be weaned in hyperglycaemia?
cards.back: When glucose <10 mmol/ℓ: wean insulin and change to neonatal normal saline

cards.front: How does albumin administration affect bilirubin management?
cards.back: 20% albumin enhances bilirubin binding to prevent free bilirubin toxicity; dose 5 ml/kg IV

cards.front: When does hypocalcaemia occur most commonly in preterm infants?
cards.back: Within the first 3 days of life due to calcium deficiency; about 1/3 of LBWI and 50% of IDM affected

cards.front: How frequently should neonatal fluid status be assessed?
cards.back: Every 6–12 hours depending on clinical state

cards.front: Why does bilirubin cross the blood-brain barrier in kernicterus?
cards.back: High free bilirubin crosses the BBB in areas with high blood flow and high metabolic rates, causing neurotoxicity

cards.front: How should feeding be managed during phototherapy for severe jaundice?
cards.back: Encourage nasogastric tube feeds or feeds under phototherapy light to maximize exposure and minimize breastfeeding interruption

cards.front: What is the mechanism of action of anti-D in Rh sensitisation prevention?
cards.back: Anti-D binds to fetal RBCs carrying D antigen, facilitating their rapid removal from maternal circulation before sensitisation occurs

cards.front: Why is the Coombs test often negative in ABO incompatibility?
cards.back: Natural anti-A and anti-B antibodies are IgM (do not cross placenta efficiently) or weakly reactive; direct Coombs may be negative despite haemolysis

cards.front: Why are VLBW infants <32 weeks prone to hyponatraemia?
cards.back: Unable to conserve sodium efficiently; high urinary sodium losses lead to hyponatraemia

cards.front: How does RDS combined with asphyxia lead to hyponatraemia?
cards.back: RDS + asphyxia → SIADH (syndrome of inappropriate antidiuretic hormone) → sodium loss in urine, water intoxication, diarrhoea

cards.front: What is the mechanism of brain damage in hypernatraemia?
cards.back: Water shift from brain tissue causes brain cell shrinkage, pressure differences → capillary dilatation and rupture → cerebral venous thrombosis and haemorrhage

cards.front: What causes excessive insensible water loss in phototherapy and radiant heaters?
cards.back: Phototherapy lights and radiant heaters increase insensible water loss, predisposing to dehydration and hypernatraemia

cards.front: How does fluid overload contribute to necrotizing enterocolitis (NEC)?
cards.back: Excessive fluid administration can lead to bowel distension, vascular compromise, and NEC development

cards.front: What is the 1st mode of HIV transmission to children (perinatal)?
cards.back: Perinatal transmission occurs through: intrauterine, intrapartum, and breastfeeding.

cards.front: What is the estimated risk of HIV transmission via contaminated blood product in the US?
List 3 types of sexual activity that can transmit HIV to children.
cards.back: Heterosexual, homosexual, and sexual abuse.

cards.front: What are 2 settings for parenteral transmission of HIV?
cards.back: Sharing contaminated needles (illicit drugs, anabolic steroids) and iatrogenic (re-use of non-sterilized equipment).

cards.front: What is the MTCT transmission rate WITHOUT any intervention?
What is the MTCT transmission rate WITH effective interventions?
cards.back: Below 5%

cards.front: What is the mechanism of in utero HIV transmission - timing and mechanism?
cards.back: Majority occurs in 3rd trimester via breakdown of placental integrity leading to microtransfusions of viraemic maternal blood across placenta to fetus.

cards.front: What conditions increase in utero HIV transmission risk?
cards.back: Genital tract infections and placental inflammation.

cards.front: What is the mechanism of intrapartum HIV transmission?
cards.back: Contact of infant mucosal membranes with HIV virus in maternal blood and secretions, with microtransfusions crossing placenta during labour contractions.

cards.front: What is the effect of ruptured membranes >4 hours on intrapartum HIV transmission?
cards.back: Associated increased risk of transmission.

cards.front: What is the primary maternal risk factor for MTCT?
cards.back: Maternal plasma and breastmilk viral load.

cards.front: What are 2 additional maternal risk factors for MTCT (besides viral load)?
cards.back: Maternal immunologic status and clinical stage.

cards.front: Can clinical findings discriminate infected from uninfected infants at birth?
cards.back: No. At birth, there are no definitive clinical findings that discriminate between infected and uninfected infants.

cards.front: List 4 slow progression signs/symptoms of perinatal HIV infection in infants.
cards.back: Oral thrush, diffuse lymphadenopathy, hepatomegaly, splenomegaly, recurrent diarrhea.

cards.front: What is a life-threatening OI in perinatally infected infants and at what age?
cards.back: PCP (Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia) at 4-6 months.

cards.front: List 4 obscure presentations of perinatal HIV infection.
cards.back: Thrombocytopenia, encephalopathy, nephrotic syndrome, heart failure, failure to thrive.

cards.front: What 2 serious complications can occur early in perinatal HIV infection?
cards.back: Growth failure and CNS disease.

cards.front: What is the post-HAART life expectancy for perinatally infected children?
cards.back: Greater than 95% living at 16 years of age.

cards.front: What is the most commonly reported AIDS-defining condition in children <13 years and its percentage?
cards.back: Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia at 35%.

cards.front: What is the 2nd most common AIDS-defining condition in children <13 years and its percentage?
cards.back: Lymphoid interstitial pneumonitis at 23%.

cards.front: What is the 3rd most common AIDS-defining condition in children <13 years and its percentage?
cards.back: Recurrent bacterial infections at 21%.

cards.front: What is the 4th most common AIDS-defining condition in children <13 years and its percentage?
cards.back: HIV wasting syndrome at 19%.

cards.front: What is the 5th most common AIDS-defining condition in children <13 years and its percentage?
cards.back: HIV encephalopathy at 17%.

cards.front: What 2 methods are used for HIV antibody testing?
cards.back: ELISA and Western Blot.

cards.front: What does 'D' stand for in HIV DNA PCR in neonatal diagnosis?
cards.back: D for 'Diagnosis' - used for diagnosis in neonates.

cards.front: What does HIV RNA quantitation (viral load) indicate?
cards.back: Prognosis and response to therapy.

cards.front: What are 2 methods to assess HIV antiretroviral resistance?
cards.back: Genotype (mutations seen in resistant strains) and phenotype (in vitro sensitivity).

cards.front: What additional laboratory test can monitor ARV therapy?
cards.back: Therapeutic drug monitoring.

cards.front: Why are antibody assays unreliable in infants and young children?
cards.back: Due to persistence of transplacentally acquired maternal antibodies.

cards.front: When may antibody tests be false negative?
cards.back: In the first weeks after acute infection.

cards.front: What does qualitative HIV-1 DNA PCR detect?
cards.back: HIV proviral DNA within peripheral blood mononuclear cells.

cards.front: What 2 sample types can be used for HIV DNA PCR?
cards.back: Whole blood or dried blood samples.

cards.front: Will HIV DNA PCR be positive even if the patient is on ART?
cards.back: Yes. Test positive even if on ART.

cards.front: Why might DNA PCR not detect very recent infection?
cards.back: Because 2/3 of MTCT occurs perinatally and DNA PCR will not detect very recent infection.

cards.front: What is the sensitivity of HIV DNA PCR at birth?
What is the sensitivity of HIV DNA PCR at 2-4 weeks of age?
cards.back: Greater than 90%

cards.front: What is the sensitivity of HIV DNA PCR at 1, 3, and 6 months of age?
What time period does EMTCT (Elimination of MTCT) services cover?
cards.back: From preconception to 18 months of age or 6 weeks post cessation of breastfeeding if continued beyond 18 months.

cards.front: List 6 settings where EMTCT services should be provided.
cards.back: Family planning, antenatal care, delivery, post-natal care, EPI visits, and IMCI visits.

cards.front: What was the 2015 guideline for HIV testing in pregnant mothers?
cards.back: At first visit and every 3 months.

cards.front: What is the 2019 guideline for HIV testing in pregnant mothers?
cards.back: At first visit and each routine basic antenatal care plus visit (8 visits in all).

cards.front: What additional guidance was added in 2019 for ART initiation in pregnant mothers?
cards.back: Guidance on adherence, considerations for adolescents, and use of dolutegravir (DTG) in child bearing age.

cards.front: When should viral load testing be done in the mother?
cards.back: At delivery.

cards.front: When should viral load testing be done postpartum?
cards.back: At 6 months postpartum.

cards.front: What is the frequency of viral load testing if breastfeeding?
cards.back: 6-monthly if breastfeeding.

cards.front: What was the 2015 guideline for infant HIV testing - timing?
cards.back: HIV PCR at birth and 10 weeks; 14 weeks PCR for high-risk infants; testing 6 weeks post cessation of breastfeeding; 18 months rapid with a second rapid for confirmation.

cards.front: What changed in 2019 regarding the 14-week PCR test?
cards.back: NO 14 WEEK TEST in 2019 guideline (removed from 2015).

cards.front: What new test was added in 2019 infant HIV testing?
cards.back: 6 month PCR for all exposed infants.

cards.front: What changed at 18 months in the 2019 infant testing guideline?
cards.back: Universal HIV testing at 18 months if exposed uninfected (changed from rapid with confirmation).

cards.front: What is the confirmatory test up to 2 years in 2019 guideline?
cards.back: PCR used as confirmatory test up to 2 years of age (changed from rapid).

cards.front: What was the 2015 definition of 'high risk' infant at birth (maternal VL)?
cards.back: Maternal VL >1000 c/ml.

cards.front: What was the 2015 definition of 'high risk' infant at birth (maternal ART)?
cards.back: Maternal ART <4 weeks prior to delivery.

cards.front: What is the 2019 definition of 'high risk' infant (maternal VL at delivery)?
cards.back: Mother VL >1000 c/ml at delivery or most recent viral load taken during the last 12 weeks of ANC.

cards.front: What is the 2019 definition of 'high risk' infant (maternal VL unknown)?
cards.back: Mother with no VL in the last 12 weeks of ANC.

cards.front: What was the 2015 PEP regimen for high-risk infants?
cards.back: AZT for 6 weeks and NVP for 12 weeks.

cards.front: What is the 2019 PEP regimen for high-risk infants?
cards.back: High risk: AZT for 6 weeks and NVP for a minimum of 12 weeks.

cards.front: What is the 2019 rule for stopping NVP in high-risk infants?
cards.back: Stop NVP if mothers VL <1000 c/ml or until 1 week after she stops breastfeeding.

cards.front: What is the ART duration recommended for breastfeeding mothers with HIV?
cards.back: 24 months or longer, in line with recommendation for general population.

cards.front: What should be done for HIV exposed infant at 3-6 days after birth?
cards.back: Follow up PCR and start ARVs.

cards.front: What should be done for HIV exposed infant at 6 weeks?
cards.back: Ensure birth PCR and mother's VL checked.

cards.front: What should be done for HIV exposed infant at 10 weeks?
cards.back: PCR for all previously negative infants.

cards.front: What should be done for HIV exposed infant at 6 months?
cards.back: PCR for exposed uninfected; establish HIV status of unexposed via test of mother or rapid on child if no mother available.

cards.front: What should be done for HIV exposed infant at 18 months?
cards.back: Universal HIV rapid for all infants unless known to be infected.

cards.front: When should age-appropriate testing be done post breastfeeding?
cards.back: 6 weeks post cessation of breastfeeding.

cards.front: What is the confirmatory test rule if patient <2 years and positive PCR or rapid?
cards.back: CONFIRM with a HIV PCR test on a new sample.

cards.front: Should you wait for confirmatory result before starting ART?
cards.back: No. Do not wait for confirmatory result to initiate therapy.

cards.front: What is the screening and confirmatory test algorithm for age <18/12?
cards.back: Screening: PCR; Confirmation: PCR.

cards.front: What is the screening and confirmatory test algorithm for age 18/12–2 years?
cards.back: Screening: Rapid; Confirmation: PCR.

cards.front: What is the screening and confirmatory test algorithm for age >2 years?
cards.back: Screening: Rapid; Confirmation: Rapid.

cards.front: What should you do at every maternal visit if VL elevates?
cards.back: Check results of mother's most recent viral load.

cards.front: What is the regimen if mother's VL is elevated?
cards.back: Restart high risk prophylaxis for infant with AZT 12 hourly (6 weeks) and NVP daily (min 12 weeks).

cards.front: What adjustment must be made when restarting PEP for elevated maternal VL?
cards.back: Remember to adjust dosages according to infant weight.

cards.front: When can NVP be stopped in exposed infant?
cards.back: Stop only if mother's VL <1000 c/ml after >12 weeks.

cards.front: When must NVP be continued in exposed infant?
cards.back: Continue if VL >1000 c/ml until one week after breastfeeding.

cards.front: When can cotrimoxazole (CTP) be stopped?
cards.back: Stop until infant is confirmed HIV negative six weeks post cessation of breastfeeding.

cards.front: When can CTP be stopped if not breastfeeding?
cards.back: If 10 week PCR negative and no breastfeeding can stop CTP.

cards.front: What is the management approach for abandoned infants with unknown HIV exposure?
cards.back: Treat as high risk infant - do PCR and start high risk prophylaxis.

cards.front: What is the management if abandoned infant's PCR is positive?
cards.back: Do confirm viral load and start ARV, stop NVP and AZT.

cards.front: What is the management if abandoned infant's PCR is negative?
cards.back: Do PCR at ten weeks of age.

cards.front: What is the first step in managing an indeterminate PCR result?
cards.back: Check prior PCR and VL.

cards.front: What action if PCR is pos/indeterminate but VL is detectable?
cards.back: Treat as infected and start ARVs.

cards.front: What action if PCR is neg/indeterminate, VL undetectable, and no prior PCR/VL?
cards.back: Repeat PCR and VL urgently.

cards.front: What action if repeat PCR and VL are negative?
cards.back: Follow testing algorithm for the exposed infant.

cards.front: When should the first dose of NVP be given to exposed infant?
cards.back: As soon as possible after birth.

cards.front: Should birth PCR be done before or after infant NVP administration?
cards.back: Ideally before, but any delay in testing should not delay the NVP administration.

cards.front: What is the frequency and repeat rule for NVP dosing?
cards.back: Only one dose per 24-hour period; repeat dose once only if baby vomits.

cards.front: What should be done if infant HIV PCR is positive while on NVP?
cards.back: Stop NVP, confirm with second HIV PCR test and initiate ART while awaiting the second PCR test result.

cards.front: Should breastfeeding continue while on NVP?
cards.back: Yes. Continue breastfeeding.

cards.front: What is the initial management if neonate is TB exposed?
cards.back: Do a thorough clinical examination.

cards.front: What is the TB prophylaxis regimen if neonate is asymptomatic?
cards.back: INH 10 mg/kg for 6 months and DO NOT GIVE BCG.

cards.front: When should BCG be given after INH prophylaxis completion?
cards.back: Give BCG 2 weeks after completion of INH prophylaxis.

cards.front: What action if clinical suspicion of TB in infant?
cards.back: Refer for TB evaluation.

cards.front: What is the cumulative MTCT risk without preventative interventions?
What is the intrauterine MTCT risk without intervention?
What is the peripartum MTCT risk without intervention?
What is the breastfeeding MTCT risk without intervention?
What percentage of perinatal HIV transmission occurs in utero?
What percentage of perinatal HIV transmission occurs intrapartum?
What is the breastfeeding transmission risk with established maternal infection?
What is the breastfeeding transmission risk with primary maternal infection?
What is the primary maternal factor influencing perinatal transmission?
cards.back: HIV-1 RNA levels (viral load).

cards.front: What maternal immunologic factor influences perinatal transmission?
cards.back: Low CD4 lymphocyte count.

cards.front: What 2 viral co-infections increase perinatal transmission?
cards.back: Hepatitis C and CMV.

cards.front: What bacterial condition increases perinatal transmission?
cards.back: Bacterial vaginosis.

cards.front: What maternal substance use increases perinatal transmission?
cards.back: Maternal drug use.

cards.front: What prophylaxis lack increases perinatal transmission?
cards.back: Lack of AZT during pregnancy.

cards.front: What maternal habit increases perinatal transmission?
cards.back: Cigarette smoking.

cards.front: What obstetrical complication increases perinatal transmission?
cards.back: Length of ruptured membranes/chorioamnionitis.

cards.front: What obstetrical delivery mode increases perinatal transmission?
cards.back: Vaginal delivery.

cards.front: What obstetrical invasive procedure increases perinatal transmission?
cards.back: Scalp electrodes.

cards.front: What obstetrical condition about twins increases perinatal transmission?
cards.back: First born twin.

cards.front: What infant factor increases perinatal transmission?
cards.back: Prematurity.

cards.front: What is the HIV transmission rate (95% CI) with VL <400 copies/ml?
What is the HIV transmission rate (95% CI) with VL 400–3500 copies/ml?
What is the HIV transmission rate (95% CI) with VL 3500–10,000 copies/ml?
What is the HIV transmission rate (95% CI) with VL 10,000–30,000 copies/ml?
What is the HIV transmission rate (95% CI) with VL >30,000 copies/ml?
Is perinatal transmission possible at all maternal viral load levels?
cards.back: Yes. Perinatal transmission can potentially occur at all levels of maternal viral load.

cards.front: How does transmission risk correlate with maternal viral load?
cards.back: Risk of transmission increases with increasing maternal viral load.

cards.front: Do maternal plasma and cervico-vaginal secretion viral loads always correlate?
cards.back: No. Maternal plasma and cervico-vaginal secretion viral loads do not always correlate.

cards.front: What is known about ZDV (AZT) safety in neonatal prophylaxis?
cards.back: ZDV generally safe, may cause transient anemia.

cards.front: What is the risk of 3TC + ZDV combination in infants?
cards.back: May increase hematologic toxicity.

cards.front: What are rare severe side effects of NVP in infants?
cards.back: Severe rash and hepatotoxicity; resistance may occur in infants who become HIV infected.

cards.front: Why are protease inhibitors not recommended for neonates?
cards.back: No PK data for most; LPV/r: possible cardiac and other toxicity.

cards.front: By how much does breastfeeding increase HIV transmission?
cards.back: Increases HIV transmission by 5–28%.

cards.front: Is exclusive breastfeeding lower risk than mixed feeding?
cards.back: Yes. Exclusive breastfeeding is lower risk than mixed breastfeeding.

cards.front: What is the transmission risk difference between seroconversion after vs before delivery?
cards.back: Women who seroconvert after delivery have a higher rate of transmission than women infected prior to delivery.

cards.front: How does mastitis affect breastfeeding transmission?
cards.back: Mastitis has been associated with increased breast milk HIV RNA levels and increased breast milk transmission.

cards.front: How often should HIV counselling and testing be done during pregnancy?
cards.back: Every 3 months throughout pregnancy.

cards.front: When should HCT be done during delivery?
cards.back: At labour/delivery.

cards.front: When should HCT be done in the postpartum period?
cards.back: At the 6 week EPI visit.

cards.front: How often should HCT be done throughout breastfeeding?
cards.back: Every 6 months throughout breastfeeding.

cards.front: What is step 1 of the HIV life cycle?
cards.back: Free Virus.

cards.front: What is step 2 of the HIV life cycle (Binding and Fusion)?
cards.back: Virus binds to a CD4 molecule and one of two coreceptors (CCR5 or CXCR4). Receptor molecules are common on the cell surface. The virus then fuses with the cell.

cards.front: What is step 3 of the HIV life cycle (Infection)?
cards.back: Virus penetrates cell. Contents emptied into cell.

cards.front: What is step 4 of the HIV life cycle (Reverse Transcription)?
cards.back: Single strands of viral RNA are converted into double-stranded DNA by the reverse transcriptase enzyme.

cards.front: What is step 5 of the HIV life cycle (Integration)?
cards.back: Viral DNA is combined with the cell's own DNA by the integrase enzyme.

cards.front: What is step 6 of the HIV life cycle (Transcription)?
cards.back: When the infected cell divides, the viral DNA is 'read' and long chains of proteins are made.

cards.front: What is step 7 of the HIV life cycle (Assembly)?
cards.back: Sets of viral protein chains come together.

cards.front: What is step 8 of the HIV life cycle (Budding)?
cards.back: Immature virus pushes out of the cell, taking some cell membrane with it. The protease enzyme starts processing the proteins in the newly forming virus.

cards.front: What is step 9 of the HIV life cycle?
cards.back: Immature virus breaks free of the infected cell.

cards.front: What is step 10 of the HIV life cycle (Maturation)?
cards.back: The protease enzyme finishes cutting HIV protein chains into individual proteins that combine to make a new working virus.

cards.front: What is the cumulative perinatal HIV transmission risk without intervention?
cards.back: 13% to 39% (broken down as: intrauterine 5%, peripartum 13-18%, breastfeeding 5-28%).

cards.front: Why are perinatal transmission rates quoted as both '15-45%' and '13-39%' cumulatively?
cards.back: 15-45% is the global range without intervention. 13-39% is the cumulative breakdown by mode: intrauterine (5%) + peripartum (13-18%) + breastfeeding (5-28%) = 23-51% theoretical max, but actual cumulative is 13-39%.

cards.front: What is the first management step for an abandoned infant with unknown HIV exposure?
cards.back: Treat as a high-risk infant: perform HIV PCR and initiate high-risk prophylaxis (AZT + NVP).

cards.front: What is the management if an abandoned infant's initial PCR is positive?
cards.back: Confirm with viral load test, initiate ART, and stop NVP and AZT.

cards.front: What is the management if an abandoned infant's initial PCR is negative?
cards.back: Repeat HIV PCR at 10 weeks of age.

cards.front: What is the first step when managing an indeterminate HIV PCR result?
cards.back: Check prior PCR results and viral load history.

cards.front: What action if indeterminate or positive PCR shows detectable viral load?
cards.back: Treat as infected: initiate ART immediately.

cards.front: What action if indeterminate/negative PCR with undetectable VL and no prior tests?
cards.back: Repeat HIV PCR and viral load urgently.

cards.front: What is the final step if repeat PCR and VL are negative in indeterminate case?
cards.back: Follow the standard testing algorithm for an HIV-exposed infant.

cards.front: What is the TB prophylaxis regimen for TB-exposed asymptomatic neonates?
cards.back: INH 10 mg/kg daily for 6 months. Do NOT give BCG during this period.

cards.front: When can BCG be administered after TB prophylaxis completion?
cards.back: 2 weeks after completion of INH 6-month course.

cards.front: Calculate INH dose for a 3 kg TB-exposed neonate (10 mg/kg regimen).
cards.back: 3 kg × 10 mg/kg = 30 mg daily for 6 months.

cards.front: Calculate INH dose for a 4 kg TB-exposed neonate (10 mg/kg regimen).
cards.back: 4 kg × 10 mg/kg = 40 mg daily for 6 months.

cards.front: When should the first dose of NVP be administered to exposed infant?
cards.back: As soon as possible after birth.

cards.front: Should birth PCR testing delay NVP administration?
cards.back: No. Ideally birth PCR should be done before NVP, but any delay in testing should NOT delay NVP administration.

cards.front: What is the dosing frequency for NVP in exposed infants?
cards.back: One dose per 24-hour period; repeat dose once only if baby vomits.

cards.front: What is HIV transmission rate with VL <400 copies/ml?
What is HIV transmission rate with VL 400-3500 copies/ml?
What is HIV transmission rate with VL 3500-10,000 copies/ml?
What is HIV transmission rate with VL 10,000-30,000 copies/ml?
What is HIV transmission rate with VL >30,000 copies/ml?
Compare 2015 vs 2019 maternal HIV testing frequency guidelines.
cards.back: 2015: At first visit and every 3 months. | 2019: At first visit and each routine basic antenatal care plus visit (8 visits in all).

cards.front: Compare 2015 vs 2019 infant HIV testing timeline (14-week and 6-month tests).
cards.back: 2015: PCR at birth, 10 weeks, 14 weeks (high-risk). | 2019: PCR at birth, 10 weeks, NO 14-week test, 6-month PCR for all exposed infants.

cards.front: Compare 2015 vs 2019 high-risk infant definition criteria.
cards.back: 2015: Maternal VL >1000 c/ml OR maternal ART <4 weeks prior to delivery. | 2019: Mother VL >1000 c/ml at delivery (or most recent VL in last 12 weeks ANC) OR mother with no VL in last 12 weeks ANC.

cards.front: Compare 2015 vs 2019 infant PEP regimen and NVP stopping rules.
cards.back: 2015: AZT 6 weeks + NVP 12 weeks. | 2019: AZT 6 weeks + NVP minimum 12 weeks; STOP NVP if mother's VL <1000 c/ml OR 1 week after breastfeeding stops.

cards.front: What is the primary US resource for HIV/AIDS clinical information and guidelines?
cards.back: www.aidsinfo.nih.gov (NIH AIDSinfo resource)

cards.front: What is the CDC's primary website for HIV information?
cards.back: www.cdc.gov/hiv

cards.front: What is WHO's HIV resource website?
cards.back: www.who.int/hiv/en

cards.front: What is the South African HIV Society resource for 2015 HIV guidelines?
cards.back: www.sahivsoc.org/upload/documents/HIV%20guidelines%20_Jan%202015.pdf

cards.front: What is AVERT's resource for South African HIV/AIDS statistics?
cards.back: www.avert.org/south-africa-hiv-aids-statistics.htm

cards.front: What does OOSHI stand for in newborn assessment?
cards.back:
O - Observations
O - Oxygen
S - Sugar
H - Hypothermia
I - Infections

cards.front: List the causes of respiratory distress in the newborn.
cards.back:
1. HMD (Hyaline Membrane Disease)
2. MAS (Meconium Aspiration Syndrome)
3. TTN (Transient Tachypnea of Newborn)
4. Congenital pneumonia
5. Metabolic acidosis
6. Cardiac causes
7. Rare: Diaphragmatic hernia

cards.front: At what gestational age does Hyaline Membrane Disease typically occur?
cards.back: Usually before 34 weeks gestation due to immature lungs that do not produce enough surfactant

cards.front: Who should receive steroids for threatened pregnancy before 35 weeks gestation and why?
cards.back: The MOTHER must receive steroids to help the baby's lungs mature and increase surfactant production (NOT the baby)

cards.front: What is the function of surfactant in the lungs?
cards.back: Surfactant is a substance that prevents the lungs from collapsing, particularly at the alveolar level

cards.front: What is the characteristic chest X-ray appearance in Hyaline Membrane Disease?
cards.back: Ground glass appearance due to micro-collapse of alveoli (not a completely collapsed lung). Affects both lungs equally with small lung volumes.

cards.front: Why do air bronchograms form on chest X-ray in HMD and what do they indicate?
cards.back: Air bronchograms form because air in the airways appears in more detail against a white background of collapsed lung tissue. They indicate more severe HMD where extensive alveolar collapse has occurred.

cards.front: How is surfactant administered in HMD and what supervision is required?
cards.back: Surfactant can be given endotracheally and the patient can be put back on CPAP, but this requires training and supervision first before it can be initiated in the district hospital

cards.front: What is the standard approach for CPAP initiation in HMD at district hospitals?
cards.back: All district hospitals must initiate CPAP as soon as it is needed, following the KINC chart on respiratory distress. If baby is not coping with CPAP, contact the referral hospital immediately.

cards.front: What are the X-ray findings in mild Hyaline Membrane Disease?
cards.back: Ground glass appearance to lung fields extending to the periphery of both lungs; relatively small lungs

cards.front: What are the X-ray findings in moderate Hyaline Membrane Disease?
cards.back: Dense ground glass appearance extending to periphery of lungs; air bronchograms visible (air in smaller airways); both lungs equally involved

cards.front: What are the X-ray findings in severe Hyaline Membrane Disease?
cards.back: Dense lung fields; air bronchograms; presence of endotracheal tube with tip above carina

cards.front: Why does a fetus produce meconium and in which babies does it occur?
cards.back: Fetus produces meconium as a stress response to something going wrong in utero (indicating urgent need for delivery). Meconium is usually produced in term babies and rare in preterm babies.

cards.front: Is meconium sterile or contaminated and what damage does it cause?
cards.back: Meconium is STERILE. The damage caused to the lungs is chemical pneumonitis (similar to paraffin ingestion). Antibiotics are used because the damaged lung is easily infected by bacteria.

cards.front: What is the ball-valve effect of meconium in Meconium Aspiration Syndrome?
cards.back: Meconium acts as a one-way valve allowing air in but preventing air from leaving, causing hyperinflation of the lungs

cards.front: What is the main complication of Meconium Aspiration Syndrome due to hyperinflation?
cards.back: Pneumothorax is a major risk and complication of MAS due to the ball-valve effect causing air trapping and hyperinflation

cards.front: What is the correct suctioning technique in meconium-stained deliveries?
cards.back: Suction baby below the cords under direct vision BEFORE stimulating baby during resuscitation. If baby is active, crying, and vigorous, do NOT suction below cords.

cards.front: When should a baby with MAS be admitted immediately?
cards.back: If patient's chest is hyperinflated and distressed, admit immediately. If worsening respiratory distress and not coping per KINC Chart, contact referral hospital immediately.

cards.front: Why is CPAP a relative contraindication in Meconium Aspiration Syndrome?
cards.back: CPAP may cause pneumothoraces in MAS due to air trapping from the ball-valve effect of meconium

cards.front: What is the characteristic chest X-ray appearance in Meconium Aspiration Syndrome?
cards.back: Relatively large lung fields with flat diaphragm; coarse patchy appearance of consolidation/collapse in lung fields; areas of normal/over-expanded lung

cards.front: What are the X-ray findings in neonatal pneumothorax?
cards.back: Free air in pleural space; mediastinal shift away from the pneumothorax; collapsed lung

cards.front: What is the most common cause of pneumonia early in the neonatal period and what antibiotic is it sensitive to?
cards.back: Group B Strep is the most common cause of early neonatal pneumonia and is sensitive to penicillin

cards.front: List other causes of congenital or acquired pneumonia in the newborn besides Group B Strep.
cards.back:
1. CMV (Cytomegalovirus)
2. Herpes simplex virus
3. Syphilis
4. TB (Tuberculosis)

cards.front: How does pneumonia affect the lungs in distribution and what type of infection can it be?
cards.back: Pneumonia affects the lungs unequally and may be congenital or acquired

cards.front: Why is maternal history important in neonatal pneumonia?
cards.back: Maternal history may direct diagnosis toward chorioamnionitis and allows checking the mother to identify the causative organism (e.g., TB)

cards.front: What is the standard treatment for pneumonia in high-risk neonates?
cards.back: Penicillin and gentamicin for all high-risk babies (see KINC chartbook)

cards.front: What is the characteristic chest X-ray appearance in neonatal pneumonia?
cards.back: Variable consolidation in lungs; relatively large lung fields; areas of apparently normal lung (unequal distribution)

cards.front: What is Transient Tachypnea of the Newborn (TTN) and when does it occur?
cards.back: TTN occurs in babies post C-section who have increased amount of fluid in the lungs with mild to moderate respiratory distress

cards.front: How long does Transient Tachypnea of the Newborn typically last?
cards.back: TTN normally lasts about 4-6 hours but may persist longer

cards.front: What should be done if Transient Tachypnea of the Newborn does not resolve?
cards.back: Treat baby as per KINC booklet; patient will need to be admitted and treated for pneumonia or sepsis

cards.front: What is the characteristic chest X-ray appearance in Transient Tachypnea of the Newborn?
cards.back: Bilateral streaky appearance; possible fluid in the fissure; peripheral aspect of X-ray is normal

cards.front: What are the two main classifications of congenital cardiac disease relevant to RDS presentation?
cards.back:
1. Acyanotic vs Cyanotic
2. Oligaemic vs Plethoric

cards.front: What is the management approach for cyanotic cardiac disease in the newborn?
cards.back: OOSHI FIRST, then CXR, then ECG/4-limb BP if possible. Cyanotic in trouble requires stabilization and transfer.

cards.front: How should an acyanotic stable cardiac baby be managed?
cards.back: Can be referred electively to cardiac clinic at 6 weeks of age. Parent education: return to hospital if any change in baby's condition including POOR FEEDING.

cards.front: What are the X-ray findings in cardiomegaly from congenital heart disease?
cards.back: Large heart shadow (>60% of chest width); increased vascular markings in lung fields

cards.front: What is a diaphragmatic hernia and how does it affect the lungs?
cards.back: Diaphragmatic hernia occurs when bowel protrudes into the chest cavity, hindering lung development and resulting in hypoplastic lungs which cause respiratory distress

cards.front: Is there a genetic component to diaphragmatic hernia and which lung is affected?
cards.back: There is genetic predisposition because the other lung is also affected (bilateral involvement)

cards.front: What is the management of diaphragmatic hernia in the severely distressed newborn?
cards.back: Referral to tertiary hospital urgently if severely distressed

cards.front: What is the characteristic chest X-ray appearance in diaphragmatic hernia?
cards.back: Heart displaced to contralateral chest; bowel visible in chest cavity; compressed lung on affected side

cards.front: How does metabolic acidosis present with respiratory distress and why?
cards.back: Metabolic acidosis presents with respiratory distress because the body compensates by blowing off CO2 through fast breathing (tachypnea)

cards.front: What is the treatment approach for metabolic acidosis presenting as respiratory distress?
cards.back: OOSHI and appropriate fluid management will result in correction of the acidosis

cards.front: List conditions that can cause metabolic acidosis and respiratory distress in the newborn.
cards.back:
1. Hypoxia
2. Hypothermia
(These can cause metabolic acidosis leading to compensatory tachypnea)

cards.front: What is the key management principle emphasized in KINC when dealing with respiratory distress?
cards.back: WHEN IN DOUBT – ASK!!!

cards.front: Which lungs are affected in Hyaline Membrane Disease and are they equally involved?
cards.back: HMD affects both lungs equally (bilateral involvement)

cards.front: Why does HMD produce a ground glass appearance rather than a collapsed lung appearance on X-ray?
cards.back: Lack of surfactant causes alveolar micro-collapse (not complete lung collapse), resulting in a ground glass appearance rather than a white-out collapsed lung

cards.front: What happens to lung volumes in Hyaline Membrane Disease?
cards.back: Small lung volumes result from alveolar micro-collapse due to surfactant deficiency

cards.front: Explain the visual mechanism of air bronchograms in HMD X-rays.
cards.back: Air bronchograms appear because air in patent airways looks black against a white background of collapsed alveolar tissue. More severe HMD with more alveolar collapse makes bronchograms more visible.

cards.front: What is the appropriate action if a baby with HMD is not coping with CPAP?
cards.back: Contact the referral hospital immediately if baby is not coping with CPAP

cards.front: What is required before surfactant can be initiated in a district hospital?
cards.back: Surfactant administration requires training and supervision before it can be initiated in the district hospital

cards.front: Why is meconium production in utero significant clinically?
cards.back: Meconium production is a fetal stress response indicating something is wrong in utero and usually means the baby needs urgent delivery

cards.front: In which gestational age group is meconium aspiration more common?
cards.back: Meconium is usually produced in term babies and is rare in preterm babies

cards.front: What type of lung injury does sterile meconium cause and what is a similar clinical condition?
cards.back: Meconium causes chemical pneumonitis (similar to lung injury seen in paraffin ingestion). Antibiotics are used because the damaged lung is susceptible to secondary bacterial infection.

cards.front: Describe the ball-valve mechanism of meconium in the airways.
cards.back: Meconium acts as a one-way valve: it allows air in during inspiration but prevents air from leaving during expiration, causing air trapping and hyperinflation

cards.front: When should suctioning below the cords be avoided in meconium-stained deliveries?
cards.back: Do NOT suction below the cords if the baby is active, crying, and vigorous

cards.front: What is the admission criterion for MAS based on chest appearance?
cards.back: If patient's chest is hyperinflated and the baby is distressed, admit immediately

cards.front: What are the specific X-ray findings that indicate pneumothorax with mediastinal shift?
cards.back: Free air in pleural space, mediastinal shift away from the pneumothorax side, collapsed lung, and often an intercostal drain visible

cards.front: What is the specific timing and cause of Transient Tachypnea of the Newborn?
cards.back: TTN occurs in babies after C-section delivery due to increased fluid in the lungs

cards.front: What alternative diagnoses should be considered if TTN does not resolve?
cards.back: If TTN does not resolve, treat as per KINC booklet and consider pneumonia or sepsis requiring admission and treatment

cards.front: Why should maternal risk factors be assessed in suspected neonatal pneumonia?
cards.back: Maternal history may indicate chorioamnionitis and allows assessment of the mother to identify the causative organism (e.g., TB)

cards.front: What is the immediate management priority for cyanotic cardiac disease in the newborn?
cards.back: Cyanotic disease in trouble requires stabilization and transfer to a tertiary center

cards.front: What specific clinical signs should parents be educated to recognize in a stable acyanotic cardiac baby at home?
cards.back: Parents should return to hospital if there is any change in baby's condition, especially POOR FEEDING

cards.front: What is the radiological definition of cardiomegaly on chest X-ray?
cards.back: Heart shadow greater than 60% of the chest width, often with increased vascular markings in lung fields

cards.front: How does bowel in the thorax affect lung development in diaphragmatic hernia?
cards.back: Bowel protruding into the chest hinders lung development, resulting in hypoplastic (underdeveloped) lungs that cause respiratory distress

cards.front: What is the specific mechanism by which metabolic acidosis causes respiratory distress?
cards.back: The body compensates for metabolic acidosis by increasing ventilation to blow off CO2 (hyperventilation), causing tachypnea

cards.front: What is the relationship between hypoxia, hypothermia, and metabolic acidosis in the newborn?
cards.back: Both hypoxia and hypothermia can cause metabolic acidosis, which in turn causes compensatory tachypnea (respiratory distress)

cards.front: Initial care after birth: what is the first action for any newborn?
cards.back: Dry the baby with a warm towel
cards.source: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN
cards.sourceFile: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN.yml

cards.front: What three assessment questions must be asked after birth to classify the baby?
cards.back:
1. Is the baby breathing spontaneously?
2. Is the heart rate more than 100 bpm?
3. Is the baby active and moving?
cards.source: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN
cards.sourceFile: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN.yml

cards.front: When does a baby need resuscitation after birth?
cards.back: If the answer to any of the three assessment questions is 'no'
cards.source: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN
cards.sourceFile: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN.yml

cards.front: At what times is the Apgar score assessed?
cards.back: At 1 minute and 5 minutes after birth
cards.source: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN
cards.sourceFile: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN.yml

cards.front: What does the 1-minute Apgar score indicate?
cards.back: Whether or not the baby needs resuscitation
cards.source: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN
cards.sourceFile: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN.yml

cards.front: What does the 5-minute Apgar score indicate?
cards.back: Whether or not the resuscitation has been successful
cards.source: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN
cards.sourceFile: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN.yml

cards.front: What immediate care does every newborn require? (list 7 items)
cards.back:
1. Cord clamping
2. Vitamin K
3. Dry and warm
4. NVP +/- AZT if HIV exposed
5. Immunizations
6. Maternal and birth history
7. Give to mother and put to breast if well
cards.source: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN
cards.sourceFile: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN.yml

cards.front: List the high-risk baby categories that require special attention
cards.back:
1. Prem, LBW <2 kg
2. Maternal diabetes
3. Maternal TB
4. Other maternal illness (e.g., cardiac with warfarin exposure)
5. RPR positive mother
6. Rh negative mothers
7. Congenital abnormalities
cards.source: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN
cards.sourceFile: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN.yml

cards.front: Where should ELBW (Extremely Low Birth Weight) babies be admitted?
cards.back: All ELBW babies must be admitted to the High care area
cards.source: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN
cards.sourceFile: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN.yml

cards.front: Where should VLBW (Very Low Birth Weight) babies be admitted?
cards.back: Admit to high care area for initial observation
cards.source: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN
cards.sourceFile: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN.yml

cards.front: Where should LBW (<2 kg) babies be admitted?
cards.back: Admit to neonatal unit for observation. Once well and stable and coping with intermittent KMC can be sent to KMC
cards.source: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN
cards.sourceFile: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN.yml

cards.front: Where should babies >2 kg who are ill or high-risk be admitted?
cards.back: High care
cards.source: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN
cards.sourceFile: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN.yml

cards.front: What warmth provision is indicated for ELBW babies?
cards.back: Servo controlled incubator and wrap in plastic
cards.source: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN
cards.sourceFile: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN.yml

cards.front: What warmth provision is indicated for VLBW babies?
cards.back: Servo controlled incubator if ill; standard incubator if well; once well intermittent KMC
cards.source: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN
cards.sourceFile: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN.yml

cards.front: What warmth provision is indicated for LBW (<2 kg) babies?
cards.back: Standard incubator; once well intermittent KMC
cards.source: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN
cards.sourceFile: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN.yml

cards.front: What warmth provision is indicated for LBW (>2 kg) babies?
cards.back: KMC (Kangaroo Mother Care)
cards.source: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN
cards.sourceFile: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN.yml

cards.front: Which babies are kept NIL by mouth for at least 24 hours?
cards.back: Babies under 1.5 kg and sick babies
cards.source: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN
cards.sourceFile: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN.yml

cards.front: What feeding method is used for sick babies once condition stabilizes?
cards.back: Nasogastric tube (NGT) feeding
cards.source: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN
cards.sourceFile: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN.yml

cards.front: When can a baby transition from oxygen support to cup feeding?
cards.back: Once the baby is no longer receiving head box oxygen
cards.source: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN
cards.sourceFile: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN.yml

cards.front: When can a baby transition from cup feeding to breastfeeding?
cards.back: Once the baby can suckle
cards.source: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN
cards.sourceFile: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN.yml

cards.front: How frequently should blood glucose be checked in newborns?
cards.back: 3 hourly for the first 24 hours
cards.source: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN
cards.sourceFile: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN.yml

cards.front: How should oxygen saturation be monitored in babies receiving oxygen?
cards.back: Continuously until optimal saturation is achieved
cards.source: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN
cards.sourceFile: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN.yml

cards.front: What vital parameters should be observed and recorded in newborns?
cards.back: Heart rate, respiratory rate, temperature, colour and activity according to their classification
cards.source: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN
cards.sourceFile: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN.yml

cards.front: Which babies are prone to apnoea and require monitoring?
cards.back: Babies with birth weight <1.5 kg or gestational age <35 weeks
cards.source: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN
cards.sourceFile: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN.yml

cards.front: What respiratory stimulants are used for apnoea in newborns?
cards.back: IV aminophylline, oral theophylline, or caffeine
cards.source: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN
cards.sourceFile: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN.yml

cards.front: How long should apnoea stimulant treatment continue?
cards.back: Until the baby is 1.8 kg or has been apnoea-free for at least 7 days
cards.source: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN
cards.sourceFile: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN.yml

cards.front: When can a baby with apnoea history be safely discharged?
cards.back: Only after being apnoea-free for more than 7 days
cards.source: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN
cards.sourceFile: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN.yml

cards.front: What is the respiratory rate cutoff for indicating oxygen therapy?
cards.back: Respiratory rate >80
cards.source: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN
cards.sourceFile: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN.yml

cards.front: What is the oxygen saturation cutoff for indicating oxygen therapy?
cards.back: Blood oxygen saturation less than 88%
cards.source: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN
cards.sourceFile: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN.yml

cards.front: List all indications for oxygen therapy in newborns
cards.back:
1. Respiratory rate >80
2. Severe chest indrawing
3. Grunting
4. Blood oxygen saturation <88%
cards.source: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN
cards.sourceFile: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN.yml

cards.front: Which babies should receive antibiotics prophylactically?
cards.back:
1. Babies with UPTL (unknown prolonged rupture of membranes)
2. Babies with PROM (premature rupture of membranes)
3. Babies with obvious signs of infection
cards.source: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN
cards.sourceFile: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN.yml

cards.front: What is the standard penicillin dosage for newborn antibiotics?
cards.back: IV penicillin 100,000 U/kg/dose twice daily
cards.source: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN
cards.sourceFile: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN.yml

cards.front: What is the standard gentamycin dosage for newborn antibiotics?
cards.back: Gentamycin 5 mg/day daily
cards.source: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN
cards.sourceFile: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN.yml

cards.front: When should CRP be checked in babies receiving prophylactic antibiotics?
cards.back: After 48 hours of prophylactic treatment
cards.source: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN
cards.sourceFile: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN.yml

cards.front: When should prophylactic antibiotics be stopped?
cards.back: If CRP is normal and the baby is well
cards.source: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN
cards.sourceFile: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN.yml

cards.front: How long should antibiotics be given if infection is confirmed?
cards.back: For 5 days
cards.source: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN
cards.sourceFile: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN.yml

cards.front: What is the dosage of multivitamin drops and when should it start?
cards.back: 0.6 ml daily from 5 days if feeding is well established
cards.source: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN
cards.sourceFile: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN.yml

cards.front: What vitamin D content must be in multivitamin preparations for newborns?
cards.back: 400 IU vitamin D daily
cards.source: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN
cards.sourceFile: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN.yml

cards.front: Which babies require separate vitamin D supplementation?
cards.back: Babies <1.5 kg should receive vitamin D 400 IU daily
cards.source: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN
cards.sourceFile: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN.yml

cards.front: What is the dosage of ferrous lactate (Ferrodrops) and when should it start?
cards.back: 0.6 ml ferrous lactate daily from 14 days if the baby is feeding
cards.source: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN
cards.sourceFile: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN.yml

cards.front: What measurements should be charted daily on the baby record?
cards.back: Daily weight
cards.source: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN
cards.sourceFile: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN.yml

cards.front: What measurements should be charted weekly on the baby record?
cards.back: Head circumference and haemoglobin (Hb)
cards.source: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN
cards.sourceFile: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN.yml

cards.front: What administrative checks are required prior to discharge?
cards.back:
1. Immunizations need to be given
2. Details about birth and post-natal period recorded (Road to Health card and discharge summary)
3. Dates for routine follow-up visits recorded
4. Mother counselled on emergency signs and when to return
cards.source: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN
cards.sourceFile: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN.yml

cards.front: What is the minimum weight requirement for discharge?
cards.back: Weight 1.8 kg or more
cards.source: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN
cards.sourceFile: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN.yml

cards.front: What KMC score is required for discharge in breastfeeding babies?
cards.back: KMC score >19
cards.source: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN
cards.sourceFile: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN.yml

cards.front: What is the apnoea requirement for discharge?
cards.back: No apnoea for at least 7 days and no longer on theophylline
cards.source: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN
cards.sourceFile: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN.yml

cards.front: What feeding criteria must be met prior to discharge?
cards.back: Adequate weight gain and appropriate feeding
cards.source: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN
cards.sourceFile: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN.yml

cards.front: What HIV-related requirement must be met before discharge?
cards.back: HIV status must be known and, if positive, antiretrovirals must be prescribed
cards.source: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN
cards.sourceFile: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN.yml

cards.front: What supplement requirement must be met before discharge?
cards.back: Iron and multivitamin supplements must be prescribed
cards.source: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN
cards.sourceFile: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN.yml

cards.front: List discharge criteria for a normal, well term baby
cards.back:
1. Mother is well
2. Mother is coping well with baby
3. Baby is well
4. Baby is feeding well (preferably breastfeeding)
5. If birth weight <2.5 kg, mother practising KMC
cards.source: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN
cards.sourceFile: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN.yml

cards.front: List discharge criteria for a small or sick baby
cards.back:
1. Baby is no longer sick
2. Baby has been growing well (weight loss <10% of birth weight, weight gain ≥10 g/kg/day)
3. Baby is feeding well
4. Mother is well
5. Mother is coping well with baby and feeding
6. Mother is practising KMC
7. KMC score is adequate
cards.source: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN
cards.sourceFile: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN.yml

cards.front: Which conditions warrant a PHC clinic visit?
cards.back:
1. Discharge from eyes
2. Umbilical sepsis (especially with pus draining and red skin around umbilicus)
3. Septic skin lesions (usually pustules)
4. Jaundice
cards.source: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN
cards.sourceFile: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN.yml

cards.front: Which conditions warrant a hospital visit?
cards.back:
1. Baby not taking feeds well or not suckling well
2. Convulsions
3. Rapid breathing
4. Diarrhoea
5. Bleeding
cards.source: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN
cards.sourceFile: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN.yml

cards.front: Which babies require routine follow-up after discharge?
cards.back:
1. Less than 2 kg
2. Sepsis, NEC or meningitis
3. Encephalopathy
4. Severe jaundice
5. Congenital abnormalities
6. Hypoglycaemia
7. IPPV or CPAP
cards.source: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN
cards.sourceFile: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN.yml

cards.front: What components are assessed during follow-up visits?
cards.back:
1. Growth parameters
2. Neurodevelopment and milestones
3. Feeding history
4. Social grants and care dependency grants
5. New problems and parental concerns
6. Outstanding investigations
7. ROP and audiology screening/follow-up
8. Referral to physiotherapy, OT, speech, or dietician
9. HIV - repeated PCR
cards.source: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN
cards.sourceFile: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN.yml

cards.front: Why is early poor feeding considered significant in sick newborns?
cards.back: Poor feeding is often an early sign of severe illness
cards.source: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN
cards.sourceFile: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN.yml

cards.front: What is the target acceptable weight loss in small/sick babies?
cards.back: Less than 10% of birth weight
cards.source: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN
cards.sourceFile: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN.yml

cards.front: What is the target weight gain rate for small/sick babies?
cards.back: At least 10 g/kg/day
cards.source: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN
cards.sourceFile: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN.yml

cards.front: What is the cutoff weight for babies at risk of apnoea who require respiratory stimulant treatment?
cards.back: Babies with birth weight <1.5 kg or gestational age <35 weeks
cards.source: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN_supplement
cards.sourceFile: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN_supplement.yml

cards.front: What is the weight threshold for stopping apnoea treatment, and what is the time-based alternative?
cards.back: Stop when baby reaches 1.8 kg OR has been apnoea-free for at least 7 days (whichever comes first)
cards.source: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN_supplement
cards.sourceFile: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN_supplement.yml

cards.front: What is the weight cutoff that determines which babies are kept NIL by mouth for the first 24 hours?
cards.back: Babies under 1.5 kg (and all sick babies)
cards.source: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN_supplement
cards.sourceFile: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN_supplement.yml

cards.front: What happens to CRP management in babies given prophylactic antibiotics for UPTL or PROM?
cards.back: Check CRP after 48 hours; if normal and baby is well, stop antibiotics. If infection is confirmed, continue for 5 days total
cards.source: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN_supplement
cards.sourceFile: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN_supplement.yml

cards.front: When should multivitamin drops be started and what is the requirement for vitamin D content?
cards.back: Start 0.6 ml daily from day 5 if feeding well established. Must contain 400 IU vitamin D daily
cards.source: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN_supplement
cards.sourceFile: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN_supplement.yml

cards.front: Which low birth weight babies need additional vitamin D supplementation beyond the multivitamin preparation?
cards.back: Babies <1.5 kg receive vitamin D 400 IU daily separately
cards.source: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN_supplement
cards.sourceFile: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN_supplement.yml

cards.front: When should iron supplementation (Ferrodrops) begin and what is the dosage?
cards.back: 0.6 ml ferrous lactate (Ferrodrops) daily from day 14, if the baby is feeding
cards.source: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN_supplement
cards.sourceFile: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN_supplement.yml

cards.front: What are the exact weight loss and weight gain targets for safe discharge of small or sick babies?
cards.back: Weight loss <10% of birth weight AND weight gain ≥10 g/kg/day
cards.source: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN_supplement
cards.sourceFile: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN_supplement.yml

cards.front: List all babies who require routine follow-up visits after discharge
cards.back:
1. Birth weight <2 kg
2. Sepsis, NEC, or meningitis
3. Encephalopathy
4. Severe jaundice
5. Congenital abnormalities
6. Hypoglycaemia
7. IPPV or CPAP use during admission
cards.source: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN_supplement
cards.sourceFile: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN_supplement.yml

cards.front: What must be included in the assessment of a baby during a routine follow-up visit?
cards.back:
1. Growth parameters
2. Neurodevelopmental assessment and milestones
3. Feeding history
4. Social and care dependency grants
5. Assessment for new problems and parental concerns
6. Follow-up of outstanding investigations
7. ROP and audiology screening/follow-up
8. Referrals as needed (physiotherapy, OT, speech therapy, dietician)
9. Repeat HIV PCR for exposed infants
cards.source: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN_supplement
cards.sourceFile: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN_supplement.yml

cards.front: What are the clinical indications for a baby to be brought to PHC clinic (not hospital)?
cards.back:
1. Discharge from eyes
2. Umbilical sepsis (pus draining, red skin around umbilicus)
3. Septic skin lesions (pustules)
4. Jaundice
cards.source: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN_supplement
cards.sourceFile: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN_supplement.yml

cards.front: What are the clinical indications for a baby to be brought to hospital (not PHC)?
cards.back:
1. Poor feeding or poor suckling (early sign of severe illness)
2. Convulsions
3. Rapid breathing
4. Diarrhoea
5. Bleeding
cards.source: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN_supplement
cards.sourceFile: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN_supplement.yml

cards.front: What is the relationship between penicillin and gentamicin dosing in newborn antibiotic therapy?
cards.back: Penicillin: 100,000 U/kg/dose twice daily (BD) AND Gentamicin: 5 mg/kg/day daily (administered together)
cards.source: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN_supplement
cards.sourceFile: ROUTINE CARE AND FOLLOW UP OF THE NEWBORN_supplement.yml
